# Cluster D: Peak and Testing Powerlifting


# Cluster D: Peak Week, Attempt Selection, and Strength Expression

A six-article coaching cluster for advanced strength athletes, powerlifters, and hybrid BJJ athletes. Written for coach-level use inside a program generator vault. Numbering follows the user's requested sequence (39 through 44). Conservative injury language throughout. No medical diagnosis. No guarantees of injury prevention.

---

# 39. Powerlifting Peak Week: Tapering Volume Without Losing Readiness

## What Peak Week Actually Is

Peak week is not a deload. A deload reduces stress to recover from a block. Peak week reduces accumulated fatigue while preserving the neural sharpness, intramuscular coordination, and bar-speed feel built across the block. The goal is to express strength on the platform, not to keep building it. If you are still chasing PRs in the gym during peak week, you are still training, and you will likely under-perform the meet.

The working definition: peak week is the controlled removal of fatigue at a rate that exceeds the loss of fitness, so that readiness on meet day is the highest single point of the block.

## Prerequisites Before You Peak

Peak week only works if the block behind it earned a peak. Confirm the following before committing to a taper:

- The athlete hit prescribed top sets at RPE 8 or below in the last two heavy sessions of the block.
- Bar speed at meet-opener weight is unambiguous and crisp, not grinding.
- Sleep, body weight, and resting heart rate are inside the athlete's normal band.
- No new joint pain that altered technique in the last 14 days.
- The athlete's competition technique (commands, pause length, depth standard) has been rehearsed under load, not just in warm-ups.

If any of these are off, peak week becomes a damage-control window, not a sharpening window. That is a different intent and a different plan.

## The Core Tapering Logic

Volume comes down first and most. Intensity stays relatively high but is held below max effort. Frequency stays similar or drops by one session. Bar speed becomes the primary autoregulation signal.

A workable structure for a 7-day peak week into a Saturday meet:

### Monday (8 days out, last real session of the block end)

- Squat: work up to opener for a single at RPE 7.
- Bench: work up to opener minus 5 to 10 kg for a single at RPE 7.
- Light accessory: one upper back movement, one triceps movement, low volume.
- Total session time under 75 minutes.

### Tuesday or Wednesday (mid-week stimulus)

- Deadlift: work up to opener minus 10 to 15 kg for a single, then stop.
- Optional: light bench technique work, 3 sets of 3 at 60 percent, focus on leg drive and bar path.
- No back-down volume sets. No top sets above RPE 7.

### Thursday (skill maintenance)

- Squat: 2 to 3 singles at 60 to 70 percent, slow eccentric, fast concentric. Practice command timing.
- Bench: 2 to 3 singles at 60 to 70 percent. Practice the pause.
- Sled or light walking, optional.

### Friday (meet eve)

- Optional empty bar to mid 50 percent. Five to ten minutes of movement. Stop.
- Or full rest. Decide based on athlete preference, not principle.

### Saturday (meet day)

- Compete.

## Decision Rules for Bar Speed

Bar speed is the cleanest cheap signal during peak week. Use it as a routing tool:

- If opener speed on Monday is faster than block average: hold the plan.
- If opener speed is equal: hold the plan but drop one accessory.
- If opener speed is visibly slower at the same load: cut Tuesday or Wednesday's deadlift single to 70 percent for a double at RPE 6 and skip the mid-week back work entirely.
- If the athlete reports "heavy" sensation but speed looks fine on video: trust video, not feel. Peak-week perception of heaviness is normal.

## Fatigue Triggers That Change the Plan

Pause and review the plan if any of the following appear in the seven days before the meet:

- Two or more nights of sub-baseline sleep with no clear cause.
- Resting heart rate elevated 8 percent or more over a three-day rolling mean.
- New low-back stiffness on first morning movement that does not clear after warm-up by Wednesday.
- Bar path drift on bench at sub-maximal loads.
- Loss of appetite or unintended weight drop beyond planned cut.

When two or more triggers appear together: drop the second heavy session of the week, keep skill work only, and reassess Thursday. Do not chase one more "feel good" session. The most common peak-week error is one too many sessions.

## Powerlifting-Specific Application

Squat-dominant athletes generally benefit from earlier squat volume reduction, around 10 days out, with skill singles held longer. Bench-dominant athletes can hold a small dose of bench frequency deeper into peak week because bench tolerates volume better. Deadlift is the most fatigue-cost-heavy lift; the last heavy deadlift touch should usually be 10 to 12 days out, with peak week containing at most one moderate single. Sumo pullers often need an extra day of recovery before the meet compared to conventional pullers; consider moving the deadlift single to Monday instead of Tuesday.

## Hybrid and BJJ Athlete Considerations

For athletes who also train BJJ, peak week is not the time to test conditioning, spar at full intensity, or attend an open mat. Combat-sport stimulus during peak week introduces uncontrolled eccentric load, sympathetic activation, and skin-and-joint irritation that interfere with platform readiness.

Suggested routing:

- Drop live rolling 10 days out.
- Keep technical drilling without resistance through Wednesday of peak week.
- Stop all mat work by Thursday.
- Resume rolling no earlier than 5 to 7 days post-meet, with intensity restored gradually.

## Current-to-Goal Gap Before Choosing a Peak Style

Before locking the taper, walk through:

- Current best gym single on each lift, and the date.
- Goal attempt at the meet, with opener, second, and third defined.
- Recovery capacity: sleep, age, life stress, training age.
- Pain constraints: any joint that limited a session in the last block.
- Technical limiter: depth, lockout, leg drive, sumo break, hip hinge.

The wider the gap between gym numbers and goal numbers, the more conservative the taper should be. Athletes attempting a 5 percent jump over best gym single need a longer, gentler taper than athletes opening near a recent gym single.

## Coach and Clinician Review Triggers

Pause and review with the coach, or with a clinician where appropriate, if:

- Pain at a joint changes the lift pattern visibly on video.
- A nerve-type sensation (numbness, shooting, weakness that is new) appears at any load.
- Sleep collapses for three or more nights without clear cause.
- The athlete cannot complete an opener single at RPE 7 with normal speed.

These are routing signals, not diagnoses. The plan changes; the meet decision stays the athlete's.

## Source-Domain Notes

Powerlifting practice, tapering theory, neural readiness, autoregulation, velocity-based training, hybrid athlete interference management.

---

# 40. Attempt Selection for Powerlifting: Choosing Openers, Seconds, and Thirds

## The Frame for Attempt Selection

Attempt selection is risk management under partial information. The athlete does not know exact meet-day strength. The coach does not know the platform conditions, the judging standard for the day, or how the athlete will respond to the warm-up room. Attempt selection is the system that absorbs that uncertainty without sacrificing total.

The two failure modes are symmetric:

- Too conservative: the athlete finishes with all whites and leaves 10 to 20 kg on the platform. Total under-represents actual strength.
- Too aggressive: the athlete bombs a lift, total goes to zero on that lift, qualifying total is missed.

A 10/10 attempt selection plan prevents both, in that order. Avoiding the bomb-out is the higher priority.

## Opener Logic

The opener is the attempt the athlete could make on a bad day, with cold conditions, after a poor warm-up, with imperfect technique. The opener is not a number to be proud of. It is a number that buys total insurance.

Working anchors:

- Squat opener: a confident gym triple converted to a single, or roughly 90 to 93 percent of the realistic third attempt. Speed should look fast on warm-up video.
- Bench opener: 91 to 94 percent of realistic third. Pause discipline is the limiter, not load.
- Deadlift opener: 88 to 92 percent of realistic third for conventional. Sumo openers tend to feel slightly heavier off the floor; many sumo pullers open at 89 to 91 percent.

If the opener does not move with clear bar speed, the second is in question, and the third may need to be re-thought.

## Second Attempt Logic

The second attempt is the working attempt. It should represent the athlete's current realistic best single, in good conditions, with a clean opener behind it. The second attempt is where total is built.

Routing rules for the second:

- If the opener moved at expected or better speed: second is the planned number.
- If the opener moved noticeably slower than expected: the second drops by half of what it would have added. For example, planned jump of 10 kg becomes a 5 kg jump.
- If the opener was a grind: the second is the smallest legal jump that still increases total. Protect the lift; consider the third based on second performance.
- If the opener was missed: the second is a re-take of the opener. Do not chase.

## Third Attempt Logic

The third is built on information that did not exist before the opener and second moved. The third should be re-decided in the warm-up room and confirmed after the second.

Two coherent third-attempt styles:

- The total builder: a number that beats the second by a clear, achievable margin, with a high but not guaranteed probability of success. Often 2 to 4 percent above the second.
- The PR attempt: a number above any previous best, accepted as lower probability, only when total is secure and the meet position justifies risk.

Default to the total builder. PR attempts on the third are a coach decision, not an athlete decision, and they require a secure total and a specific reason.

## If/Then Routing for the Day

- If the opener missed on a technical fault (depth, command, hitched deadlift): re-take the opener exactly. Do not lower the load unless the coach saw a strength miss, not a technical miss.
- If the opener missed on strength: the second drops to a load the athlete has confidently hit in training in the last 4 to 6 weeks.
- If the second missed on strength: the third is the opener weight plus the smallest meaningful jump that still beats the opener. Total preservation is the priority.
- If the second missed on technical fault: re-take the second.
- If the second moved unexpectedly well: increase the third by no more than 2 to 3 percent over the originally planned third. Do not invent a new PR on the platform.

## Powerlifting-Specific Application

Federation matters. In federations with three commands on squat, opener confidence is more important because mis-timed lifts cost more attempts. In bench-only meets, opener percentage often runs lower because of the higher cost of a single bomb in a one-lift meet. In multi-ply contexts, equipment fit changes the math significantly and is outside the scope of this article.

The athlete's lifting style also matters:

- Grinders (long sticking points, slow concentric on heavy singles) need lower openers and smaller second-to-third jumps.
- Fast-twitch athletes with sharp lockouts tolerate larger jumps but should still respect a conservative opener.

## Hybrid and BJJ Athlete Considerations

Hybrid athletes often live further from their absolute strength ceiling because they share recovery with skill sport. For these athletes:

- Openers should be more conservative, near 92 to 94 percent of recent best gym single.
- Second attempts should match a recent confident gym single, not a stretch target.
- Third attempts are total-builder style by default. Save the PR chase for a strength-only block.

This is not a smaller goal. It is a goal that respects the dual demand of the training history.

## Current-to-Goal Gap Before Locking Attempts

Build the attempt sheet against:

- Current best gym single, with date, conditions, and equipment.
- Goal total, broken into squat, bench, deadlift contributions.
- Recovery state during peak week.
- Pain constraints that affect a specific lift, depth, or grip.
- Technical limiter that the athlete has not solved yet.

Attempt selection respects what the body has actually done recently, not what the spreadsheet says is "possible."

## Coach and Clinician Review Triggers

Pause and review the attempt sheet if:

- The athlete has not hit the planned opener as a clean single in the last 6 weeks.
- A pain pattern altered technique in the last block.
- The athlete is returning from time away from the platform and the gym numbers have not yet stabilized.

These are not reasons to skip the meet. They are reasons to write a more conservative sheet.

## Source-Domain Notes

Powerlifting practice, attempt selection theory, risk management, autoregulation, federation-specific judging standards, biomechanics of grinders versus speed lifters.

---

# 41. Testing 1RM Without Ruining the Next Training Block

## When 1RM Testing Earns Its Cost

A 1RM test is expensive. It uses neural capacity, joint tolerance, and recovery currency that could otherwise build the next block. Test 1RM only when the result will be used. Common legitimate uses:

- Setting accurate training maxes for a percentage-based block.
- Confirming readiness inside 12 weeks of a meet.
- Establishing a baseline after a long layoff or after a significant program change.
- Closing a coaching question that cannot be answered with rep-based estimation.

If the answer can come from a heavy double or triple plus a velocity check, that is the cheaper and usually better path. Default to the rep-based route unless one of the above conditions is present.

## Testing Modalities and Their Costs

Three reasonable testing modalities, in order of recovery cost:

### True 1RM Single

Highest neural and joint cost. Best ecological validity for meet prediction. Recovery typically 7 to 10 days for the lift tested, longer for deadlift.

### Top Single at RPE 9

Programmed as the heaviest single the athlete believes leaves one in the tank. Cost is lower than a true max because the athlete is not chasing the absolute ceiling. Recovery typically 5 to 7 days.

### Heavy Triple or Double Plus Estimation

A confident triple at RPE 8 to 9, run through a conservative estimator. Lowest cost. Recovery typically 3 to 5 days. Less accurate at the very top end, but adequate for most programming decisions.

Pick the lowest-cost option that answers the actual question.

## Prerequisites Before Testing

Confirm before scheduling a max:

- The block leading in was not in a fatigued phase.
- The week before the test contains reduced volume, similar to a mini taper.
- The athlete has hit a confident heavy single or top set inside the last 3 weeks.
- No new joint pain that altered technique recently.
- Sleep, body weight, and resting heart rate inside normal band.

If any of these are off, postpone. Testing a fatigued or compromised athlete delivers a fake number and a real recovery debt.

## A Defensible Testing Day Structure

A clean 1RM test for one lift looks like:

- General warm-up: 8 to 12 minutes.
- Empty bar: 2 sets of 5, technique focus.
- 40 percent: 5 reps.
- 55 percent: 3 reps.
- 70 percent: 2 reps.
- 80 percent: 1 rep.
- 88 percent: 1 rep.
- 93 percent: 1 rep, this is the opener-style single.
- 97 to 100 percent: planned top single.
- Optional 101 to 103 percent: only if the prior single moved with clear bar speed and no technical breakdown.

Stop on the first missed lift, slow grind, or technical breakdown. Do not chase.

## Decision Rules During the Test

- If the 93 percent single grinds: the planned top single is the test, no jump above it.
- If the 88 percent single moves slowly: drop the planned top to 95 percent and call it.
- If bar speed at the top single is unexpectedly fast: a 2 to 3 percent jump is allowed if the athlete is in a good state and the lift is technically clean.
- If technique breaks at any point (depth, bar path, lockout): the lift before the breakdown is the test result.

## Limiting Block Damage After Testing

The post-test window decides whether the test cost was worth paying.

For 5 to 7 days after a true 1RM test on a given lift:

- No top sets above RPE 7 on that lift.
- Volume on that lift at 60 to 70 percent, focused on technique.
- Light pulling or pressing accessories at low RPE.
- Sleep priority and protein adequacy.

For other lifts not tested: training proceeds at normal intensity, but expect some systemic carryover for 48 to 72 hours.

If two lifts were tested in the same week: assume a full reduced-intensity week behind both. Triple-lift testing in one week is rarely justified and routinely costs more than it informs.

## If/Then Routing After the Test

- If the test confirmed expected numbers: program the next block off the new training max.
- If the test exceeded expectations: program off the original projected number, not the new one. Banked progress is more durable than chased progress.
- If the test underperformed: do not raise volume to compensate. Examine sleep, stress, technical pattern, and block design before changing the training max.
- If the test produced a technical miss but the load was completable: re-test with technique fix in 7 to 10 days at the same load, not above it.

## Powerlifting-Specific Application

Do not test 1RM inside 8 weeks of a planned meet. Inside that window, use top singles at RPE 8 to 8.5 as readiness checks. The test in this window is the meet. Within 12 to 16 weeks of a meet, one 1RM test on one lift can be appropriate to calibrate the peaking block; usually that lift is the one with the most uncertainty.

## Hybrid and BJJ Athlete Considerations

Hybrid athletes should test 1RM less often. The recovery window after a true max competes directly with skill-sport intensity, and combat-sport stimulus inside the post-test window risks compounding fatigue and irritation at joints already taxed.

Routing for hybrid athletes:

- Default to top single at RPE 8.5 to 9 instead of a true 1RM.
- If a true 1RM is needed, reduce mat intensity for 5 to 7 days after.
- Avoid testing in the week before a competition or hard tournament prep block.

## Current-to-Goal Gap Before Testing

Walk through before scheduling a test:

- Current best confident single, with date.
- Goal number and the timeline to reach it.
- Recovery state and life-stress conditions over the last 4 weeks.
- Pain constraints that affect the lift to be tested.
- Technical limiter that has not been solved.

If the technical limiter is the bottleneck, testing measures the limiter, not strength. Solve the limiter first, then test.

## Coach and Clinician Review Triggers

Pause and review with the coach, or with a clinician where appropriate, before testing if:

- A pain pattern changed the lift's technique in the last block.
- A new nerve-type sensation appeared at any load.
- Sleep has been disrupted for more than a week.
- The athlete has not hit a confident heavy single in 4 to 6 weeks.

These do not rule testing out. They route the decision toward a lower-cost modality, or a delay.

## Source-Domain Notes

Powerlifting practice, maximal strength testing, RPE and velocity-based autoregulation, recovery monitoring, periodization, hybrid athlete interference management.

---

# 42. Meet Week Warm-Ups: Timing, Jumps, and Readiness Checks

## What Meet Warm-Ups Are For

The warm-up room is a controlled environment for one job: deliver the athlete to the platform with the opener loaded, the nervous system primed, and zero unnecessary fatigue. Warm-ups are not training. The temptation to add "just one more single" is the most common warm-up-room mistake.

A high-quality warm-up plan answers three questions before the meet starts:

- How many warm-up sets and what loads?
- What is the timing window between the last warm-up and the opener?
- What readiness checks confirm the athlete is ready for the platform?

## Structuring Warm-Up Sets

Default warm-up structure for a competition lift, scaled for time and athlete preference:

- Empty bar: 1 to 2 sets of 5, slow and clean.
- 40 percent of opener: 5 reps.
- 55 percent: 3 reps.
- 70 percent: 2 reps.
- 80 percent: 1 rep.
- 88 percent: 1 rep, this is the "almost opener" single.
- Optional 93 percent: 1 rep, only if the athlete wants a final touch.

Then the opener happens on the platform. Six to eight total warm-up sets is typical. More than ten is usually a mistake.

## Timing the Last Warm-Up

The last warm-up single should land somewhere between 6 and 15 minutes before the opener is called. The exact window depends on the athlete:

- Athletes who feel "cold" quickly: 6 to 8 minutes.
- Athletes with stable readiness: 8 to 12 minutes.
- Athletes who tend to over-rev and need calm: 10 to 15 minutes, with a single empty-bar touch around 4 to 5 minutes out.

The coach's job in the warm-up room is to read flight pace and back-calculate. A flight running fast may require collapsing two warm-up sets together. A flight running slow may require a re-warm with the empty bar.

## Re-Warming Strategies

If the flight stalls and the gap before the opener stretches past 15 minutes:

- Empty bar to 50 percent for one single, 4 to 6 minutes before the call.
- Stay moving between sets. Sitting cools the athlete.
- Do not re-take the last heavy single. The neural cost is real and the load does not need re-priming.

If the flight runs unexpectedly fast:

- Skip the 80 or 88 percent jump, not the lighter ones.
- A direct jump from 70 to opener is acceptable in an emergency. A direct jump from 55 to opener is not.

## Readiness Checks Before the Opener

Three quick checks before the athlete is sent to the platform:

- Bar speed on the last warm-up single, by eye and by video if available. Speed should match block-average bar speed at that percentage.
- Technical sharpness: clean depth on squat, clean leg drive and bar path on bench, clean break on deadlift.
- Subjective state: athlete reports "ready," not "hyped" and not "flat."

If two of three are off, the opener is in question. Pause and review with the coach.

## If/Then Routing in the Warm-Up Room

- If the 88 percent single moves slowly: the opener stands but the coach should be ready to call a smaller second-attempt jump.
- If the 88 percent single grinds: drop one warm-up step, take an empty bar to 60 percent, then go open. Reassess the rest of the day after the opener.
- If the athlete misses a warm-up single at sub-opener load: re-take it lighter. If still missed, pause and review with the coach about opener load.
- If a sharp pain appears in a warm-up set: stop the warm-up, walk, breathe, retry one lighter set. If pain persists, pause and review.

## Powerlifting-Specific Application

Squat warm-ups should include the actual competition bar if available, especially for athletes used to a different bar. Bench warm-ups should rehearse the pause command at every load above 70 percent; a clean pause is the most common warm-up-room oversight. Deadlift warm-ups should mirror the actual stance and grip used on the platform; switching grip mid-warm-up adds variance the athlete did not train for.

For deadlift specifically, the last warm-up before the platform tends to feel slightly heavier than expected because of the platform plates and the time gap. Plan for this. The 88 percent single should feel solid, not borderline.

## Hybrid and BJJ Athlete Considerations

Hybrid athletes often arrive at the platform with a different baseline arousal pattern than strength-only lifters. BJJ athletes are typically calm in competition environments, which is an advantage; the risk is under-arousal at the platform.

Routing for hybrid athletes:

- Slightly shorter warm-up gaps before the opener, often 6 to 10 minutes.
- One extra "wake-up" single at the empty bar in the final 5 minutes if subjective state reads flat.
- Avoid breath-work or meditation patterns in the final 10 minutes that pull arousal too low.

## Current-to-Goal Gap Before Setting the Warm-Up Plan

The warm-up plan respects the same gap analysis as the rest of meet week:

- Current best confident single, with date.
- Opener, second, and third targets.
- Recovery state through peak week.
- Pain constraints that affect any warm-up movement.
- Technical limiter that needs rehearsal in warm-ups.

If the technical limiter is depth, add one paused squat at 50 percent in the warm-up. If the limiter is leg drive, add a paused bench at 60 percent. Warm-ups can rehearse the limiter without taxing the athlete.

## Coach and Clinician Review Triggers

Pause and review during the warm-up room if:

- A new sharp pain appears at any load.
- A nerve-type sensation appears that was not present in training.
- The athlete cannot complete a sub-opener single with normal speed.
- The athlete reports a sudden change in subjective state (dizziness, lightheadedness, nausea) that does not clear with one minute of rest.

The opener decision can be re-routed quickly. The decision to compete at all is the athlete's, supported by the coach, supported where appropriate by a clinician on site.

## Source-Domain Notes

Powerlifting practice, warm-up structure, neural priming, autoregulation, velocity-based readiness assessment, hybrid athlete arousal management.

---

# 43. Post-Meet Recovery: Rebuilding Training After a Peak

## The Post-Meet Window

A meet is a high-intensity, high-arousal, high-skill day. The post-meet window is not just physical recovery; it includes neural, hormonal, and psychological restoration. The first mistake coaches make in the post-meet window is treating it like a normal deload. The second mistake is letting the athlete dictate the rebuild based on how they feel three days after the meet, when they typically feel better than they actually are.

A defensible post-meet window has three phases:

- Phase 1: Decompression, 3 to 7 days.
- Phase 2: Re-introduction, 7 to 14 days.
- Phase 3: Rebuild, 14 to 28 days.

## Phase 1: Decompression (Days 0 to 7)

The job: remove fatigue, sleep, eat, walk. Not zero training, but minimal directed training.

- Days 0 to 2: no barbell work. Walking, optional light cycling, mobility.
- Days 3 to 5: empty bar to 40 percent on competition lifts if the athlete wants to move, otherwise rest. No top sets, no fatigue.
- Days 5 to 7: optional light upper-body or lower-body accessory session at low RPE.

This window is also where the post-meet psychological dip tends to show up. Build it into the plan: the athlete may feel flat, restless, or unmotivated. That is part of the cycle. Pushing through it with intensity often backfires.

## Phase 2: Re-Introduction (Days 7 to 14)

The job: re-establish training rhythm at a sub-stressful level. Build movement quality back before building load back.

A typical Phase 2 week:

- Three to four sessions.
- Competition lifts at 60 to 70 percent for sets of 3 to 5 at RPE 6.
- One accessory movement per major muscle group, at moderate volume.
- No top singles. No max effort. No grinders.

End-of-week check: is the athlete moving cleanly at 70 percent on all three lifts? If yes, Phase 3 begins. If no, hold Phase 2 for one more week.

## Phase 3: Rebuild (Days 14 to 28)

The job: restore training maxes and prepare to enter the next programmed block.

- Week one of Phase 3: top sets at RPE 7, sets of 3 to 5.
- Week two of Phase 3: top sets at RPE 7.5 to 8, sets of 1 to 3.
- End of Phase 3: a confident heavy single at RPE 8 on one lift can confirm the training max for the next block.

By the end of Phase 3, the athlete is in normal training. The next block begins with a clear training max and a clean recovery state.

## If/Then Routing Through the Post-Meet Window

- If the athlete reports persistent low-back stiffness past day 5: keep Phase 1 longer, add walking and breath-driven mobility, no barbell pulling until clear.
- If the athlete is eager to train hard at day 3: respect Phase 1. Use the energy on walking, technical video review, or skill sport at low intensity.
- If a joint pattern that bothered the athlete during the block returns in Phase 2: pause and review with the coach, and a clinician where appropriate. Do not push through to Phase 3.
- If the athlete is flat through Phase 2 and into Phase 3: extend Phase 2 by another week. A premature Phase 3 makes the next block harder.

## Fatigue and Recovery Markers

Useful signals during the post-meet window:

- Sleep duration and subjective sleep quality.
- Resting heart rate, tracked against the pre-meet baseline.
- Body weight, especially if a cut preceded the meet.
- Appetite and mood.
- Joint stiffness on first morning movement.

Two or more markers off baseline for more than 5 days suggest a longer decompression window, not a shorter one.

## Powerlifting-Specific Application

Deadlift recovery in particular often lags squat and bench by 3 to 5 days. Re-introduce deadlift after squat in Phase 2. The first deadlift session post-meet is often best as a paused deadlift at 50 to 60 percent, focused on the setup and break, not load.

For athletes coming off an aggressive water cut, the first 48 hours after the meet are rehydration and re-feeding, not training. Plan for it.

## Hybrid and BJJ Athlete Considerations

Hybrid athletes are often eager to resume mat work immediately after the meet because they have been off it for one to two weeks. The risk is compounding meet fatigue with sympathetic load from live rolling before the nervous system is restored.

Routing for hybrid athletes:

- No live rolling for at least 5 to 7 days post-meet.
- Technical drilling can resume in Phase 1, days 4 to 5, at low intensity.
- Light rolling at controlled pace in Phase 2, days 8 to 14.
- Full intensity rolling no earlier than Phase 3.

The mat will be there. The cost of a tweak in the first week after a meet is disproportionate.

## Current-to-Goal Gap Before Planning the Rebuild

Before designing the next block:

- Current numbers from the meet, by lift and by attempt.
- Goal numbers for the next meet or training cycle, with a timeline.
- Recovery state through the post-meet window.
- Pain constraints, including anything that flared during the meet.
- Technical limiters revealed by meet video.

Meet video review is the single highest-value coaching task in the first two weeks after the meet. The next block is shaped by what the platform exposed.

## Coach and Clinician Review Triggers

Pause and review with the coach, and a clinician where appropriate, if:

- Pain that appeared during the meet has not cleared by day 10.
- A new pattern of joint discomfort emerges in Phase 2.
- Sleep does not return to baseline within 10 days.
- Mood remains flat for more than two weeks with no clear external cause.

These are routing signals. The post-meet window is the right time to address them, not to push through them.

## Source-Domain Notes

Powerlifting practice, tapering and re-loading, recovery monitoring, autoregulation, post-competition psychology, hybrid athlete interference management.

---

# 44. Strength Expression vs Strength Building: Why Peak Blocks Are Different

## Two Different Jobs

Strength building and strength expression are not the same training task. They share equipment and movement patterns, but the inputs, the volume distributions, the readiness signals, and the success criteria diverge. A coach who treats a peak block like a stronger build block produces a flat meet day. A coach who treats a build block like an extended peak produces a stalled cycle.

Strength building is the work of changing the athlete's strength ceiling. Strength expression is the work of arriving at the platform with that ceiling intact and accessible.

## What Strength Building Looks Like

Strength building blocks are characterized by:

- Higher total volume across the week.
- Moderate to high intensity, with most work in the 70 to 85 percent range.
- Progressive overload across weeks.
- Accessory work that targets weak points and adds tissue.
- RPE running 7 to 9 on top sets.
- Some level of accumulated fatigue that is intentional and managed.

The athlete in a build block does not feel sharp every session. Some grinders are normal. The point is to push fitness up while keeping fatigue inside a recoverable band.

## What Strength Expression Looks Like

Strength expression blocks (the peak block, and the meet itself) are characterized by:

- Lower total volume.
- Higher intensity, with frequent exposure to 85 to 95 percent.
- Reduced accessory work, especially anything that adds eccentric load.
- RPE running 7 to 8.5 on top singles, with bar speed as the primary check.
- Fatigue actively shed week over week.
- Skill rehearsal of competition technique, commands, and timing.

The athlete in an expression block should feel sharp, light, and confident at heavy singles. Grinders are red flags, not normal.

## Why the Difference Matters

The neural and tissue adaptations that build strength take weeks to install. The fatigue that masks those adaptations clears in days. A peak block exploits this asymmetry: hold the adaptations, drop the fatigue.

Treat the peak block like a build block, and you arrive at the platform with intact fatigue and no sharpening. Treat the build block like a peak, and you never accumulate enough stimulus to drive new adaptations. Both errors look similar on paper. They produce different platform outcomes.

## Decision Rules for Block Type

Before writing a block, answer:

- Is there a meet inside 12 weeks? If yes, the block within 4 to 6 weeks of the meet is an expression block. The block before that is a build block transitioning into expression.
- Is the athlete returning from a layoff or a missed cycle? If yes, the next block is a build block, regardless of calendar pressure.
- Has the athlete plateaued for more than two consecutive cycles? If yes, the next block is a build block with significant changes (volume, frequency, exercise selection, or rest distribution).
- Is the athlete inside 2 weeks of a meet? The block is peak week. Nothing about it should resemble a build block.

## If/Then Routing Inside a Block

- If a build block produces sharp readiness early: hold the build, do not collapse into a peak. The signal is good, the time is not.
- If a build block produces persistent fatigue late: shorten the block by one week and transition to a brief expression block before the next build.
- If a peak block produces grinders: cut the second heavy session of the week and re-evaluate the opener. Do not chase the planned numbers.
- If a peak block produces unexpectedly fast singles: do not raise the meet numbers beyond the planned third attempt. Trust the plan; the meet is not a PR-hunting context.

## Fatigue and Readiness Signals by Block Type

In a build block, useful signals are:

- Top-set RPE relative to plan.
- Volume tolerance week over week.
- Recovery between sessions, especially on the same lift.

In an expression block, useful signals are:

- Bar speed at sub-maximal loads compared to block average.
- Single-rep technique on competition lifts.
- Sleep, resting heart rate, and body weight stability.
- Subjective sharpness, not just absence of pain.

A build-block coach who watches bar speed obsessively will under-train the athlete. An expression-block coach who watches volume tolerance will over-train the athlete. The signal must match the job.

## Powerlifting-Specific Application

In powerlifting, the cleanest split is roughly 70 to 80 percent of the year in build blocks, with two to three expression blocks per year culminating in meets. Lifters who try to live in a permanent expression state lose the ceiling they built. Lifters who never enter an expression block under-perform their gym numbers on the platform.

The build-to-express transition is not abrupt. The last two to three weeks of a build block already start trimming accessory volume, and the first week of an expression block still carries small build elements. The transition is a gradient, not a switch.

## Hybrid and BJJ Athlete Considerations

Hybrid athletes face a unique problem: skill-sport demands an ongoing low-grade expression state (the mat punishes athletes who arrive fatigued), while strength progression requires build blocks that produce fatigue.

Routing for hybrid athletes:

- Use shorter build blocks (3 to 5 weeks) with a brief re-set week before the next build, rather than longer build blocks.
- Time expression blocks with reduced mat volume.
- Avoid full expression blocks during tournament prep; that block belongs to the mat.
- Accept that strength ceiling will grow more slowly than for strength-only athletes. The trade is the dual capacity.

This is a design choice, not a deficiency.

## Current-to-Goal Gap Before Choosing Block Type

Before locking the next block type, walk through:

- Current numbers and recent block history.
- Goal numbers and timeline, including any planned meet.
- Recovery capacity, life stress, and training age.
- Pain constraints and joint history.
- Technical limiter that the next block should address.

A build block with no clear technical or capacity goal is just volume. An expression block with no meet on the calendar is just under-training. Both block types need a specific reason.

## Coach and Clinician Review Triggers

Pause and review with the coach, and a clinician where appropriate, before the next block if:

- Pain altered technique in the previous block.
- Sleep or stress conditions have shifted significantly.
- A nerve-type sensation appeared at any point in the last cycle.
- The athlete has not stabilized after a previous meet or test.

These do not block progress. They shape the block that follows.

## Source-Domain Notes

Powerlifting practice, periodization theory, strength expression versus strength building, autoregulation, velocity-based training, hybrid athlete interference management, training age considerations.


---


# Cluster E: GPP and Conditioning Framework


# Cluster E: GPP and Conditioning Framework

Articles 45 through 52. Elite coaching reference for advanced lifters, BJJ and MMA athletes, calisthenics and gymnastics athletes, powerlifters, strongmen, and hybrid athletes. Built for vault use and direct integration into a program generator.

---

# 45. GPP for Advanced Strength Athletes: Building Capacity Without Stealing Recovery

## What GPP Means for an Advanced Athlete

General Physical Preparation, or GPP, is the support layer beneath specific work. For an advanced athlete it is not a warmup, it is not random conditioning, and it is not filler. It is targeted work that raises the floor of what the athlete can absorb, recover from, and repeat.

GPP buys capacity. It pays in fatigue. The job is to extract more capacity than you spend.

## GPP versus SPP

Specific Physical Preparation, or SPP, is the work that drives the competitive lift or skill. GPP is everything that supports that work without overlapping with it.

A useful rule of thumb:

- If the exercise looks like the sport, it is SPP.
- If it shares an energy system or a tissue demand but not the pattern, it is GPP.
- If it shares neither, it is restoration or play.

The cleaner the boundary, the easier it is to dose both layers without overlap.

## When GPP Becomes Junk Volume

GPP is junk when one of three things is true:

- It does not address a real limiter.
- It costs more recovery than the gain it returns.
- It cannibalizes the priority session that follows.

Junk GPP looks busy. Useful GPP looks boring. The athlete should leave the session less stiff, not more, in most cases.

## Current to Goal Gap Thinking

Before assigning GPP, the coach maps:

- The goal block. Hypertrophy, strength, peak, in-season, off-season.
- Current capacity. Resting heart rate trend, work tolerance, soreness curves, session quality.
- Sport load. Mat time, sparring, comp prep, meet prep, skill volume.
- Recovery resources. Sleep, nutrition, stress, training age.
- Pain or limit constraints. Joints that need a more tolerable pathway.
- Equipment. Sled, prowler, bike, rower, pool, open ground.
- Limiting quality. Aerobic base, repeated effort, grip, trunk stiffness, hip capacity, knee tolerance.

The GPP prescription is the smallest dose that closes the most relevant gap.

## Dosing Rules for Advanced Lifters

- Place GPP after the priority session, on a lower CNS day, or as a short separate session.
- Hold heavy lower-body soreness as the first stop signal. Do not pile loaded carries on top of a max effort squat day if the athlete is already cooked.
- Cap sled and carry volume to a level that leaves the next priority session intact.
- Use unilateral and trunk-stiffness work as low-cost, high-yield GPP.

## If, Then Routing

- If the athlete is in a peak block, then strip GPP to short alactic taps and easy aerobic flushes only.
- If the athlete is in an accumulation block, then expand GPP into loaded carries, sled work, and Zone 2.
- If grip is the limiter, then move grip GPP off lifting days and into separate short blocks.
- If the athlete reports stacked fatigue across two sessions, then cut GPP volume before cutting SPP.

## Fatigue Triggers and Coach Review Cues

Pause and review when any of these stack:

- Bar speed drops on planned submax sets for two sessions in a row.
- Resting heart rate trends up over a week with no clear cause.
- Sleep quality drops and morning mood is flat for three days.
- Grip fails earlier than usual across unrelated lifts.
- A nagging joint moves from background ache to session limiter.

These are coach review signals. Persistent pain or a clear loss of function is a clinician review signal.

## Prerequisites and Substitutions

Prerequisites for loaded GPP:

- Clean hinge and brace pattern under moderate load.
- Tolerable carry posture without shoulder shrug collapse.
- A working aerobic base, even modest.

Substitutions:

- Sled push or drag in place of running for joint-sensitive athletes.
- Rower or ski erg in place of bike when seated hip flexion is aggravating.
- Pool work in place of impact when foot, ankle, or knee tolerance is low.

## Templates

Three light GPP templates an advanced coach can scale:

- After-lift tail. Eight to twelve minutes. Sled push, easy carry, trunk anti-rotation. Keep heart rate moderate.
- Separate aerobic session. Thirty to forty-five minutes. Zone 2 bike or rower. Nasal breathing if tolerated.
- Capacity circuit. Three rounds. Loaded carry, sandbag pickup to shoulder, controlled step ups. Long rests, clean reps.

## Source Domain Notes

Drawn from energy system development practice, strength sport coaching, hybrid athlete programming, autoregulation literature, and recovery management practice. No specific brand or person cited.

## Program Generator Notes

- Tags: gpp_overview, advanced, recovery_aware, block_aware.
- Routing: include in every block at a scaled dose. Reduce to minimum during peak weeks.
- When to include: any athlete past the novice ceiling who needs capacity insurance.
- When to exclude or trim: deload, peak, illness recovery, post-comp week.
- What to avoid: stacking loaded GPP onto a max effort lower day, treating GPP as a finisher race, programming GPP that maps onto the same tissue as the priority lift.

---

# 46. Conditioning for Strength Athletes: Alactic Power, Alactic Capacity, Aerobic Base, and Tempo Work

## Why Strength Athletes Need a Conditioning Map

A strong athlete with a poor aerobic base recovers slowly between sets, between sessions, and across the week. Conditioning is not a hobby for strength athletes. It is the recovery substrate that lets heavy work repeat.

The trap is using the wrong tool. Hard intervals look productive and feel productive. They also wreck a heavy week if dosed without thought.

## A Conditioning Taxonomy

Four layers, plus restoration:

- Alactic power. Very short, very hard efforts, full recovery. Five to ten seconds of work, two to four minutes rest.
- Alactic capacity. Short hard efforts, near full recovery. Ten to twenty seconds work, sixty to ninety seconds rest, repeated.
- Aerobic base, Zone 2. Long, easy, nasal breathing or conversational pace. Twenty to sixty minutes.
- Tempo and extensive intervals. Moderate efforts at sub-threshold intensity, controlled rest. Builds repeatability.
- Threshold work. Only when sport or hybrid demand justifies it. Otherwise skip for pure strength athletes.
- Restoration. Easy walks, easy bike spins, mobility, breathwork.

## Where Each Layer Earns Its Keep for Strength

- Alactic power supports peak effort lifts and short bursts. Cheap on recovery if dosed correctly.
- Alactic capacity is helpful for strongman events with repeated short efforts.
- Aerobic base is the missing layer in most strength programs. It raises between-set recovery and overall work tolerance.
- Tempo work cleans up between-session recovery and supports body composition without bleeding into lifts.
- Threshold work belongs to hybrid athletes, not classical powerlifters.

## Current to Goal Gap Thinking

Map the athlete:

- Goal. Peak meet, off-season hypertrophy, general capacity, hybrid event.
- Current capacity. How fast does heart rate drop after a hard set? How well does the athlete handle back to back heavy sessions?
- Sport load. Strongman event practice volume, meet timing, accessory load.
- Recovery. Sleep, calories, stress.
- Pain. Running tolerant or not. Bike tolerant or not. Shoulder tolerant carries or not.
- Equipment. Bike, rower, ski erg, sled, prowler, hill, pool.
- Limiting quality. Aerobic base almost always wins for advanced strength athletes who have ignored it.

## Dosing Templates

For an advanced strength athlete who has neglected conditioning:

- Two Zone 2 sessions per week, thirty to forty-five minutes each, low impact tool.
- One alactic session every seven to fourteen days during off-season. Six to eight efforts of five to eight seconds, full recovery.
- Tempo or extensive interval session one time per week if work capacity is a clear bottleneck. Pull back as meet approaches.

## If, Then Routing

- If the athlete is twelve weeks or more from a meet, then aerobic base is the priority.
- If the athlete is four to eight weeks out, then taper conditioning to maintenance.
- If the athlete is in peak week, then strip to walks and very easy spins.
- If the athlete reports cold hands and feet, slow warmups, and slow between-set recovery, then aerobic base is the limiter.
- If the sport is strongman, then add alactic capacity work that mirrors event timing.

## Fatigue Triggers

- Morning resting heart rate elevated more than five to seven beats over baseline for several days.
- Lift performance dropping while subjective fatigue is high.
- Sleep onset delayed after evening intervals. Move conditioning earlier.

## Prerequisites and Substitutions

Prerequisites for intervals:

- Established aerobic base, even modest.
- A pain tolerant tool. Bike, rower, ski erg, hill push, sled.

Substitutions:

- Sled push for hill sprints when slope is not available.
- Ski erg for runner who is not impact tolerant.
- Pool intervals for the athlete with shin or knee flare ups.

## Coach and Clinician Review Triggers

- Chest pressure, unusual breathlessness at low intensity, or a syncope-like event. Stop and refer.
- Persistent calf pain with running. Switch modality and review.
- New onset hip or low back pain after conditioning bouts. Pause and review.

## Source Domain Notes

Drawn from energy systems literature, endurance coaching practice, strength sport conditioning practice, and autoregulation work.

## Program Generator Notes

- Tags: conditioning, alactic, aerobic_base, tempo, strength_athlete.
- Routing: assign at least two aerobic base sessions per week to every advanced strength athlete unless contraindicated.
- When to exclude: peak weeks, illness, acute injury, late competition prep.
- What to avoid: stacking threshold intervals onto a heavy squat day, running for athletes with intolerant joints, intervals at the end of a fatiguing lifting session.

---

# 47. Conditioning for Grappling and MMA: Energy Systems, Intervals, and Mat-Specific Transfer

## What Combat Conditioning Actually Demands

Grappling and MMA are repeated near maximal efforts over rounds, interspersed with brief drops in intensity. The athlete needs:

- High alactic power for scrambles, takedown entries, and pass attempts.
- Strong alactic capacity for repeated short efforts inside a round.
- A deep aerobic base to recover between rounds and to repeat efforts.
- Sport-specific intervals that train the actual heart rate and effort pattern of competition.

A poor aerobic base shows up as gas tank failure. Poor alactic capacity shows up as the third scramble failing while the first two looked clean.

## Conditioning Taxonomy for Combat Sports

- Aerobic base. Foundation. Easy work, long duration.
- Tempo and extensive intervals. Bridges base and sport.
- Alactic power and capacity. Trains the burst.
- Sport-specific intervals. Round timed work that mimics rest and effort distribution.
- Restoration. Critical given mat volume.

## Current to Goal Gap Thinking

- Goal. Competition prep, off-season base, in-season maintenance.
- Current capacity. Round tolerance, repeat scramble quality, recovery between sparring rounds.
- Sport load. Mat sessions per week, hard sparring count, drilling load.
- Recovery. Sleep, weight cut status, neck and grip fatigue.
- Pain. Mat-acquired joint flare ups, neck tolerance, finger tolerance.
- Equipment. Bike, rower, ski erg, sandbag, hills, ropes, partner.
- Limiting quality. Often aerobic base or repeated effort capacity.

## Intervals That Transfer

- Bike or ski erg, ten seconds hard, twenty seconds easy, eight to twelve rounds. Cycle alactic power.
- Bike or rower, thirty seconds hard, thirty seconds easy, six to ten rounds. Builds repeated effort.
- Round-timed work. Five minutes of mixed efforts on a bike or sandbag complex, one minute rest. Two to five rounds matched to the athlete's round length.
- Sandbag medleys. Pickup, carry, shoulder, throw. Round timed.

## Mat-Specific Transfer

Pure cardio does not transfer one to one. To improve the actual mat output:

- Use partner drills with controlled output as conditioning, not just skill.
- Limit hard sparring to no more than two sessions per week in most blocks. Hard sparring is conditioning whether the athlete likes it or not.
- Place sport-specific intervals on the same day as a mat session, not separated, to consolidate fatigue.

## If, Then Routing

- If a sparring day is heavy, then no additional hard conditioning on that day.
- If competition is more than eight weeks out, then aerobic base sits in the foreground.
- If competition is four weeks out, then sport-specific round intervals dominate.
- If competition is one to two weeks out, then taper. Short alactic taps and easy aerobic flushes only.
- If grip and neck are fried, then move grip-heavy GPP off the schedule and use bike or ski erg instead of ropes and carries.

## Scheduling Inside a Combat Week

- Same-day consolidation is the rule. Pair conditioning with mat work to protect off days.
- Lower CNS days carry aerobic base.
- Strength sessions sit on the highest energy day available.
- A genuine rest day or two is non-negotiable.

## Fatigue Triggers and Review Cues

- Repeat sparring rounds losing quality across the week.
- Sleep disturbance the night after heavy intervals. Pull dose.
- Weight cut layered onto interval load. Reduce conditioning, hold strength.
- New cervical or shoulder symptoms after grappling. Pause and review with a clinician.

## Prerequisites and Substitutions

- Prerequisites. Tolerable cardio modality, base aerobic capacity, pain tolerant grip if rope or carry work is used.
- Substitutions. Ski erg or bike for runner with shin or knee complaints. Pool intervals for impact intolerant phase. Shadow grappling intervals when a partner is not available.

## Source Domain Notes

Combat sport conditioning practice, energy systems work, repeated sprint literature, applied autoregulation, and weight cut management practice.

## Program Generator Notes

- Tags: combat_conditioning, mma, bjj, intervals, sport_specific.
- Routing: always co-schedule with mat days where possible. Cap hard sparring weeks.
- When to include: combat athletes in any block.
- When to exclude: fight week, illness, weight cut crisis.
- What to avoid: long slow runs the day before sparring, threshold intervals on a heavy sparring day, grip heavy GPP layered onto a grip heavy mat week.

---

# 48. Conditioning for Hybrid Athletes: Balancing Strength, Skill, Endurance, and Recovery

## What Hybrid Means Here

A hybrid athlete trains for two or more qualities at once. Common stacks:

- Strength plus distance running.
- Strength plus grappling.
- Calisthenics plus endurance.
- Strongman plus running events.

The hybrid trap is doing every quality enough to be tired, not enough to improve. The coach's job is to defend each quality with the smallest cost to the others.

## Conditioning Taxonomy for Hybrids

- Aerobic base. Larger share than for pure strength athletes.
- Threshold and tempo. Real role. Pull back during heavy strength blocks.
- Alactic power and capacity. Keep alive year round, even at a small dose.
- Sport or event specific intervals. Driven by the calendar.
- Loaded GPP. Smaller share than for pure strength athletes.

## Current to Goal Gap Thinking

- Goal. What event or season is closest. That priority sets the conditioning dose.
- Current capacity. Aerobic base, strength baseline, skill volume tolerance.
- Sport load. Race calendar, comp calendar, skill demands.
- Recovery. Sleep, nutrition surplus or deficit, life stress.
- Pain. Running tolerance, lift tolerance, sport specific flare ups.
- Equipment. Bike, run, pool, gym, sled, open ground.
- Limiting quality. Whichever is closest to the next event.

## Interference Management

Interference is real. It can be managed:

- Separate sessions by six hours or more where possible.
- Strength before endurance when both must occur in one day, unless skill is the priority.
- Lower-body soreness rules. Avoid hard running the day after a heavy squat day. Use the bike, ski erg, or pool.
- Trunk fatigue and grip fatigue limit subsequent sessions. Plan around them.

## If, Then Routing

- If the next event is an endurance event, then endurance is the priority quality. Strength shifts to two short maintenance sessions per week.
- If the next event is a strength event, then strength is the priority. Endurance shifts to two Zone 2 sessions and a short tempo or interval session.
- If both seasons overlap, then alternate which quality leads each microcycle.
- If the athlete shows persistent stiffness or pain after a high running week, then switch to low impact aerobic tools and review form.

## Scheduling

- Same-day pairing reduces total recovery debt for many hybrids. Strength plus easy aerobic on one day, hard interval on a separate day.
- Reserve at least one true off day.
- Pre-competition tapering: cut interval and threshold volume first. Keep alactic and easy aerobic for sharpness.

## Fatigue Triggers and Review Cues

- Resting heart rate climbing more than five to seven beats for a week.
- Run paces slowing at equivalent heart rate.
- Lift bar speed flat or down across two weeks.
- Mood flat. Sleep onset delayed. Pull volume.
- Persistent localized pain. Coach review. If not resolving, clinician review.

## Prerequisites and Substitutions

- Prerequisites. Strength baseline that supports the running or sport load. Aerobic base sufficient to absorb interval work.
- Substitutions. Bike or rower for any running session when the athlete cannot tolerate impact. Pool runs for high mileage de-load. Sled push for hill repeats.

## Source Domain Notes

Hybrid training practice, concurrent training research domains, endurance coaching practice, strength sport practice, recovery management.

## Program Generator Notes

- Tags: hybrid, concurrent, interference_aware, multi_quality.
- Routing: read the calendar first. Set the priority quality. Dose other qualities at maintenance.
- When to include: any athlete training for two or more qualities at once.
- When to exclude: classical powerlifter or pure endurance athlete. Use the dedicated path instead.
- What to avoid: a flat week with hard work in every quality every day, threshold sessions in peak strength weeks, long slow runs in peak meet week.

---

# 49. Loaded Carries, Sleds, and Medleys: GPP Tools for Strength, Trunk, and Work Capacity

## Why These Tools Earn the Spot

Loaded carries, sled work, and medleys deliver three things at once:

- Trunk stiffness under real load.
- Work capacity that translates to long sets, long matches, and long days.
- Joint friendly options for athletes who do not run well.

These tools fit nearly every athlete category when dosed correctly.

## Tool by Tool

- Sled push. Strong knee and quad bias. Concentric only. Very forgiving on the spine.
- Sled drag, backward. Knee and quad bias. Useful for knee tolerance.
- Sled drag, forward. Posterior chain and hip bias.
- Prowler. Sled cousin. Better grip positions for many athletes.
- Yoke carry. Spinal load. Trunk stiffness. Coach the brace.
- Farmer carry. Grip, trunk, posture.
- Suitcase carry. Unilateral trunk demand. Anti-lateral flexion.
- Front carry, sandbag or zercher. Anterior load. Trunk and upper back.
- Overhead carry. Shoulder stability under load. Use selectively.
- Sandbag medley. Pickup, shoulder, carry. Mimics strongman and grappling demand.
- Battle ropes. Alactic capacity. Shoulder durable.
- Med ball throws. Bridge to power. Covered in article fifty.

## Current to Goal Gap Thinking

- Goal. Capacity, hypertrophy support, sport prep, work tolerance.
- Current capacity. Brace tolerance under carry load. Grip endurance.
- Sport load. Strongman event timing, grappling neck and grip demand.
- Recovery. Lower-body soreness budget for the week.
- Pain. Low back tolerance under axial load. Shoulder tolerance for overhead.
- Equipment. Sled, prowler, yoke, dumbbells, kettlebells, sandbags, open path.
- Limiting quality. Trunk stiffness, grip, hip capacity, or repeat effort.

## Dosing Rules

- Place after the priority lift or on a separate lower CNS day.
- Choose one or two tools per session. Avoid the medley buffet that adds fatigue without focus.
- For trunk emphasis. Heavier load, shorter distance, slower walk.
- For capacity emphasis. Moderate load, longer distance or time, controlled pace.
- For event prep. Match event time and load when possible.

## If, Then Routing

- If the athlete has low back sensitivity under axial load, then prefer sled, prowler, and suitcase carries. Pause yoke and zercher.
- If grip is a sport limiter, then place farmer carries off the same day as grip-heavy sport work.
- If knees are sensitive, then backward sled drags often feel better than forward.
- If shoulder is irritable, then strip overhead carries. Use front or suitcase.
- If the athlete is in peak week, then short, light, sharp work only.

## Fatigue Triggers

- Posture collapse under carry. Stop the set.
- Grip failure before time or distance target. Reset or reduce.
- New low back or hip discomfort. Pause and review.

## Prerequisites and Substitutions

- Prerequisites. Brace pattern, hinge pattern, basic carry posture.
- Substitutions. Trap bar carry for farmer if equipment is limited. KB front rack carry for sandbag. Backward sled walk for forward when knee is irritable.

## Templates

- Trunk biased. Three rounds. Heavy suitcase carry twenty meters per side, walk back, rest sixty to ninety seconds.
- Capacity biased. Four to six rounds. Sled push twenty meters, walk back, repeat. Moderate load, low rest.
- Event biased. Match event distance and load. Two to four sets at competition cadence.

## Source Domain Notes

Strongman practice, GPP literature, trunk and bracing biomechanics practice, autoregulation, and applied loaded carry practice.

## Program Generator Notes

- Tags: loaded_carries, sled, medley, gpp_tools, trunk.
- Routing: assign across nearly all advanced athletes. Choose tool by limiting structure.
- When to include: accumulation and base blocks. Reduced in peak weeks.
- When to exclude: acute low back flare, acute shoulder issue for overhead variants, post-event recovery week.
- What to avoid: yoke and zercher walks on a flared back, long farmer carries the day before a grip-heavy mat session, overhead carries for an irritable shoulder.

---

# 50. Plyometrics and Medicine Ball Power: When to Jump, Throw, Bound, and Rotate

## The Plyometric Brief

Plyometrics develop fast force expression. For an advanced athlete the role is precise:

- Maintain rate of force development.
- Bridge slow strength to fast sport expression.
- Provide low-cost CNS priming without grinding sets.

Plyos are not conditioning. Dosed as conditioning they fail at both jobs.

## A Progression That Holds Up

Stage by stage:

- Landing mechanics. Step off a low box, land soft, freeze. Owns absorption before rebound.
- Pogo hops. Stiff ankle, short ground contact. Ankle and foot rhythm.
- Skips. A skip and B skip. Coordination and rhythm.
- Broad jump and box jump. Concentric biased. Cheap on CNS.
- Bounds. Single leg power. Higher demand.
- Depth and drop jumps. Reactive. Higher CNS cost. Earn this.
- Med ball throws. Linear and rotational. Bridge to sport.
- Contrast pairs. Heavy lift paired with a short plyo, when potentiation is the goal.

## Med Ball Power

Med ball throws cover what bar work cannot. Use:

- Chest pass for upper body horizontal power.
- Overhead slam for trunk to floor power.
- Rotational throw for hip to shoulder transfer. Critical for grapplers, strikers, throwers, and rotational sport athletes.
- Scoop throw for posterior chain to anterior chain transfer.

Keep med ball sets short. Two to five reps, full intent, full recovery.

## Current to Goal Gap Thinking

- Goal. Peak power expression, sport transfer, maintenance.
- Current capacity. Landing quality, jump symmetry, single leg control.
- Sport load. Striking volume, jumping in sport, throwing in sport.
- Recovery. Lower-body soreness budget. CNS budget.
- Pain. Knee, foot, ankle, lower back tolerance.
- Equipment. Box, open floor, soft floor, med balls of varied weight, wall.
- Limiting quality. Landing mechanics, reactive ability, rotational transfer.

## If, Then Routing

- If landing mechanics are not clean, then no depth or drop jumps. Stay at pogos, skips, and broad jumps.
- If the athlete is in peak strength week, then keep one to two short plyo or throw sets for sharpness only.
- If the sport is rotational, then prioritize rotational med ball work over linear jumps.
- If knee or foot pain is present, then strip impact plyos. Use med ball work and seated rotational throws.
- If CNS is fried, then skip the depth jump session and use light pogos as priming only.

## Dosing Rules

- Total contacts per session sit low for advanced athletes who lift heavy. Twenty to fifty contacts is often enough for non-impact plyos.
- Depth and drop jumps. Ten to twenty contacts in a session, rarely more.
- Place plyos early in the session. Fresh, not fatigued.
- Pair contrasted lift and plyo with adequate rest. Three to five minutes between contrast pairs.

## Fatigue Triggers and Review Cues

- Loss of bounce. Slower ground contact. End the session.
- Knee or foot irritation increasing across the week. Pause and review.
- Calf strain history flaring. Strip impact, use seated and low-impact options.

## Prerequisites and Substitutions

- Prerequisites. Strength baseline (commonly cited reference points include a multiple of bodyweight squat for higher-stress plyos). Clean landing mechanics. Pain free under low intensity contacts.
- Substitutions. Pool jumps for impact intolerant athlete. Wall ball for overhead slam if shoulder is irritable. Seated rotational throw if standing rotation is aggravating.

## Source Domain Notes

Plyometric practice, rate of force development literature, jump training practice, rotational power practice, return to sport practice.

## Program Generator Notes

- Tags: plyometrics, power, med_ball, rotational_power, contrast.
- Routing: include at low to moderate dose across most advanced athletes. Match plyo type to sport demand.
- When to include: any block where power must be maintained.
- When to exclude: acute lower-limb pain, peak fatigue weeks, return from injury without clearance.
- What to avoid: depth jumps for unprepared athletes, rotational throws into an irritable hip or shoulder, high contact volume as a conditioning finisher.

---

# 51. Swim-Based Conditioning for Strength and Combat Athletes: Low-Impact Capacity and Recovery Options

## Why Swimming Is Underused

Water removes impact. Water adds breath restriction. Water rewards calm output. For an advanced athlete with cranky joints, a long lifting career, or an aggressive mat week, the pool is often the cleanest conditioning tool available.

It is also the most neglected, because the athlete is rarely a good swimmer.

## What Swimming Offers

- Aerobic base development with low joint cost.
- Breath restricted work that builds tolerance to discomfort.
- Recovery sessions that flush without adding pounding.
- Aqua jogging as a near pain-free substitute for running during flare ups.

## Conditioning Modes in the Pool

- Easy continuous swim. Aerobic base. Long, calm.
- Interval swim. Short hard efforts with timed rest.
- Aqua jog. Deep water running with belt. Aerobic substitute for runners.
- Walking and resisted water work. Low CNS, easy on joints.
- Breath-restricted drills. Use carefully. See cautions.

## Current to Goal Gap Thinking

- Goal. Aerobic base, recovery, in-season maintenance, off-season variety.
- Current capacity. Comfort in water. Swim stroke quality.
- Sport load. Mat volume, running mileage, lifting volume.
- Recovery. The athlete who is overcooked benefits from one to two easy pool sessions per week.
- Pain. Joint, foot, ankle, knee tolerance. The pool is often the answer.
- Equipment. Lap pool, aqua jog belt, kickboard, pull buoy.
- Limiting quality. Aerobic base, joint tolerance, breath tolerance.

## If, Then Routing

- If the athlete has a stress reaction or impact intolerance, then move runs to aqua jog and easy swim.
- If a meet or fight is approaching and the joints are flared, then add one pool flush per week.
- If the swimmer is technically weak, then keep efforts at conversational pace until stroke is clean.
- If breath restricted work is on the table, then never alone, never under pressure, and never to failure. See cautions.

## Cautions on Breath Restricted Work

- No solo breath holding. Period.
- No prolonged underwater swims. Hypoxic blackout is a real risk.
- Brief intervals with short breath holds may have a role in some sport prep, but the safer default is paced swimming and not breath restriction.
- For combat athletes interested in air hunger tolerance, build it through nasal breathing on the bike or rower, not under water.

## Dosing Templates

- Recovery flush. Twenty to thirty minutes easy continuous swim or aqua jog.
- Aerobic base. Thirty to forty-five minutes easy continuous swim. Light effort.
- Aqua jog substitute. Forty to sixty minutes deep water running, varied cadence.

## Fatigue Triggers and Review Cues

- New shoulder pain after swim sessions. Pause overhead stroke. Switch to kicking or aqua jog. Review with a clinician if not resolving.
- Chronic ear infections in pool athletes. Standard ENT review.
- Sleep disruption after evening pool sessions. Move sessions earlier.

## Prerequisites and Substitutions

- Prerequisites. Basic water safety. Stroke that allows continuous effort, even if slow.
- Substitutions. Aqua jog when stroke is too rough to sustain. Rower for the athlete without pool access.

## Source Domain Notes

Aquatic training practice, return to running practice, masters swimming domain, low-impact rehab practice, breath safety practice.

## Program Generator Notes

- Tags: swim, aqua_jog, low_impact, recovery, aerobic_base.
- Routing: offer as a tool to any athlete with impact intolerance or recovery need.
- When to include: deload, in-season high impact phase, post-injury return, hybrid offseason variety.
- When to exclude: athlete with open wound, ear infection, or active shoulder symptoms aggravated by stroke.
- What to avoid: programming breath holds, scheduling hard pool intervals on the same day as hard mat work, replacing skill swimming with junk laps for a non-swimmer.

---

# 52. GPP Decision Rules: Choosing the Right Support Work for the Athlete, Goal, and Fatigue Budget

## How This Article Sits Inside the Cluster

This is the routing piece. Articles forty-five through fifty-one cover the tools. This one orders them and writes the rules.

## The Five-Question Filter

Before any GPP or conditioning piece is added, the coach answers:

- What is the priority quality for this block?
- What is the limiting structure right now?
- What is the fatigue budget left after priority work?
- Which equipment and modality is pain tolerant?
- What does the calendar require in the next four weeks?

## Quality and Tool Matrix

- Aerobic base. Bike, rower, ski erg, easy swim, sled walks, hills at easy pace.
- Tempo and extensive intervals. Bike, rower, ski erg, runs for tolerant athletes.
- Alactic power. Short hill sprints, bike or ski erg sprints, broad jumps, throws.
- Alactic capacity. Short bike intervals, repeated short hills, battle ropes, sandbag bursts.
- Sport-specific intervals. Round timed work for combat, event mimic for strongman.
- Loaded GPP. Sled, prowler, yoke, carries, medleys.
- Plyometrics. Pogos, skips, broad jumps, box jumps, bounds, depth jumps, throws.
- Restoration. Walks, easy bike, mobility, pool flush.

## Selection by Limiting Structure

- Trunk stiffness limited. Bias suitcase carries, sled push, slow yoke if tolerated.
- Scapular control limited. Bias unilateral carries, controlled rowing, throw progressions.
- Hip capacity limited. Bias sled push, low box step ups, A and B skips.
- Knee tolerance limited. Bias backward sled drag, bike, pool, low box work.
- Foot and ankle limited. Bias pool, bike, controlled pogos only when tolerated.
- Neck and grip limited for grapplers. Bias bike or rower over rope and carry, reduce grip GPP.
- Aerobic base limited. Bias Zone 2 work across multiple modalities.
- Repeated sprint ability limited. Bias short interval work matched to sport demand.

## Decision Rules

- Rule one. The priority quality gets the cleanest day. GPP supports it, never crowds it.
- Rule two. If two pieces compete for the same tissue, drop one.
- Rule three. Loaded GPP and heavy lower work do not share a day at full dose.
- Rule four. Hard intervals and heavy lower work do not share a day at full dose.
- Rule five. Skill and sport sessions come before GPP in the day order.
- Rule six. When in doubt, do less. Capacity comes from repeatable weeks, not from a hero session.

## If, Then Routing Across Categories

- If athlete is an advanced powerlifter, then GPP is two Zone 2 sessions, one short loaded carry tail, optional plyo priming, restoration as needed.
- If athlete is a strongman, then GPP includes event mimic medleys, one Zone 2, one alactic capacity, restoration.
- If athlete is a BJJ competitor, then GPP includes two Zone 2, one sport-specific interval co-scheduled with mat, light plyo and throw, careful grip and neck management.
- If athlete is MMA, then GPP includes sport-specific round intervals, two Zone 2, light alactic, careful sparring count.
- If athlete is calisthenics or gymnastics focused, then GPP includes scapular and trunk biased carries, low-impact aerobic base, restorative pool work, careful plyo dosing.
- If athlete is hybrid, then priority quality drives the calendar. The other quality holds at maintenance.

## Fatigue Triggers Across Tools

- Bar speed drop on planned submax. Pull GPP volume before SPP volume.
- Resting heart rate climbing. Cut intensity, keep easy aerobic.
- Sleep disruption after late conditioning. Move sessions earlier.
- Recurrent local pain at one site. Pause that tool. Switch modality. Coach review. Clinician review if not resolving.

## Pre-Competition Tapering

- Two to three weeks out. Cut interval volume. Keep alactic taps and easy aerobic.
- One week out. Walks, very easy spins, sharp short throws or jumps, technique only.
- Day before. Movement and rest.

## Prerequisites and Substitutions Summary

- Loaded carries need a clean brace and hinge.
- Plyometrics need clean landing mechanics. Depth jumps need a strength baseline.
- Intervals need an aerobic base.
- Pool work needs basic water competence.
- Breath restricted work is not recommended without specialist supervision. The safer path is paced nasal breathing on a bike or rower.

## Conservative Injury Language for the Generator

When the article assigns work near a sensitive site, output should use phrases like:

- May support tolerance.
- Often a more tolerable pathway.
- Pause and review.
- Coach review. Clinician review if symptoms persist.

The generator should never claim to prevent injury, diagnose pain, or guarantee a result.

## Source Domain Notes

GPP literature, applied conditioning practice, sport specific energy systems work, autoregulation, recovery management, return to sport practice.

## Program Generator Notes

- Tags: gpp_routing, decision_rules, fatigue_budget, calendar_aware.
- Routing: this article supplies the priority filter and modality selector for every other article in the cluster.
- When to include: every athlete. Treat as the rulebook layer.
- When to exclude: never. The rules still apply during peak and deload, only the dose changes.
- What to avoid: a flat schedule that treats every tool as equal, hard intervals stacked on heavy lower days, prescribing GPP that maps onto the limiter rather than supporting it.

## PART B — ARTICLES 52-102 (full text)

Raz Pilosof's Strength Program Coaching Engine
Advanced Layer: Articles 52–102

---
About This Document

This file contains the complete advanced layer of the Raz Pilosof Strength Program Coaching Engine, covering Articles 52 through 102. It is intended for vault use as a direct reference layer for program generation, ongoing coaching, and decision-making across the full range of athlete types in the system.

The articles are organized into eight clusters, each addressing a distinct domain of coaching logic. Together they form an integrated, decision-driven system for programming, adjusting, and managing training in real-world conditions.

Scope: Advanced lifters, BJJ and MMA athletes, calisthenics and gymnastics athletes, powerlifters, strongmen, hybrid athletes, and the coaches who program for them.

How to use this document: Each cluster and article can be read independently, but the full system operates as an integrated engine. Cluster F stress-cost scoring is a prerequisite for most volume decisions in Cluster G. Cluster H nutrition signals modify session prescriptions from all clusters. Cluster J governs ongoing coaching behavior after programs are delivered. Cluster K applies sport-specific constraints to base templates. Cluster L governs competition preparation and peaking. Cluster M provides the data interpretation and readiness layer that overrides or confirms all other prescriptions.

Language standards throughout: All content is training and programming guidance, not medical advice. No diagnoses, no injury-prevention guarantees, no medical claims. Conservative, load-management language is used throughout.

Cluster E — GPP and Conditioning Framework

Note: Articles 45–51 of Cluster E are contained in a separate file. Article 52 is reproduced here as it serves as the routing hub for the entire GPP cluster and is the entry point for the advanced layer.
