Article 52: GPP Decision Rules — Choosing the Right Support Work for the Athlete, Goal, and Fatigue Budget

How This Article Sits Inside the Cluster

This is the routing piece. Articles 45 through 51 cover the tools. This one orders them and writes the rules.

The Five-Question Filter

Before any GPP or conditioning piece is added, the coach answers:

- What is the priority quality for this block?
- What is the limiting structure right now?
- What is the fatigue budget left after priority work?
- Which equipment and modality is pain tolerant?
- What does the calendar require in the next four weeks?

Quality and Tool Matrix

- Aerobic base. Bike, rower, ski erg, easy swim, sled walks, hills at easy pace.
- Tempo and extensive intervals. Bike, rower, ski erg, runs for tolerant athletes.
- Alactic power. Short hill sprints, bike or ski erg sprints, broad jumps, throws.
- Alactic capacity. Short bike intervals, repeated short hills, battle ropes, sandbag bursts.
- Sport-specific intervals. Round timed work for combat, event mimic for strongman.
- Loaded GPP. Sled, prowler, yoke, carries, medleys.
- Plyometrics. Pogos, skips, broad jumps, box jumps, bounds, depth jumps, throws.
- Restoration. Walks, easy bike, mobility, pool flush.

Selection by Limiting Structure

- Trunk stiffness limited. Bias suitcase carries, sled push, slow yoke if tolerated.
- Scapular control limited. Bias unilateral carries, controlled rowing, throw progressions.
- Hip capacity limited. Bias sled push, low box step ups, A and B skips.
- Knee tolerance limited. Bias backward sled drag, bike, pool, low box work.
- Foot and ankle limited. Bias pool, bike, controlled pogos only when tolerated.
- Neck and grip limited for grapplers. Bias bike or rower over rope and carry, reduce grip GPP.
- Aerobic base limited. Bias Zone 2 work across multiple modalities.
- Repeated sprint ability limited. Bias short interval work matched to sport demand.

Decision Rules

- Rule one. The priority quality gets the cleanest day. GPP supports it, never crowds it.
- Rule two. If two pieces compete for the same tissue, drop one.
- Rule three. Loaded GPP and heavy lower work do not share a day at full dose.
- Rule four. Hard intervals and heavy lower work do not share a day at full dose.
- Rule five. Skill and sport sessions come before GPP in the day order.
- Rule six. When in doubt, do less. Capacity comes from repeatable weeks, not from a hero session.

If, Then Routing Across Categories

- If athlete is an advanced powerlifter, then GPP is two Zone 2 sessions, one short loaded carry tail, optional plyo priming, restoration as needed.
- If athlete is a strongman, then GPP includes event mimic medleys, one Zone 2, one alactic capacity, restoration.
- If athlete is a BJJ competitor, then GPP includes two Zone 2, one sport-specific interval co-scheduled with mat, light plyo and throw, careful grip and neck management.
- If athlete is MMA, then GPP includes sport-specific round intervals, two Zone 2, light alactic, careful sparring count.
- If athlete is calisthenics or gymnastics focused, then GPP includes scapular and trunk biased carries, low-impact aerobic base, restorative pool work, careful plyo dosing.
- If athlete is hybrid, then priority quality drives the calendar. The other quality holds at maintenance.

Fatigue Triggers Across Tools

- Bar speed drop on planned submax. Pull GPP volume before SPP volume.
- Resting heart rate climbing. Cut intensity, keep easy aerobic.
- Sleep disruption after late conditioning. Move sessions earlier.
- Recurrent local pain at one site. Pause that tool. Switch modality. Coach review. Clinician review if not resolving.

Pre-Competition Tapering

- Two to three weeks out. Cut interval volume. Keep alactic taps and easy aerobic.
- One week out. Walks, very easy spins, sharp short throws or jumps, technique only.
- Day before. Movement and rest.

Prerequisites and Substitutions Summary

- Loaded carries need a clean brace and hinge.
- Plyometrics need clean landing mechanics. Depth jumps need a strength baseline.
- Intervals need an aerobic base.
- Pool work needs basic water competence.
- Breath restricted work is not recommended without specialist supervision. The safer path is paced nasal breathing on a bike or rower.

Conservative Injury Language for the Generator

When the article assigns work near a sensitive site, output should use phrases like:

- May support tolerance.
- Often a more tolerable pathway.
- Pause and review.
- Coach review. Clinician review if symptoms persist.

The generator should never claim to prevent injury, diagnose pain, or guarantee a result.

- When to exclude: never. The rules still apply during peak and deload, only the dose changes.

Cluster F — Combat Athlete Strength Training and the Stress-Cost Scoring Layer

Knowledge vault module for the Raz Pilosof strength program generator. Audience: advanced combat athletes (MMA, BJJ, wrestling, grappling) and the coaches who program for them. Scope: Articles 53–60. This module assumes the reader already understands periodization basics, RPE/RIR autoregulation, and primary lift mechanics.

Important framing on language. Everything below is training and programming guidance, not medical advice. Where tissue, region, or symptom language appears, it describes training decisions and load management, never diagnosis or treatment. The generator must never claim to prevent injury, cure, rehabilitate, or guarantee an outcome. Use conservative, non-diagnostic phrasing at all times.

---
Module 0 — The Exercise Selection / Stress-Cost Scoring Layer (Shared Engine)

This is the connective tissue across Articles 53–60. Every article references these constructs. The generator should treat this section as the canonical definition and never redefine the formulas inconsistently elsewhere.

The core idea: a combat athlete has a finite, multi-channel recovery budget, and the sport itself already spends most of it. Strength work is the last claimant on that budget, not the first. So we do not select exercises by "what builds the most strength" — we select by strength return per unit of total systemic cost, given what the mat already took this week.

0.1 The Cost Channels

We track five recovery channels independently because they recover at different rates and the sport loads them unevenly:

Channel  What it is  Recovers in  Primary sport drain
Spinal  Cumulative compressive/shear/flexion-extension load on the spine and surrounding tissue  48–96 h (slow)  Scrambles, takedown defense, guard, bridging, stacking
CNS  High-threshold motor output, maximal/near-maximal intent, speed-strength  24–72 h (intent-dependent)  Hard sparring, explosive exchanges, max-effort scrambles
Peripheral (local)  Muscle/connective tissue fatigue and soreness in a specific region  24–96 h (region-dependent)  Grip, neck, forearms, hips, lower back
Skill/coordination  Motor-learning bandwidth and freshness for technique work  12–48 h  Technical drilling, new positions
Tissue tolerance  A constraint, not a fuel tank — current capacity of a region to accept contact/load without symptom flare  Variable  Contact, repeated impact, end-range loading

0.2 Spinal Debt

The spine is the single most over-drafted channel in grappling, so it gets its own running ledger.

Spinal Debt (SD) formula — weekly running score:

SDweek = Σ (SpinalLoadi × Densityi × FatigueStatei)

where for each session i:
  SpinalLoadi   = sum of exercise spinal-compression/shear scores (0–3 each)
                    + mat spinal load estimate
  Density_i       = 1.0 normal, 1.25 if two spinal-heavy sessions <24 h apart
  FatigueStatei = 1.0 fresh, 1.3 if entering session already sore/stiff in low back


Mat spinal-load estimate (per session):

Session type  Mat spinal load
Technical drilling, flow  1
Positional rounds, moderate  3
Hard sparring / live wrestling  5
Competition / hard takedown battles  7

SD interpretation (rolling 7-day):

SD_week  State  Programming response
0–10  Green  Normal spinal loading allowed (deadlift variants, loaded carries, heavy hinge)
11–18  Amber  Cap axial loading; prefer supported/neutral-spine variants; no max hinge
19–26  Red  Remove spinal-compressive lifting; substitute to low-SD options
27+  Black  Strength session becomes restorative only; reassess weekly mat load

Coaching note: SD is additive across mat and gym. The most common programming error is scoring the gym in isolation. A "light" 70% deadlift day on top of a hard wrestling week is not light — it is a Red-zone decision.

0.3 CNS Fatigue Score

CNS cost is driven by intent and proximity to maximum, not just load. A fast, maximal-intent movement at 60% can cost more CNS than a grindy 85% single.

CNS Fatigue Score (CFS) — per session, then summed weekly:

CFSsession = Σ (Intenti × Proximityi × Volumefactor_i)

  Intent_i      = 1 (controlled), 2 (forceful), 3 (maximal/ballistic)
  Proximity_i   = 1 (≤70% / RIR≥3), 2 (70–85% / RIR 1–2), 3 (>85% / RIR 0)
  Volume_factor = 1 (≤3 hard sets), 1.5 (4–6), 2 (7+)

Add mat CNS load:
  +2 hard sparring,  +3 competition,  +1 hard positional


CFS interpretation (per microcycle / rolling 7-day):

CFS_week  State  Response
≤14  Recoverable  Plan a true high day this week
15–24  Loaded  One high day max; keep speed work crisp and low-volume
25–32  Saturated  No max-effort gym work; convert to medium; protect sparring quality
33+  Overdrawn  Strength = maintenance dose only; flag deload candidate

Rule of thumb: sparring quality is the canary. If bar speed and sparring sharpness both drop, CNS is overdrawn regardless of what the number says — the number serves the eye, not the reverse.

0.4 Peripheral (Local) Fatigue Table

Region  Typical recovery after hard load  Sport reload demand  Reload caution
Grip / forearms  24–48 h, but cumulative across a week  Every session (gi especially)  High-grip lifting day before gi sparring degrades grip endurance on the mat
Neck  48–72 h (slow, tendon/connective heavy)  Constant in grappling  Never train neck to true failure within 72 h of hard sparring
Lower back (erectors/QL)  48–96 h  Constant  Most over-drafted; see Spinal Debt
Hips/adductors/glutes  24–72 h  High (guard, shots)  Heavy adductor eccentrics can degrade shot speed for 48 h
Shoulders (cuff/scapular)  24–72 h  High (framing, posting, pressing)  Pressing volume + heavy posting in sparring stacks local cost
Lats/upper back  24–48 h  Moderate  Recovers fast; good "safe" volume sink
Quads  24–48 h  Moderate  Heavy eccentric squats sore for shots/sprawls
Hamstrings  48–72 h  Moderate–high  Eccentric-biased work lingers; respect before live

0.5 Exercise Stress-Cost Scoring Dimensions

Every exercise the generator considers is scored 0–3 on each dimension (0 = negligible, 3 = severe). The sum is the exercise's Total Stress Cost (TSC); individual dimensions also gate placement.

Dimension  What it captures
Spinal compression/shear  Axial load + shear on spine
Eccentric cost  Magnitude/duration of lengthening tension
Grip/forearm cost  Demand on grip and forearm flexors
Local tissue cost  Concentrated load on one region
Skill cost  Coordination/learning bandwidth used
CNS cost  Intent × proximity to max
Soreness risk  Likelihood of next-day soreness affecting mat
Sport interference  Direct conflict with imminent mat quality
Injury-constraint cost  How much it loads a currently sensitive/limited region

TSC = sum of all nine (0–27). But TSC alone never decides placement — the highest individual dimension governs day-type and timing. A TSC-9 exercise that scores 3 on Spinal in a Red SD week is disallowed even though its total looks moderate.

0.6 Low-Cost Substitution Library

When a channel is over-budget, swap to same-pattern, lower-cost options:

Pattern  Standard (higher cost)  Low-spinal sub  Low-CNS sub  Low-grip sub
Hinge  Conventional DL  Hip thrust, back-extension  RDL at tempo light  Machine ham, hip thrust
Squat  Back squat  Belt squat, hack  Tempo goblet  Belt squat, leg press
Pull  Heavy bent row  Chest-supported row  Light cable row  Straps / chest-supported
Press  Barbell OHP  Landmine, machine press  DB press at RIR 3  Machine press
Carry  Heavy farmer  Front-rack carry (lighter)  Short distance  Yoke/zercher (less grip)

0.7 How Scoring Drives Programming

1. Read the week first. Estimate mat spinal/CNS load before touching the gym plan. The gym fills the remaining budget.
2. Assign day types — High / Medium / Low — based on CFS and SD state, then place high days away from the hardest mat sessions.
3. Filter exercises by the governing dimension for that day and that athlete's current constraints.
4. Substitute, don't cancel. A channel being over-budget changes the exercise, rarely the session. Maintenance trumps zero.
5. Deload triggers when two or more channels are simultaneously in Red, OR when sparring quality drops for 2+ sessions, OR a constraint flag escalates.

Universal day-type definitions used across the cluster:

- High day: highest intent/load gym session of the week; only when CFS ≤14 and SD ≤10; placed 24–48 h before a rest or light mat day, never before hard sparring.
- Medium day: moderate load, RIR 1–3, no max intent; the default during camps.
- Low/Restorative day: pumps, blood flow, positional isometrics, mobility-with-load; CNS ≈ 0; usable even in Red weeks.

---
Article 53 — Strength Training Around Hard Sparring Weeks

Coaching Problem

Hard sparring weeks are the highest-value, highest-cost training the athlete does. The mistake coaches make is treating the gym as an independent variable — programming a normal hypertrophy or strength block on top of weeks where the mat is already drawing maximally on the CNS and spinal channels. The result is degraded sparring quality, accumulating Spinal Debt, and the illusion that the athlete is "lazy" when they are simply overdrawn. The coach who fixes this stops asking "what should the gym do this week?" and starts asking "what budget did the mat leave me?"

The deeper trap is that hard sparring feels like conditioning, so coaches under-count its strength-channel cost. A live wrestling round is a series of near-maximal isometric and explosive efforts under fatigue — it is, in CNS and spinal terms, indistinguishable from a heavy lifting session the body has to recover from. Two of those in a week, plus a "normal" lower-body day, is three heavy CNS/spinal exposures stacked on a recovery budget built for maybe two.

Operating Principles

- The mat is the priority stimulus during a hard sparring week. The gym protects and maintains; it does not compete.
- Strength qualities are retained for 2–3 weeks even at maintenance dosing. There is no urgency to "make progress" during a hard sparring block — only urgency to not erode it.
- The two channels most at risk are CNS and Spinal. Peripheral grip is a close third in gi weeks.
- Place the gym in the troughs, not on the peaks. The best lifting day of a hard sparring week is the one with the most recovery buffer before the next live session.

Stress-Cost Implications

A hard sparring session adds +2 CFS and +5 mat spinal load by the Module 0 estimates. Two hard sparring sessions in a week place an athlete near Amber CFS and Amber/Red SD before any lifting. Therefore:

- Heavy hinge (Spinal 3) is effectively disallowed mid-week.
- Max-effort gym work (CNS 3) competes directly with sparring sharpness and is converted to medium.
- Grip-cost ≥2 the day before gi sparring degrades on-mat grip endurance — a direct, observable interference cost.

Worked SD/CFS example — a typical 2-hard-spar week (Tue + Thu live):

Mat-only baseline before any lifting:
- Mon drilling: spinal 1, CFS 0
- Tue hard spar: spinal 5, CFS +2
- Wed technical: spinal 1, CFS 0
- Thu hard spar: spinal 5, CFS +2
- Fri positional: spinal 3, CFS +1
- Mat-only SD ≈ 15 (Amber), mat-only CFS ≈ 5.

Adding a naive "normal" leg day Wednesday: back squat 4×5 @ 82% (Spinal 2, CNS 6) and conventional deadlift 3×3 @ 85% (Spinal 3, CNS 6) pushes SD into Red and dumps fresh CNS fatigue directly into Thursday's sparring. The Density 1.25 multiplier applies when the Wednesday lift sits less than 24 h after Tuesday's spar and is spinal-heavy.

Programming Framework

A repaired 2-hard-spar week:

Day  Mat  Gym day-type  Focus  Notes
Mon  Drilling  Medium  Belt squat 4×6 @ RIR 2, chest-supported row 3×10, front-rack carry  Spinal ≤1, CNS modest, full buffer to Tue
Tue  Hard spar  None / AM restorative  Neck isometric maintenance, light grip flush  Protect the spar
Wed  Technical  Low  Hip thrust 3×8, back-extension 3×12, sled, lat volume  Spinal 0–1, CNS ≈ 0
Thu  Hard spar  None  —  Protect the spar
Fri  Positional  Medium  Landmine press 4×6, pull volume, hip/adductor low-cost  Upper bias, low spinal
Sat  Optional roll  High (only if CFS≤14)  One heavy main lift, low-spinal  Most buffer to next hard spar
Sun  Off  —  —  —

Exercise Selection and Tradeoffs

- Belt squat vs back squat: belt squat keeps the quad/leg-drive stimulus at Spinal 0–1 instead of Spinal 2–3. Large drop in spinal cost for a trade of slightly less trunk/bracing stimulus — which the mat already over-supplies.
- Hip thrust vs deadlift: the hip thrust delivers hip-extension strength at Spinal 0–1 and near-zero shear, versus Spinal 3 for conventional pulls. Acceptable mid-week.
- Chest-supported row vs heavy bent row: removes both the spinal cost and the grip-as-limiter problem.
- Sled vs any eccentric leg work: sled is concentric-only (Eccentric 0, Soreness 0–1), so it builds work capacity and leg drive without next-day soreness that would slow shots.

If/Then Rules

- If two hard spars are scheduled and CFS ≥15 → no High gym day this week; cap at Medium.
- If SD reaches Red → replace all hinge/squat axial work with belt squat + back-extension at low load.
- If sparring sharpness drops two sessions running → cut gym to restorative regardless of the calendar.
- If a heavy lift is desired → place it the day after the last hard spar with ≥36 h before the next, never the day before.
- If gi sparring is next-day → grip-cost ≤1 in the gym today.
- If two spinal-heavy exposures fall <24 h apart → apply Density 1.25 and re-check the SD tier before finalizing.

Common Mistakes

- Programming a "leg day" (squat + deadlift) into a hard sparring week and blaming the athlete for flat rounds.
- Chasing PRs because the block "called for it" while the real priority was the sparring.
- Treating gym soreness as harmless — DOMS in quads/hams directly degrades shot and sprawl speed.
- Scoring the gym in isolation and missing the Density multiplier when a lift sits next to a spar.

Generator-Use Rules

- Detect "hard sparring week" flag → cap gym at Medium by default; unlock one High day only if CFS ≤14 and SD ≤10 after adding mat estimates.
- Compute Density 1.25 automatically whenever a spinal-scored lift is within 24 h of a mat session of spinal load ≥5.
- Auto-substitute any Spinal-3 or CNS-3 exercise to the 0.6 library equivalent when SD/CFS thresholds are crossed, and log the substitution reason.
- Never schedule grip-cost ≥2 within 24 h before a gi session; route to a low-grip variant.
- Place the highest-load gym session at the calendar slot with maximal buffer to the next live session; never the day before.
- Output conservative language only; never claim the plan "prevents injury."

---
Article 54 — Strength Training During MMA/BJJ Competition Camp

Coaching Problem

Camp compresses everything: more sparring, more conditioning, weight management, and rising psychological stress, all while the temptation to "get stronger for the fight" peaks. Adding training stress in camp usually subtracts from fight readiness. The job of strength work in camp is fidelity — keep the qualities the athlete already built, stay fresh, and get out of the sport's way.

Most blown camps share a signature: the gym volume that worked in the off-season was carried into camp unchanged, so total systemic load climbed week over week until sparring quality, then mood, then bodyweight management all degraded together. The strength coach's contribution to a good camp is almost entirely subtractive — knowing precisely how little is enough and removing everything else.

Operating Principles

- Camp is a taper of strength stress and a peak of sport stress. Gym volume should decrease across camp while intensity is kept just high enough to maintain.
- Maintenance requires surprisingly little: approximately 2 hard sets per major pattern at a meaningful load (≥80% or RIR ≤2), 1–2×/week, retains strength for a standard 6–8 week camp.
- Weight cuts compress every recovery channel. As the cut tightens, all gym cost dimensions must drop.
- Novelty is the enemy in camp. A new exercise carries skill cost and soreness risk — two dimensions that buy nothing and can cost a sparring session.

Programming Framework — Camp Phasing

Camp phase  Weeks out  Gym dose  Day types  CNS ceiling
Early  8–6  Highest gym tolerance of camp  2 Medium, occasional 1 High early-week  CNS ≤2
Mid  5–3  Maintenance, volume trimmed  2 Medium  CNS ≤2
Late  2  Minimal dose  1 Medium + 1 Low  CNS ≤1
Fight week  1  Movement only  1–2 Low/restorative, sharp and brief  CNS ≤1, no soreness

Maintenance template (mid-camp, 2×/week):

- Day A (Medium): belt squat 2×3 @ RIR 2 (Spinal ≤1), horizontal pull 3×8, front-rack carry, neck isometric maintenance. Placed ≥36 h before hard sparring.
- Day B (Medium): hip thrust 3×6, landmine press 3×6 @ RIR 2, low-cost hip/adductor, grip maintenance only. Placed on a lighter mat day.

The gym's job is to fall as the mat rises. The gym contribution line descends (12 → 8 → 5 → 2 in CFS units) while the mat line climbs across the camp. If the gym line is flat or rising across a camp, the plan is wrong regardless of how good each individual session looks.

Exercise Selection and Tradeoffs

- Machines/belt squat vs free-bar maxes: machine and belt-supported variants deliver near-equal maintenance stimulus at lower spinal/skill/CNS cost.
- Short crisp jumps vs heavy lower-body: 2–3 low-volume jumps keep speed-strength "switched on" at a fraction of the CNS cost of heavy squats. Cut entirely in late camp.
- Sled vs eccentric leg work: sled keeps conditioning and leg drive alive with Eccentric 0 — no soreness to compromise sparring. Eccentric-biased work is a late-camp liability.
- During the cut: drop grip-cost and eccentric-cost to ≤1; a glycogen- and fluid-depleted athlete bruises and sores more easily and recovers slower.

If/Then Rules

- If weeks-out ≤2 → no exercise with Eccentric cost ≥2.
- If in an active weight cut → all gym TSC ceilings drop one tier; convert High→Medium, Medium→Low.
- If CFS Saturated for 2 consecutive weeks → drop to one gym session, maintenance only, and flag for the head coach.
- If the athlete reports a flagged sensitive region → reduce injury-constraint-cost loading to 0 on that region; substitute the pattern.
- If fight week → only crisp, sub-maximal, confidence-building movement; nothing that can create soreness.
- If a new exercise is requested in camp → deny by default (skill + soreness cost); keep the patterns the athlete already owns.

Generator-Use Rules

- Require a "weeks_out" input; auto-select the phase template and clamp CNS/eccentric/spinal ceilings accordingly.
- If a "weightcutactive" flag is set, drop every cost ceiling one additional tier and disable jumps/throws.
- In fight week, hard-block any Eccentric ≥2, CNS ≥2, Spinal ≥2 exercise, and any exercise not already used earlier in camp.
- Auto-trim volume_factor across phases so gym CFS contribution falls each phase even if the athlete asks for more.
- Never present strength changes as fight-outcome guarantees; keep language about maintenance and freshness.

---
Article 55 — Lower-Back Management for Guard Players and Wrestlers

Coaching Problem

Guard players and wrestlers live in repeated flexion, extension, rotation, and axial load on the mat. The lower back is the single most over-drafted region in grappling. When coaches then program heavy axial lifting "for a strong back," they double-dip the most fatigued, slowest-recovering region. The goal is building tolerant, capable trunk strength while keeping the spinal channel out of chronic debt — framed strictly as load management, never as treating a condition.

The conceptual error is treating the deadlift as the definition of a "strong back." For a grappler whose mat life is already a relentless spinal load, the deadlift is often the least cost-efficient way to build trunk capacity. Carries, anti-movement work, and hip-dominant patterns build a robust, capable trunk at a fraction of the spinal cost.

Operating Principles

- The lower back's gym budget is a remainder, computed after mat spinal load. Spinal Debt is the governing number here.
- Build trunk capacity primarily through lower-spinal-cost tools: anti-movement bracing, supported extension, controlled loaded carries, and hip-dominant work that spares the axial spine.
- Heavy axial loading (conventional deadlifts, heavy good mornings) is a periodized minority of the work, used only in Green SD windows and away from hard mat days.
- A stiff-back day is a signal to downgrade, not to "work through it." The Fatigue_State multiplier exists precisely for this.

Programming Framework

Trunk capacity menu, organized by spinal cost:

Spinal cost  Tools  Weekly role
0–1  Loaded carries, suitcase/front-rack, dead-bug progressions, Pallof, reverse hyper light, back-extension (bodyweight→light)  Staple — most of the volume
2  RDL moderate, Zercher carry, moderate good morning, trap-bar (neutral)  Periodic, Green SD only
3  Heavy conventional/sumo, heavy good morning  Rare, Green SD, away from hard mat

Sample week (wrestler, 2 hard mat sessions):

- Early week (Medium): front-rack carries + suitcase carries + Pallof anti-rotation + hip thrust 3×8. SD contribution ≈ 1–2.
- Mid week (Low): back-extension (controlled) + reverse hyper light + dead-bug. SD ≈ 0–1.
- One moderate hinge (trap-bar or RDL, Spinal 2) only if SD ≤10 on a buffered day.

If/Then Rules

- If SD Amber → all hinging capped at Spinal ≤1 (hip thrust, back-extension); no barbell deadlift.
- If SD Red → trunk work becomes carries + bracing only; remove all hinge load.
- If the athlete reports low-back stiffness entering a session → apply Fatigue_State 1.3, downgrade the plan one tier, and avoid end-range loading (conservative, non-diagnostic).
- If a Green SD window appears (light mat week) → that is when to schedule the rare heavy hinge.
- If a wrestler has heavy takedown sparring next day → no Spinal ≥2 today.
- If Density and Fatigue_State multipliers both apply to one session → re-check the SD tier before finalizing; they compound.

Generator-Use Rules

- Compute SD every week including mat estimates; gate hinge cost by the SD tier.
- Apply FatigueState 1.3 automatically when a "lowback_stiff" flag is set for the day, and Density 1.25 when a spinal lift is <24 h from a mat session of spinal load ≥5.
- Default the hinge slot to hip thrust / back-extension; only offer barbell deadlift when SD ≤10 and ≥48 h buffer to hard mat.
- Use load-management language only — never "fix," "heal," "rehab," or "prevent injury."

---
Article 56 — Neck Training for Grapplers: Isometrics, Harness Work, and Contact Tolerance

Coaching Problem

The neck is loaded constantly in grappling — framing, posting, bridging, defending guillotines and front headlocks, scrambles. It is a slow-recovering, connective-tissue-heavy region (48–72 h). Coaches either ignore it or train it aggressively to failure, both errors. The objective is building neck strength and contact tolerance with conservative, sub-maximal, well-spaced dosing — described as capacity work, never as injury prevention.

The neck punishes impatience more than almost any region. It adapts slowly, sores deeply, and the sport never gives it a day off. The coach's job is to find the small, repeatable dose that builds capacity over months without ever colliding with a hard sparring session.

Operating Principles

- Neck work is almost never trained to failure and almost never within 72 h of hard sparring.
- Isometrics first. They build strength with minimal soreness and minimal local tissue cost.
- Progress time, then position, then gentle load, in that order. Range and contact tolerance build slowly.
- Consistency beats intensity here: 2 small doses a week for months outperforms occasional hard sessions.

Programming Framework

Progression ladder (conservative):

1. Isometric holds — flexion, extension, both lateral sides. Start 3×10–20 s sub-maximal against hand/band.
2. Longer/loaded isometrics — light head-harness held statically, or banded, longer durations.
3. Slow dynamic — controlled harness flexion/extension through a comfortable range, light load, high control.
4. Contact-tolerance integration — bridging / wrestler's-bridge variations progressed gradually, only when prior tiers are comfortable.

Dosing: 2×/week, sub-maximal, placed away from hard sparring (ideally a clean window before the next live session). 3–4 directions, low volume each. Progress one variable at a time.

Exercise Selection and Tradeoffs

- Manual/banded isometrics vs harness: isometrics give the most strength per unit of soreness and require no equipment — the right default and the right re-entry point after any layoff.
- Isometric vs dynamic: isometrics let you train an exact angle with near-zero soreness; dynamic harness work trains through range but carries more soreness risk.
- Bridging progression: supported/partial bridges build contact tolerance at lower cost than full bridges; rushing to full bridging is the classic over-reach.

If/Then Rules

- If hard sparring within 72 h → neck work is isometric maintenance only, sub-maximal, low volume — or skipped.
- If the athlete reports any neck stiffness or sensitivity → reduce to gentle isometrics in a comfortable range only; do not load into discomfort (conservative, non-diagnostic).
- If new to neck training → start with isometrics for 2–3 weeks before any harness load.
- If competition week → maintenance isometrics only; no novel load.
- If bridging is introduced → progress from supported/partial before full; never rush range.
- If the weekly calendar offers no clean 72 h window → default to maintenance isometrics, never harness.

Generator-Use Rules

- Hard-block neck training within 72 h of a hard sparring flag (allow only sub-maximal isometric maintenance).
- Default new athletes to the isometric tier; gate harness/dynamic work behind 2–3 weeks of logged isometrics.
- Set CNS cost for neck work to 0; never schedule it as a "hard" element or count it toward a High day.
- Score any harness/dynamic neck work the day before a spar as Sport-interference 3 and block it.
- Use capacity/tolerance language; never claim it prevents injury.

---
Article 57 — Shoulder Durability for BJJ: Framing, Posting, and Pressing Selection

Coaching Problem

BJJ loads the shoulder in vulnerable, end-range, often-fatigued positions: framing under pressure, posting on scrambles, defending kimuras/americanas, stiff-arming. Many lifters bring a bench-heavy pressing diet that over-loads the front of the shoulder and under-builds the cuff, scapular control, and overhead/end-range capacity the sport actually demands. The goal is balanced shoulder strength biased toward control, scapular stability, and tolerable end-range positions — framed as building capacity, not as injury prevention.

Operating Principles

- Pull volume ≥ push volume for grapplers; the sport and most lifting programs already over-bias the front.
- Build the cuff and scapular stabilizers directly and frequently — low cost, high value.
- Choose pressing variations that load shoulder-friendly angles and let the scapula move freely (landmine, neutral-grip DB, incline) over high-volume flat barbell bench.
- End-range capacity is built patiently and in comfortable ranges first.

Programming Framework

Weekly shoulder structure:

Slot  Tool  Sets  Cost notes
Primary pull  Row / pull-up variants, 8–15 reps  6–10  Low spinal if chest-supported
Primary press  Landmine / neutral DB / incline, RIR 1–2  4–6  Moderate local
Cuff/scapular  External rotation, prone/wall scap, face pulls  6–10 across week, 2–3×/wk  Very low cost
End-range capacity  Controlled overhead carries, light bottoms-up, gradual overhead loading  3–6  Low–moderate, progress slowly

Pull:push volume ratio target ≈ 1.5–2 : 1.

If/Then Rules

- If the athlete reports shoulder sensitivity in a position → reduce injury-constraint-cost on that range to 0; keep pressing/posting work in comfortable ranges, bias cuff/scapular work (non-diagnostic).
- If heavy posting / scramble sparring next day → cap pressing local cost ≤1 today.
- If introducing overhead pressing → progress landmine → neutral DB → barbell over weeks, not in one jump.
- If push volume has crept above pull volume → rebalance before adding any pressing intensity.
- If pull:push falls below 1.3:1 → the generator rebalances by trimming pressing sets and adding pulling before anything else.

Generator-Use Rules

- Enforce a pull:push volume ratio ≥1.3:1 for grappling profiles; auto-add pulling / trim pressing to hit it.
- Default the press slot to landmine / neutral-grip; gate barbell overhead behind a logged progression flag.
- Always include low-cost cuff/scapular work 2–3×/week regardless of phase.
- Cap pressing local cost ≤1 within 24 h of a heavy-posting sparring flag.
- Capacity language only; no injury-prevention or guarantee claims.

---
Article 58 — Hip Strength for Guard Retention, Passing, and Takedowns

Coaching Problem

The hips power nearly every grappling action: guard retention and recovery, passing pressure, shots, sprawls, bridging. Yet hip training is often reduced to back squats, which over-load the spine and under-train the hip's true demands — abduction/adduction strength, hip extension power, end-range control, and unilateral capacity. The goal is comprehensive, multi-vector hip strength delivered at a manageable spinal and soreness cost, so it complements the mat instead of taxing shots and scrambles.

The reductive "hip strength = back squat" model fails twice: it over-spends the spinal channel and it neglects the lateral and rotational vectors that decide guard retention and passing battles.

Operating Principles

- Train the hip in all its sport vectors: extension (thrust/hinge), flexion, abduction/adduction, rotation, and unilateral stability — not just the bilateral squat.
- Bias toward low-spinal, low-eccentric-soreness tools so hip work doesn't degrade shot/sprawl speed.
- Power expression (jumps, throws, sled) is valuable but CNS-costed; ration it.
- Match the vector emphasis to the athlete's game — a guard player and a wrestler weight the menu differently.

Programming Framework

Hip vector menu:

Vector  Lower-cost staples  Higher-cost / rationed
Extension  Hip thrust, back-extension, sled  Heavy RDL / deadlift (spinal)
Squat pattern  Belt squat, split squat, hack  Heavy back squat (spinal)
Abduction/adduction  Cable/band ab-/adduction, Copenhagen progressions (controlled)  Heavy adductor eccentrics (soreness)
Flexion/control  Loaded hip flexion, knee-drive, dead-bug  —
Power  Sled, low-volume jumps, KB swing  Max jumps / throws (CNS)

Vector emphasis by game:

- Guard player: weight abduction, hip flexion control, and unilateral end-range work.
- Wrestler: weight extension power and squat-pattern leg drive, while respecting the adductor-soreness rule before live.

If/Then Rules

- If shooting-heavy sparring within 48 h → adductor/hamstring Eccentric cost ≤1; keep hip power crisp and low-volume.
- If SD Amber/Red → squat/hinge slot to belt squat / hip thrust / split squat; no heavy back squat.
- If introducing Copenhagen/adductor work → start short-lever, controlled; soreness risk is high early.
- If CFS Saturated → cut jump/throw volume; keep extension strength via thrust/sled.
- If guard-retention is the priority quality → emphasize abduction, hip flexion control, and unilateral end-range work.
- If a heavy eccentric hip exercise falls before takedown sparring → move it after the session or substitute.

Generator-Use Rules

- Ensure weekly coverage across extension, squat, abduction/adduction, and unilateral vectors.
- Gate heavy squat/hinge by SD tier; default to belt squat / hip thrust when SD ≥ Amber.
- Cap adductor/hamstring eccentric cost ≤1 within 48 h of a shooting-heavy sparring flag; offer to reschedule heavy eccentrics to after the session.
- Ration jump/throw volume by CFS tier; cut entirely when Saturated.
- Bias the vector menu by a "game_style" input (guard vs wrestler). Performance/capacity language only.

---
Article 59 — Isometric Strength for Grappling: Pins, Frames, Holds, and Scrambles

Coaching Problem

Grappling is full of positions where the athlete must produce force without movement — holding a pin, maintaining a frame under pressure, gripping in a stalemate, surviving a scramble in an awkward joint angle. Dynamic-only training leaves these specific demands under-developed. Isometrics directly build position-specific strength and joint-angle robustness, and crucially they do it at very low spinal and soreness cost, making them ideal fillers in over-budget weeks.

Isometrics are the most under-used precision tool in combat strength work. Because they let a coach apply load to an exact joint angle with almost no eccentric component, they are uniquely suited to a population that is constantly spinal- and soreness-limited.

Operating Principles

- Two isometric flavors with different jobs:
  - Yielding isometrics (resisting a load, holding a position) → build position-holding endurance and tissue tolerance at length; low CNS cost.
  - Overcoming isometrics (pushing/pulling against immovable resistance) → high force expression and joint-angle strength; CNS-costed (covered in Article 60).
- Isometrics are angle-specific — train the angles the sport demands (framing, posting, pinning, gripping). Strength carries roughly ±15° around the trained angle.
- They are the best low-cost tool for maintaining strength when SD/CFS are high.

Programming Framework

Position-matched isometric menu:

Grappling demand  Isometric tool  Type  Cost profile
Pin / top pressure  Loaded position holds, prone press holds  Yielding  Low
Framing / posting  Bottoms-up holds, wall/rack press holds  Yielding  Low–moderate
Grip stalemate  Bar/towel hangs, plate pinches, gi grip holds  Yielding  High grip cost
Scramble joint angles  Split-stance holds, mid-range squat holds  Yielding  Low spinal
Force production  Overcoming pushes/pulls vs pins (see Article 60)  Overcoming  CNS

Dosing: yielding holds 20–45 s, 2–4 sets; grip holds spaced away from gi sparring; overcoming work brief and crisp (Article 60).

If/Then Rules

- If SD/CFS are in Amber/Red → yielding isometrics become primary gym content (low cost, maintains qualities).
- If gi sparring within 24 h → grip isometric cost ≤1.
- If building position-specific strength (e.g., framing) → pick the exact joint angle the position uses.
- If the athlete is fresh and a strength stimulus is wanted in a tight week → short yielding holds give stimulus with minimal cost.
- If overcoming isometrics are programmed → treat as a High/Medium CNS element, not a filler (Article 60).

Generator-Use Rules

- Tag isometrics as yielding (low cost — usable in Red weeks) vs overcoming (CNS-costed).
- Default Low/restorative days to yielding position holds when SD/CFS are high.
- Gate grip isometric cost by proximity to gi sparring (≤1 within 24 h).
- Pull yielding-isometric angles from a "problem_positions" input where available.
- Capacity language only; never claim isometrics prevent injury.

---
Article 60 — Using Overcoming Isometrics for Combat Athletes

Coaching Problem

Overcoming isometrics — maximal or near-maximal pushes/pulls against an immovable resistance (pins in a rack, against a wall, against a loaded bar that won't move) — are one of the most cost-efficient ways to express and build high force in combat athletes. They produce a strong CNS/strength stimulus with near-zero spinal compression at the chosen setup, no eccentric loading, and almost no soreness, which makes them uniquely valuable when the spinal and peripheral channels are over-budget but the athlete can still tolerate CNS output.

The single most useful thing to understand about overcoming isometrics is where their cost lands. They concentrate stress almost entirely in the CNS channel while sparing the spinal, eccentric, and soreness channels — the exact opposite of the deadlift. That makes them the precision instrument for the combat athlete's most common bind: a body deep in Spinal Debt that still has CNS headroom and wants to keep expressing high force.

Operating Principles

- Overcoming isometrics are a high-intent, CNS-costed tool — program them like heavy lifting, not like filler.
- They are angle-specific: train 1–3 joint angles per pattern per session; strength carries roughly ±15° around the trained angle.
- They allow maximal force with minimal joint travel and minimal soreness — ideal when you want a strength stimulus without adding spinal or eccentric cost.
- Set up to minimize spinal cost: pin heights and stances that keep the spine neutral and unloaded are the whole point.

Stress-Cost Implications

By the Module 0.5 dimensions, a well-set overcoming isometric typically scores: Spinal ≈0–1, Eccentric 0, Soreness 0–1, but CNS 2–3. That profile is the entire reason to use them — they concentrate cost in the one channel (CNS) that may still have room when Spinal is in debt.

The flagship use case: overcoming isometrics convert spare CNS capacity into a strength stimulus when the spine cannot pay for traditional lifting.

Programming Framework

Prescription guidelines:

- Effort: ramp to approximately 90–100% perceived effort over the set; early sets sub-maximal to groove the position.
- Duration: 3–6 s per maximal push (true overcoming intent), 3–5 hard efforts per angle.
- Angles: 1–2 angles per pattern per session, chosen for sport relevance.
- Frequency: 1–2×/week as a CNS-costed element; not daily.

Sport-matched overcoming menu:

Pattern  Setup  Sport relevance
Press  Pins in rack at framing / lockout angle  Framing, posting, stiff-arm
Pull  Bar/handle pinned, pull into immovable  Grip fights, breaking posture, pulling guard
Hinge/extension  Pins low, push into bar (low-spinal setup)  Bridging, hip-extension power
Squat / leg drive  Pins mid-range, drive up  Scramble leg drive, stand-ups

If/Then Rules

- If SD is Amber/Red but CFS ≤14 → overcoming isometrics are an ideal strength stimulus (low spinal, real strength). Promote them as the High-day tool.
- If CFS Saturated/Overdrawn → treat them as a max-effort element and pull them, like any heavy work.
- If hard sparring within 24 h → no maximal overcoming work (CNS interference).
- If building a specific position's strength → set the pin at that exact joint angle.
- If the athlete reports sensitivity at a joint angle → do not load maximally into that angle; choose a comfortable angle (conservative, non-diagnostic).
- If a weight cut is active → reduce to sub-maximal; maximal CNS efforts are poorly tolerated depleted.

Generator-Use Rules

- Classify overcoming isometrics as a CNS-costed (CFS-gated) element; never schedule them the day before hard sparring.
- Promote them automatically as the preferred strength stimulus when SD is Amber/Red but CFS ≤14.
- Enforce brief maximal-intent prescriptions (3–6 s, 3–5 efforts), not long holds; reject long-duration prescriptions as mislabeled yielding work.
- Pull target angles from a "problempositions" or "gamestyle" input where available.
- Use capacity / force-production language; no injury-prevention, no guarantees, no medical claims.

---
Cluster F: Cross-Cluster Generator Rules Summary

1. Score the week before the gym. Always estimate mat Spinal Debt and CNS load first; the gym fills the remainder.
2. Governing dimension rules placement. The highest single stress-cost dimension determines day-type and timing, not the total.
3. Substitute before you cancel. Over-budget channels change the exercise (use the 0.6 library), rarely the session.
4. Apply the multipliers. Density 1.25 when a spinal lift is <24 h from a mat session of spinal load ≥5; Fatigue_State 1.3 on a stiff-back day. They compound — re-check the tier after applying both.
5. Channel gates: SD tier gates spinal/hinge work; CFS tier gates max-intent/overcoming work; grip cost gated by gi-sparring proximity (≤1 within 24 h); eccentric cost gated by shooting-heavy-sparring proximity (≤1 within 48 h); neck work blocked within 72 h of hard sparring; pressing local cost ≤1 within 24 h of heavy-posting sparring; pull:push ≥1.3:1 for grapplers.
6. Overcoming isometrics are the spine-debt escape hatch. When SD is Red but CFS is fresh, they are the preferred way to keep a maximal-force stimulus alive.
7. Deload triggers: two channels simultaneously Red, OR sparring quality down 2+ sessions, OR a constraint flag escalates.
8. Constraints are not diagnoses. A flagged sensitive region only reduces injury-constraint-cost loading on that region and biases substitution. Never name conditions, never advise treatment.
9. Language discipline (hard rule): no citations, no external URLs, no medical claims, never "prevents injury," never guarantees outcomes. Use capacity, tolerance, load-management, and freshness language only.

---
Cluster G — Volume Thresholds and the Adaptation-Scoring Layer

Knowledge vault module for the Raz Pilosof strength program generator. Audience: advanced coaches and athletes — calisthenics/skill athletes, strength and powerlifting-adjacent lifters, hypertrophy-focused trainees, and combat-sport athletes who lift. Scope: Articles 61–68.

Relationship to Cluster F. Cluster F answered "what is this session costing across recovery channels, given what the sport already took?" Cluster G answers the adjacent question: "how much of each adaptation is worth doing in the first place, and where is the ceiling?" The two layers compose: volume thresholds set the target dose; the stress-cost layer decides whether the week can afford it and how to place it. The generator runs volume thresholds first (what to aim for), then filters through the stress-cost layer (what the week allows). Stress-cost scoring stays internal — it does not appear in client-facing programs unless the user asks.

---
Module 0 — The Volume-Threshold and Adaptation-Scoring Layer (Shared Engine)

The core idea: adaptation is dose-dependent and bounded on both ends. Too little volume and the stimulus is below threshold — nothing changes. Too much and the dose exceeds what the athlete can recover from and consolidate — quality erodes, fatigue masks fitness, and progress reverses. Between those bounds is a moving window that depends on the adaptation targeted, the athlete's training age, what else is being trained concurrently, and what the sport already spends. The job is to find the smallest dose that drives the desired adaptation, then add only as much as the athlete can recover from and still improve.

0.1 The Volume Landmarks

We use five landmarks. They apply, with different units, to every adaptation in the cluster (skill, strength, hypertrophy, endurance/density, GPP, tissue tolerance). The unit changes; the logic does not.

Landmark  Name  Definition
MEV  Minimum Effective Volume  Smallest weekly dose that produces measurable progress in the target adaptation
MAD  Minimum Adaptive Dose  The per-exposure threshold — smallest single-session dose that counts as a real stimulus
MAV  Maximum Adaptive Volume  The dose that produces the most adaptation per unit of fatigue — the efficient sweet spot
MRV  Maximum Recoverable Volume  The most the athlete can recover from across a microcycle and still adapt
AR  Adaptive Reserve  The unused gap between current working volume and MRV

MEV ≤ MAV ≤ MRV always. The window between MEV and MRV narrows the more advanced the athlete, the more concurrent the training, and the more the sport already loads the same channel. For a combat athlete in camp, MEV and MRV can sit almost on top of each other — meaning maintenance is the ceiling that week.

0.2 Why Thresholds Are Individual and Autoregulated

These landmarks are estimates that move week to week. They shift with sleep, nutrition, life stress, training age, exercise selection, proximity to failure, and concurrent sport load. The generator must:

- Treat any printed number as a starting anchor, not a prescription.
- Adjust off performance and recovery feedback (bar speed, rep quality, soreness, skill freshness, sport sharpness), not off the calendar alone.
- Never present a threshold as a guarantee — present it as an expected range under stated conditions.

0.3 The Adaptation Threshold Table (master reference)

Typical starting ranges for an advanced athlete, per adaptation, per muscle/quality/region, per week, assuming this quality is a genuine priority and not merely maintained. Ranges are anchors to autoregulate from, not universals.

Adaptation  Unit  MEV  MAV (sweet spot)  MRV (ceiling)  Warning signs of exceeding capacity
Skill acquisition (planche, HSPU, OAP)  quality exposures/week  2 exposures  3–5 exposures  5–6 exposures  form decays within a session; failed attempts rise
Maximal strength  hard sets/week per main lift  4–6  8–12  12–16  bar speed drops across weeks; top-set grind with no PR
Hypertrophy  hard sets/week per muscle/region  8–10  12–18  18–22 (region-dependent)  pump/connection fades; soreness lingers >72 h
Endurance / density (pull-up family)  total submax reps/week relative to max  ~150–200% of max-rep set  ~250–400%  ~450–550%  rep quality/ROM degrades; elbow/forearm nags
GPP / conditioning  hard work-intervals or minutes/week  1–2 sessions  2–3 sessions  3–4 sessions  flat sessions; resting HR creep; appetite/sleep disruption
Tissue tolerance / prehab  loaded exposures/week to a region  2 exposures  3–4 exposures  4–5 exposures  target region stays irritated rather than settling between exposures

Combat-athlete correction: When the sport already loads a quality (grip, posterior chain, conditioning), the sport counts toward the threshold. The gym fills the remainder between sport-supplied volume and MAV — and never pushes the combined total past MRV.

0.4 Effective Volume Units (EVU)

Not all sets are equal stimulus. The EVU normalizes a raw set into an effective-stimulus value.

EVU(set) = Proximity × Loadzone × ROMquality × (1 − Junk_penalty)

  Proximity   = 0.5 (RIR ≥4),  0.8 (RIR 3),  1.0 (RIR 1–2),  1.1 (RIR 0, true failure)
  Load_zone   = 1.0 in the adaptation's effective zone; 0.6 if clearly off-zone
  ROM_quality = 1.0 full quality ROM; 0.7 partial/cut ROM (unless partials are the intent)
  Junk_penalty= 0.2 if the set is fatigue-only (no progression, no quality)


Weekly Effective Volume = Σ EVU across qualifying sets. Compare this — not raw set count — to the MEV/MAV/MRV anchors.

0.5 Volume Cost Score (VCS)

EVU measures stimulus; VCS measures the recovery price of that stimulus.

VCS(block) = Σ [ EVUi × Failuremulti × Eccentricmulti × Noveltymulti ] × Channelweight

  Failure_mult   = 1.0 (RIR ≥2), 1.3 (RIR 1), 1.6 (RIR 0)
  Eccentric_mult = 1.0 normal, 1.4 slow/loaded eccentric, 1.6 supramaximal eccentric
  Novelty_mult   = 1.0 familiar, 1.3 new exercise/first exposure in a block
  Channel_weight = 1.0 local hypertrophy; 1.3 spinal/axial; 1.4 CNS-heavy; 1.2 connective-heavy


VCS is the explicit handshake with Cluster F: it converts planned volume into the same "cost" language the stress-cost layer uses.

0.6 Skill Quality Score (SQS)

Skill volume must be counted in quality reps/holds, not attempts. SQS decides when a skill exposure should stop today and whether weekly skill volume is real or junk.

SQS(exposure) = Qualityreps / (Qualityreps + Failedordegraded_reps)

  A "quality" rep/hold = full position, full intended duration, clean form, no grinding.
  A "failed/degraded" rep = missed, collapsed, broke position early, or visibly fought.

Stop rule: end the skill exposure when SQS drops below ~0.7, OR at the first
           true form failure on a high-skill static (planche, OAP).


Only quality reps count toward weekly skill MEV/MAV. Failed attempts count toward CNS/connective cost (VCS) but contribute zero stimulus and actively teach the wrong motor pattern.

0.7 Local Tissue Overlap Score (LTOS)

The most common volume-audit failure is counting exercises, not the regions they load. LTOS sums the real weekly load on a region across all sources (multiple exercises + sport).

LTOS(region) = Σ [ EVUexercise × Regionshareexercise ] + Sportregion_load

  Region_share = fraction of an exercise's effective volume that lands on the region
  Sportregionload = region's weekly load from the sport

Flag when LTOS(region) approaches that region's hypertrophy/tolerance MRV from 0.3.


LTOS is why a program with weighted pull-ups, barbell rows, curls, and gi sparring can overload elbow-flexors and grip even though no single line item looks high.

0.8 Adaptive Reserve (AR) Estimate

AR is the practical decision variable: it estimates how much headroom remains before MRV on the channel that is closest to its ceiling.

AR(channel) = MRV(channel) − [ current gym volume on channel + sport-supplied volume on channel ]

Express as a tier:
  AR ≥ 30% of MRV   → Green:  room to add stimulus / progress this quality
  AR 10–30%         → Amber:  hold volume; progress via intensity or quality, not added sets
  AR 0–10%          → Red:    at ceiling; maintain only; any add risks overshoot
  AR < 0            → Black:  already over MRV; cut volume now


Governing-channel rule: Plan to the channel with the lowest AR, not the average.

0.9 How the Layer Drives Programming

1. Pick the adaptation and its unit. Strength = hard sets; skill = quality exposures/reps; density = submax reps vs max; etc.
2. Read the landmarks (0.3) as starting anchors, then adjust for training age and concurrency.
3. Count stimulus in EVU (0.4), not raw sets, and count skill in SQS quality reps (0.6).
4. Add sport-supplied volume to the ledger and compute LTOS per shared region (0.7).
5. Compute AR per channel (0.8); plan to the lowest-AR channel.
6. Price the plan in VCS (0.5) and check it against the week's stress-cost budget (Cluster F). If over budget, cut volume or substitute.
7. Autoregulate: if quality (bar speed, SQS, ROM, sport sharpness) degrades, you have found MRV early — back off regardless of the printed number.
8. Deload triggers: AR < 0 on any priority channel for the week, OR quality down 2+ sessions, OR two channels simultaneously Red, OR a constraint flag escalates.

Universal volume-day language used across the cluster:

- Progression dose: working volume at/above MEV with AR Green — the dose that drives the adaptation.
- Maintenance dose: roughly MEV (often one-third to one-half of MAV) — holds the quality with minimal cost.
- Overreach dose: brief planned excursion toward/above MRV for 1–2 weeks, only when followed by a deload and only on a non-sport-priority channel.

---
Article 61 — Volume Landmarks for Advanced Athletes: MEV, MAV, MRV, and Adaptive Reserve

Coaching Problem

Advanced athletes have already harvested the easy adaptations. The lever that worked in their first three years — "add volume, get bigger and stronger" — now produces fatigue faster than fitness. The recurring mistake is treating volume as a dial that only turns up. The advanced reality is that the productive window between too little and too much has narrowed to a band that must be found, defended, and re-found every block.

The deeper trap is conflating the two failure modes. Stalling because you are below MEV looks almost identical, day to day, to stalling because you are above MRV: flat performance, no PRs, frustration. The fix is opposite in each case — add in the first, cut in the second — so guessing wrong doubles the damage. Landmarks exist precisely to tell these two states apart by reading quality and recovery, not just outcomes.

Operating Principles

- The five landmarks (Module 0.1) are the planning language for every adaptation. MEV is the floor, MAV the efficient target, MRV the ceiling, MAD the per-session minimum, AR the room left.
- Count EVU, not sets (0.4). Advanced programs are full of nominal volume that doesn't earn its keep — RIR-4 "working" sets, cut-ROM reps, junk warm-ups logged as sets.
- The window narrows with training age and concurrency. A novice's MEV–MRV band is wide and forgiving; an advanced concurrent athlete's is tight and unforgiving.
- Plan to the lowest-AR channel (0.8), not the average. A program is only as progressable as its most-constrained region or system.
- Maintenance is a legitimate, valuable dose. Most qualities hold for 2–3 weeks at roughly MEV, which is what lets a program prioritize one thing without losing the others.

Threshold Table by Adaptation

Adaptation  Unit  MEV  MAV  MRV  Warning signs
Skill (high-static)  quality exposures/wk  2  3–5  5–6  form decays in-session, failed attempts climb
Max strength  hard sets/wk per lift  4–6  8–12  12–16  bar speed falls week-over-week, no PR
Hypertrophy  hard sets/wk per muscle  8–10  12–18  18–22  connection fades, soreness >72 h
Endurance/density  submax reps/wk vs max  150–200%  250–400%  450–550%  ROM degrades, joints nag
GPP/conditioning  hard sessions/wk  1–2  2–3  3–4  flat sessions, HR/sleep creep
Tissue tolerance  loaded exposures/wk  2  3–4  4–5  region stays irritated between exposures

If/Then Rules

- If a quality has stalled 2–3 weeks and EVU sits below MAV with quality intact → you are below productive volume; add EVU.
- If a quality has stalled and quality (bar speed, ROM, SQS) is degrading → you are at/over MRV; cut, don't add.
- If AR is Amber on the priority channel → progress via load/quality, not added sets.
- If AR is Red/Black on any priority channel → maintenance dose only; plan a deload.
- If the athlete is concurrent (sport + lifting) → subtract sport-supplied volume from the channel's MRV before computing gym room.
- If logged volume is high but progress is flat → recompute in EVU before changing anything; nominal volume lies.

Generator-Use Rules

- Store landmarks per adaptation, per region/lift/quality, in the unit from 0.3 — never one global volume number.
- Compute working volume in EVU, not raw sets, before comparing to any landmark.
- Subtract sport-supplied volume from MRV for concurrent athletes; compute LTOS for shared regions.
- Plan to the lowest-AR channel; gate "add volume" suggestions behind AR Green.
- Surface landmarks as ranges-under-conditions, never as guarantees.
- Use capacity/recovery/quality language only; no injury-prevention, no medical claims, no guarantees.

---
Article 62 — Skill Acquisition Volume: How Much Practice Builds Planche, HSPU, OAP, and Gymnastics Strength

Coaching Problem

High-skill statics — planche, freestanding handstand push-up, one-arm pull-up, front lever progressions — fail under the volume logic that works for hypertrophy. The athlete who "trains planche to failure five days a week" is not accumulating skill; they are accumulating failed reps, which teach the nervous system the wrong pattern and load connective tissue that recovers slowly. The mistake is counting attempts as volume. The correct unit is quality reps/holds (Module 0.6), and the ceiling is set by coordination freshness and connective recovery — both low.

Operating Principles

- Quality reps are the only reps that count (0.6). Stop the exposure when SQS drops below approximately 0.7 or at the first true form failure on a high static.
- Frequency beats density for motor learning. 3–4 short, fresh exposures/week build a skill faster than 2 long, fatiguing ones.
- Failed attempts are pure cost, zero stimulus — they add to VCS (CNS/connective) and teach error. Minimize them.
- The connective tissue is the slow channel. Wrists, elbows, biceps tendon (OAP), and anterior shoulder (planche) set the real ceiling, not the muscles.
- Deload skill on a separate clock from strength — coordination and connective tissue accumulate fatigue you won't feel as soreness.

Skill Volume Thresholds

Skill  Frequency (exposures/wk)  Quality reps or holds per exposure  Failed-attempt cap  Deload rule
Planche (straddle→full)  3–5  4–6 quality holds  ≤1–2 per exposure  every 4–6 wk drop to MEV (2 exposures)
HSPU (freestanding/deficit)  2–4  5–10 quality reps total  ≤2  deload when balance/ROM decays
One-arm pull-up (OAP)  2–3  3–6 quality reps/side  ≤1 grind/side  every 3–4 wk; elbow is the governor
Front/back lever  3–4  4–8 quality holds  ≤2  every 4–6 wk to MEV
Press-to-handstand  3–5  5–8 quality reps  ≤2  balance-decay triggered

MEV is the floor (≈2 exposures), MAV the productive target (3–5), MRV the ceiling (5–6). Beyond MRV, quality reps fall and failed reps rise — the SQS itself signals the overshoot.

Formulas in Action — SQS Gating a Planche Session

An athlete plans 8 planche holds. Holds 1–5 are clean (quality), hold 6 breaks early, hold 7 is fought and shaky, hold 8 collapses.

- Quality reps = 5; degraded/failed = 3 → SQS = 5 / (5+3) = 0.625.
- SQS dropped below 0.7 after rep 5 → the stop rule says the productive exposure ended at rep 5.
- Reps 6–8 added CNS/connective cost (VCS) with zero skill stimulus and rehearsed failure.

If/Then Rules

- If SQS < 0.7 in a session → end the skill exposure; log only quality reps.
- If failed attempts exceed the cap two sessions running → reduce difficulty or frequency; the dose is past MRV.
- If elbow/wrist/anterior-shoulder reports lingering irritation between exposures → reduce loaded exposures to that region (LTOS), choose a lower-leverage progression (conservative, non-diagnostic).
- If the athlete also trains heavy pulling/pressing → count that region's load toward the skill's connective LTOS before adding skill frequency.
- If a hard sport/CNS week is scheduled → drop skill to MEV (2 exposures); skill quality needs CNS freshness.

Generator-Use Rules

- Count skill volume in quality reps/holds via SQS, never attempts; enforce the SQS < 0.7 stop rule.
- Hold skill frequency to the anchors (MEV 2, MAV 3–5, MRV 5–6 exposures) and cap failed attempts.
- Maintain a separate connective-tissue tolerance ledger (LTOS) for wrist/elbow/biceps/anterior shoulder; gate added skill frequency behind it.
- Deduct skill exposures to MEV automatically on flagged hard-CNS or hard-sport weeks.
- Use capacity/quality/coordination language; no injury-prevention claims, no guarantees, no diagnoses.

---
Article 63 — Strength Volume Thresholds: Heavy Sets, Top Sets, Back-Offs, and Neural Readiness

Coaching Problem

Maximal strength is the adaptation most easily over-dosed because it feels productive while it fails. Heavy work is satisfying, and the advanced athlete who loves it will keep adding top sets, singles, and "just one more triple" until bar speed quietly collapses and the lift stalls. The mistake is treating strength like hypertrophy — more hard sets, more better. Strength's productive volume band is narrow (approximately 4–12 hard sets/week per lift) and its ceiling is set by CNS recovery and neural readiness, not by how much the muscle can tolerate. Past MRV, the athlete is training fatigue management, not strength.

Operating Principles

- Strength MRV is CNS-gated. Neural readiness — bar speed at a fixed load, readiness to express force — is the limiter and the early-warning signal.
- Top sets are the stimulus; back-offs are the practice; speed work is the cheap deposit. Dose each separately.
- Effective set in the strength zone = approximately 80%+ and RIR 0–2 (0.4). A 70% "strength" set is off-zone.
- Max-effort and repeated-effort are different volume currencies. ME (heavy singles/doubles) buys neural drive at high VCS; RE (back-offs at RIR 1–2) buys technique and some hypertrophy at lower CNS cost.
- For concurrent/sport athletes, strength volume is the first thing the sport's CNS draw eats into.

Strength Volume Thresholds

Element  Unit  MEV  MAV  MRV  Warning signs
Top sets  hard sets/wk  1–2  2–4  4–5  grind with no PR, bar speed down
Back-off sets  hard sets/wk  2–3  4–8  8–10  form drifts late in sets
Heavy singles  reps/wk  1–2  3–6  6–8  misses, slow lockout
Heavy triples  sets/wk  1–2  2–4  4–5  rep 3 grinds, speed falls
Speed/dynamic  sets/wk  3–4  6–10  10–12  bar speed not actually fast

High-Concurrent Sport Modification

When the sport supplies heavy CNS load (hard sparring, live wrestling — Cluster F), the strength CNS budget shrinks. Drop ME first; keep a small top-set "primer" (1–2 crisp reps, RIR 1) to maintain neural drive, and move accumulation to RE at submax. Strength is retained 2–3 weeks at maintenance — there is no urgency to push it during a hard sport week.

If/Then Rules

- If bar speed at a fixed load falls 2+ sessions → over MRV on CNS; cut heavy exposures, not back-offs first.
- If top sets grind with no PR but speed work is crisp → too much ME volume, not too little; reduce ME, keep speed.
- If AR Green on CNS and quality intact → add a back-off set or raise top-set load, not both.
- If the athlete trains a sport with high CNS draw → 1 heavy exposure/lift/week max; bias to RE and speed.
- If a weight cut is active → reduce ME; near-maximal work is poorly tolerated depleted.
- If two main lifts both want a heavy day → they share the CNS MRV; alternate which lift gets the true ME exposure.

Generator-Use Rules

- Separate strength volume into top sets / back-offs / singles / triples / speed, each with its own anchor; never collapse to one set count.
- Cap ME exposures at 1/lift/week for advanced athletes; auto-reduce on flagged hard-CNS or sport weeks.
- Gate strength CNS volume on a shared CNS budget across lifts + sport (Cluster F handshake via VCS).
- Trigger a cut when neural-readiness (bar-speed/RPE-at-load) degrades 2+ sessions, regardless of the printed set count.
- Use force-production/readiness language; no medical claims, no injury-prevention, no guarantees.

---
Article 64 — Hypertrophy Volume Thresholds: Effective Sets, Local Recovery, and Interference Control

Coaching Problem

Hypertrophy is the adaptation with the widest volume band — it rewards and tolerates more sets than strength or skill — which is exactly why it gets mismanaged. Coaches either under-stimulate by counting RIR-4 cut-ROM sets as if they were hard sets, or over-stimulate a region by stacking exercises that all hit the same muscle and blowing past its local MRV while each lift looks reasonable on paper.

For combat athletes and other concurrent trainees there is a second layer: the sport already loads several muscles hard (forearms/grip, posterior chain, neck), so naive added hypertrophy volume to those regions stacks on top of sport volume and overshoots local MRV invisibly. The Local Tissue Overlap Score (0.7) exists precisely to make this stacking visible before it becomes a stall or a lingering soreness problem.

Operating Principles

- Count effective sets per muscle, in EVU, not nominal sets (0.4). RIR 4 and cut ROM are heavily discounted.
- MRV is local and region-specific (approximately 18–22 effective sets/wk, region-dependent). Fast-recovering muscles (delts, lats, calves, forearms-as-trained) tolerate more frequency; slow-recovering, eccentric-loaded, or large muscles (hamstrings, lower back, large multi-joint movers) recover slower and cap lower.
- Eccentric load is the soreness/recovery multiplier (VCS 1.4–1.6). A few slow-eccentric sets can cost more local recovery than many concentric-biased sets.
- Sport-supplied volume counts toward the muscle's MRV. Add it via LTOS before prescribing gym volume to that region.
- Interference is bidirectional: soreness from hypertrophy work degrades skill/sport quality, and sport soreness shrinks the recoverable hypertrophy dose.

Hypertrophy Volume Thresholds by Region Group

Region group  Recovery speed  MEV  MAV  MRV  Notes for combat/concurrent athletes
Lats / upper back  fast  8–10  14–18  20–22  safe volume sink; minimal sport overlap
Delts (all heads)  fast  6–8  12–16  18–20  tolerates frequency; watch posting overlap in grapplers
Chest  moderate  8–10  12–16  16–18  pressing overlaps posting volume
Quads  moderate  8–10  12–16  16–18  heavy eccentrics sore shots/sprawls 48 h
Hamstrings  slow  6–8  10–14  14–16  eccentric work lingers; respect before live
Lower back/erectors  slow  4–6  8–10  10–12  most over-drafted by sport; often zero gym room in camp
Elbow flexors (biceps)  moderate  6–8  12–16  16–18  high gi-grip overlap → LTOS critical
Forearms/grip  slow (cumulative)  4–6  8–12  12–14  sport supplies most; gym often only fills the gap

Adjusting Hypertrophy Volume for Combat Athletes

- Subtract sport load first (LTOS). Forearms/grip, posterior chain, and neck often need little or no added gym hypertrophy in-season — the sport fills MEV already.
- Bias gym volume to low-overlap regions (lats, delts, chest) where AR is largest.
- Cap eccentric cost near hard sport sessions — eccentric soreness in quads/hams degrades shots and scrambles for 48 h.
- Use maintenance dosing in camp. Most muscle is retained 2–3 weeks at approximately MEV, so hypertrophy yields to sport without loss.

Generator-Use Rules

- Count hypertrophy volume in effective sets per muscle (EVU); never per exercise or nominal set.
- Compute LTOS per region including sport load; gate added volume behind the region's AR.
- Apply region-specific MRV (fast vs slow recoverers) and an eccentric VCS multiplier near sport days.
- Default to maintenance dosing for sport-overlapping regions in-season; bias progression to low-overlap regions.
- Use capacity/recovery/soreness-management language; no medical claims, no "prevents injury," no guarantees.

---
Article 65 — Pull-Up Endurance and Density Volume: Ladders, EMOMs, Clusters, and High-Rep Progression

Coaching Problem

Endurance and density work — high-rep pull-ups, ladders, EMOMs, cluster sets — is where volume math goes wrong most often, because the numbers get big fast and the connective tissue pays the bill slowly. The mistake is dosing density off enthusiasm ("100 pull-ups today") instead of off the athlete's max-rep capacity, which is the correct denominator. The right unit is total submax reps/week expressed as a percentage of a max-rep set (Module 0.3), and the limiter is local/connective recovery (elbow, forearm, biceps tendon) plus rep quality, not the lungs.

Operating Principles

- Dose density off max-rep capacity, not off a round number. Weekly submax volume is a multiple of the athlete's clean max set (0.3).
- Stay submax per set. Most density reps live at 40–70% of max-rep capacity, stopping ≥2–3 reps shy of failure.
- Connective tissue is the ceiling. Elbows, forearm flexors, and biceps tendon set MRV here, and they recover slowly and cumulatively.
- Quality reps only (EVU ROM_quality). Stop a density set when ROM or tempo visibly degrades.
- Density is a low-CNS, moderate-local cost (VCS). It's a good volume sink when CNS/spinal channels are taxed — but it competes for the same elbow/grip tissue as pulling strength and gi sport.

Density Methods and Volume Profiles

Method  Per-set load vs max  Typical weekly dose  Best use
Straight sets  35–50% of max set  200–400% of max  base endurance, pain-free high-rep capacity
Ladders  ramps to ~60%  250–450% of max  volume accumulation with built-in submax cap
EMOMs  30–50% of max, lots of sets  250–400% of max  density/work-capacity, easy autoregulation
Cluster sets  50–70% of max  200–350% of max  quality reps at higher per-rep load without failure

All weekly doses are starting anchors relative to a clean max-rep set. MEV ≈ 150–200% of max; MAV ≈ 250–400%; MRV ≈ 450–550%.

Scaling Rule

Express every density prescription as a percentage of the athlete's current clean max set, not as an absolute. A fixed "4×12" is light for a 30-rep athlete and brutal for a 12-rep athlete. Re-anchor whenever the max set changes by more than approximately 15%.

A 28–30 rep athlete using 4×10–12 at roughly 33–40% of max set, pain-free, is a moderate density dose — not a hard one. This is appropriate and unremarkable for this athlete in a MAV band.

If/Then Rules

- If the athlete's max set is high and elbows/forearms are pain-free → moderate fixed schemes like 4×10–12 are an appropriate submax density dose; progress reps or sets within the MAV band.
- If ROM or tempo degrades within a set → stop the set; only full-ROM reps count (EVU).
- If elbow/forearm/biceps-tendon irritation lingers between sessions → reduce density volume or shift to a lower-overlap pattern (conservative, non-diagnostic).
- If the athlete also does heavy weighted pulling or gi sport → run LTOS on elbow flexors/grip before adding density.
- If density times/rep counts slow week over week → at/over MRV on the connective channel; deload the density block.
- If CNS/spinal channels are taxed by sport → density is a good low-CNS volume sink, provided the local channel has reserve.

Generator-Use Rules

- Prescribe density as a percentage of the athlete's current max-rep set, mapped to MEV/MAV/MRV multiples; re-anchor when the max set shifts >15%.
- Count quality full-ROM reps only (EVU ROM_quality); auto-stop sets on ROM degradation.
- Run LTOS on elbow flexors/grip across density + pulling strength + gi sport before adding density.
- Treat density as a low-CNS volume sink usable when CNS/spinal are taxed, if the local connective channel has reserve.
- Use capacity/tolerance/quality language; no injury-prevention, no medical claims, no guarantees.

---
Article 66 — Combat-Sport Volume Management: Strength Training Dose Around Sparring, Wrestling, and BJJ

Coaching Problem

For a combat athlete, the gym is not the main volume source — the mat is. The recurring failure is programming gym volume as if the athlete arrived fresh, when in reality hard sparring, live wrestling, and rolling have already spent most of the week's recoverable volume on the same channels the gym wants to load. The volume question for a fighter is never "how much should we lift?" — it is "what volume budget did the mat leave, and how do we fill the remainder without crossing MRV?"

The second problem is that mat volume is spiky and unpredictable: a hard sparring week, a competition camp, a deload week, and a technical week have radically different sport-supplied volumes. A fixed gym template ignores this and either overshoots MRV (hard weeks) or wastes reserve (light weeks). Gym volume must flex inversely to sport volume.

Weekly Sport-Context Volume Map

Sport week type  Sport-supplied volume  Gym strength dose  Gym hypertrophy dose  Governing channel to protect
Hard sparring week (2+ live)  very high CNS + spinal  maintenance (~MEV; 1 primer top set)  maintenance, low-overlap regions only  CNS + spinal
Competition camp (peak)  maximal, sport-specific  maintenance only; no ME  maintenance, low-eccentric  freshness/sharpness
Technical / skill week  low-moderate  progression allowed (MAV)  progression on low-overlap regions  none acute → use the reserve
Deload / recovery week  low  low (restorative) or 1 quality top set  low  systemic recovery

Hard / Medium / Low Day Placement Within a Week

- Low sport day → gym High day (the one real progression session, placed for maximum buffer before the next live session).
- Medium sport day → gym Medium day (RIR 1–3, no max intent).
- Hard sport day → gym Low/none (restorative, blood flow, low-overlap maintenance only; protect the mat).

If/Then Rules

- If 2+ hard live sessions this week → gym strength at maintenance (1 primer), hypertrophy on low-overlap regions only.
- If competition camp / peak week → no ME gym work; maintenance dose; protect sharpness.
- If technical/light sport week → use the reserve: run a gym progression dose (MAV).
- If combined sport+gym AR < 10% (Red) on any channel → cut gym volume; substitute, don't push.
- If gi grip/elbow LTOS is high → no added grip/elbow gym volume; the gi supplied it.
- If sparring sharpness drops 2 sessions → treat as MRV reached regardless of the printed gym numbers; deload.

Generator-Use Rules

- Require a sport-week-type input (hard spar / camp / technical / deload) and scale the gym volume fraction from it.
- Add sport-supplied volume to every shared-channel ledger before prescribing; compute combined AR and LTOS.
- Default in-season strength to maintenance + 1 primer, hypertrophy to low-overlap regions, unless the week is technical/light.
- Place the gym High day on the lowest sport day; never adjacent to hard sparring (Cluster F handshake).
- Use load-management/capacity language; no injury-prevention, no medical claims, no guarantees.

---
Article 67 — Volume Allocation in Concurrent Training: Balancing Strength, Skill, Hypertrophy, GPP, and Conditioning

Coaching Problem

The hardest volume problem is not setting one quality's threshold — it is dividing a finite, shared recovery budget across five qualities that all want volume at once: strength, skill, hypertrophy, GPP/conditioning, and sport. The recurring failure is additive planning — building each quality up to its own MAV in isolation, summing them, and discovering the total is far past the athlete's global MRV. Concurrent training requires subtractive planning: start from the global budget, fund the priority quality to MAV, then fund the rest only to MEV (maintenance) with whatever reserve remains.

Operating Principles

- One quality gets a progression dose at a time; the rest get maintenance. This is the core concurrent rule. You can hold 4 qualities at MEV while pushing 1 to MAV; you cannot push all 5.
- Allocate by channel, not just by total (governing-channel rule, 0.8). Plan to the lowest-AR channel; don't let two CNS qualities both run high.
- Maintenance is cheap and protective. Approximately MEV holds a quality for 2–3 weeks, which is what makes prioritization possible without loss.
- Interference is channel-specific. Heavy CNS conditioning blunts strength and skill; high local hypertrophy soreness blunts skill quality and sport. Separate colliding qualities in time.
- Re-rank priorities by block, not by week. Rotate which quality gets the progression dose across a training phase.

Concurrent Allocation Matrix

Priority this block  Strength  Skill  Hypertrophy  GPP/Cond.  Shared-channel watch
Strength focus  MAV  MEV (CNS-fresh, pre-strength)  MEV  MEV (low-CNS only)  CNS: skill + strength collide → skill before strength
Skill focus  MEV (1 primer)  MAV  MEV  MEV  CNS + connective: keep strength low so skill stays fresh
Hypertrophy focus  MEV (heavy primer)  MEV  MAV  MEV  local/connective: density + hypertrophy collide → LTOS
Conditioning/GPP focus  MEV  MEV  MEV  MAV  systemic: protect skill freshness; keep strength as a primer
In-season (sport priority)  MEV+primer  MEV  MEV low-overlap  sport supplies  every channel — plan to lowest AR

Interference Control

- CNS collisions (strength × skill × max conditioning): sequence skill first (fresh), strength second; keep conditioning low-CNS in a strength block.
- Local collisions (hypertrophy × density): apply LTOS (0.7); alternate which shares a region; don't run both to MAV on the same tissue.
- Systemic collisions (hard conditioning × everything): place hard conditioning on its own day or after the day's priority quality, never before skill/strength.

If/Then Rules

- If more than one quality is at a progression dose → reduce all but the priority to maintenance; the budget can't fund two.
