Arm at side, elbow 90°, press the back of the wrist gently against a doorframe or the opposite hand. Hold 3 × 30–45 seconds. No rotation — pure isometric.
This is the first loading stimulus. Evidence from patellar tendinopathy research (and increasingly shoulder tendinopathy) shows isometric loading provides immediate cortical analgesia — pain relief without compromising strength. ([Rio et al., *BJSM*, 2015](https://bjsm.bmj.com/content/49/19/1277))

**Scapular retraction-depression:**
Seated or standing; gently draw shoulder blades together and down. Hold 10 seconds × 10 reps, 2–3x/day. Re-establishes the stable base without loading the GH joint.

**Thoracic spine mobility (critical):**
Foam roller thoracic extension — roller across mid-back, hands behind head, let the upper back extend over the roller. 1–2 minutes.
*Why:* A stiff thoracic spine (Starrett's Area 2 in *Supple Leopard*) directly limits the scapula's ability to rotate upward, which compresses the subacromial space. Gray Cook: "How can you make the scapula stable if the T-spine is stiff?" Fix this first.

---

## Phase 2 — Loading & Motor Control (Weeks 3–8)

### Goal: Rebuild rotator cuff and scapular endurance under load

The 2025 JOSPT CPG makes a strong recommendation for progressive exercise. Motor control exercise programs reduce disability in both the short and medium term. ([Desmeules et al., *JOSPT*, 2025](https://www.jospt.org/doi/10.2519/jospt.2025.13182))

### Rotator Cuff Strengthening

| Exercise | Sets × Reps | Cue |
|---|---|---|
| **Side-lying ER** (dumbbell or band, towel roll under arm) | 3 × 12–15 | 2s up, 2s hold, 3s down; do not shrug |
| **Standing IR** (band, arm at side) | 3 × 12–15 | Control the band on the return; do not let it snap |
| **Scaption / Full Can** (dumbbell or band, thumb up) | 3 × 12–15 | Raise in the scapular plane (30° forward); stop at shoulder height |
| **Face Pull** (band or cable) | 3 × 15 | Pull to ear level; elbows high; squeeze mid-traps at end |

### Scapular Stabilization

| Exercise | Sets × Time/Reps | Cue |
|---|---|---|
| **Prone Y** (light dumbbells or bodyweight) | 2–3 × 10 | Thumbs up; feel mid and lower trap; slow |
| **Prone T** | 2–3 × 10 | Arms out to side at 90°; pronate hands |
| **Prone W** | 2–3 × 10 | Elbows back, forearms vertical; drive elbows to hips |
| **Push-up Plus** | 3 × 10 | At top of push-up: protract scapulae fully ("push the floor away"); hold 2s |
| **Band Row** | 3 × 12–15 | Drive elbows back; hold 1–2s; do not shrug |

### For Instability: Closed-Chain Work

The shoulder relies on co-contraction of the rotator cuff and deltoid for stability. This is best trained in closed-chain positions where the hand is fixed on a surface. ([Jaggi & Alexander, *Open Orthopaedics Journal*, 2017 — PMC5611703](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5611703/))

- **Plank** (weight-bearing through hands): Push the floor away, feel the serratus activate
- **Push-up on unstable surface** (foam pad or Bosu): 3 × 8–10
- **Wall ball rolling** (single hand on ball against wall): 3 × 30–45 seconds per side
- **"Drop and catch"** drills: Light medicine ball or weight — drop and catch with one hand to train rapid co-contraction

Focus on stability in the *apprehensive* range with *low loads* first — not high loads in the comfortable range.

---

## Phase 3 — Progressive Strengthening (Weeks 6–14)

### Goal: Build robust shoulder strength and endurance across all archetypes

**Overhead Archetype loading:**
- Seated dumbbell press → standing dumbbell press → barbell press
- 3 × 8–12; add 2.5–5% load per week when pain-free
- Monitor for scapular shrugging ("shoulder creep") — if present, reduce load

**90/90 External Rotation (band or cable):**
Shoulder abducted to 90°, elbow at 90°; rotate from internal to external rotation.
3 × 12. Mirrors the position used in armbar defense, guillotine escapes, and clinch wrestling.

**Pressing (Press Archetype):**
- Push-up progression: standard → weighted → ring push-up
- Bench press: start at 50% 1RM; progress to full load over 4–6 weeks

**Pulling (Hang Archetype):**
- Lat pulldown: 3 × 10–12 with full scapular depression at the bottom
- Progress to bodyweight pull-ups when lat pulldown is comfortable at bodyweight

**Turkish Get-Up (whole-system):**
Light kettlebell; combines overhead stability, scapular control, and whole-body proprioception. One of the best single exercises for grappling-specific shoulder resilience. 3 × 3 per side.

The JOSPT 2025 guidelines confirm that higher dose (greater volume) exercise produces significantly better functional outcomes in the medium term — progressive loading matters. ([Desmeules et al., 2025](https://www.jospt.org/doi/10.2519/jospt.2025.13182))

---

## Phase 4 — Return to Sport (Weeks 10–16+)

### Criteria before full return
- Symmetric ROM (within 5°)
- External rotation strength ≥ 90% of uninvolved side
- No apprehension or pain with resisted ER at 90° abduction
- Can perform push-up with full scapular protraction, pain-free

**Progressive return to grappling:**
1. Pummeling, collar/wrist control, no explosive pulling
2. Single-leg/double-leg entries and hip throws at 50% effort
3. Guard passing and sweep defense → 2–3 rounds → full rolling
4. Reintroduce submission defense (armbar, kimura, omoplata) progressively

**Progressive return to lifting:**
- Begin at 60–70% 1RM on all pressing/pulling
- Progress by 5% weekly
- Overhead press returns last — requires full mobility and stability across all archetypes

---

## Ongoing Prehab for Grapplers

- **Daily overhead archetype check:** Stand with back to wall, raise both arms overhead. They should touch the wall without your lower back arching. If they don't → lat and pec restriction limiting your shoulder. Address this (see Mobility: Shoulders article).
- **T-spine rotation 3x/week** (10 reps per side, quadruped or seated)
- **Face pulls in every training session** — the single best shoulder prehab exercise for grappling athletes
- **Starrett's 2-minute rule:** Before every press or overhead session, spend 2 minutes mobilizing the anterior shoulder and pec (bands, lacrosse ball). After: 2 minutes posterior shoulder (sleeper stretch or banded distraction).

---

## Citations

1. Desmeules F, et al. (2025). "Rotator Cuff Tendinopathy CPG." *JOSPT.* [doi:10.2519/jospt.2025.13182](https://www.jospt.org/doi/10.2519/jospt.2025.13182)
2. Jaggi A, Alexander S. (2017). "Rehabilitation for Shoulder Instability." *Open Orthopaedics Journal.* [PMC5611703](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5611703/)
3. Exercise for rotator cuff tendinopathy. (2023). *Revista Brasileira.* [PMC9904825](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9904825/)
4. Rio E, et al. (2015). "Isometric exercise induces analgesia." *BJSM.* [49:1277](https://bjsm.bmj.com/content/49/19/1277)
5. Cook G. (2010). *Movement: Functional Movement Systems.* Joint-by-joint concept; shoulder mobility screening.
6. Starrett K. (2015). *Becoming a Supple Leopard.* Shoulder archetypes (Overhead, Press, Hang, Front Rack); Area 2–4 mobility prescriptions.
7. Neumann DA. *Kinesiology of the Musculoskeletal System.* Scapulohumeral rhythm, GH joint stability, rotator cuff function.


---


<a id="rehab_03_knee.md"></a>

# Knee Pain in BJJ and Lifting: Patellar Tendinopathy & MCL/LCL Sprains

**Cluster:** Physio / Rehab / Mobility — Article 3 of 9
**Audience:** Intermediate-to-advanced BJJ/MMA athletes and strength athletes
**Disclaimer:** General education only. Not a diagnosis. Consult a physiotherapist or sports medicine physician.

---

## The Short Version

The knee is a stability-dominant joint. According to Gray Cook's joint-by-joint model (*Movement*, 2010), the knee sits between two mobility joints — the hip above and the ankle below. When either of those mobility joints gets stiff, the knee absorbs the compensation. Patellar tendinopathy and collateral ligament sprains are the two most common presentations in grapplers and lifters — and both respond very well to the right loading approach.

---

## Part A: Patellar Tendinopathy (Jumper's Knee)

### What It Is

The patellar tendon connects the kneecap (patella) to the tibia. In patellar tendinopathy, the tendon develops reactive or degenerative changes at its attachment point (inferior pole of the patella). This is *not* a tear — it's a capacity problem: the tendon's load tolerance has been exceeded by repeated high-force quadriceps contractions.

The tendon responds to this overload with local cellular changes, disrupted collagen organisation, and increased sensitivity — resulting in pain specifically at the inferior patella during and after loading activities.

From a kinesiology standpoint, the patellar tendon operates at peak tension during activities that combine high knee flexion angle with high quadriceps force — exactly what happens during takedown shots, landing from jumps, stance work in guard, and heavy squatting.

### Mechanisms in BJJ / Lifting

- **Takedown shooting:** Double and single-leg shots require explosive knee flexion → extension under full body-load — repeated hundreds of times across a training week
- **Guard retention and stance-switching:** Sustained squat-like postures with the knee under load
- **Heavy squatting, leg press:** High compressive patellar tendon load at depth
- **Landing and jumping drills** in MMA conditioning

**The driver is always load accumulation without adequate recovery.** A grappler who trains 6 days per week, competes on weekends, and does heavy squatting 3 days per week has a very high tendon load — without enough time for tissue adaptation. ([Rosen et al., *Journal of Athletic Training*, 2022 — PMC9528703](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9528703/))

**Gray Cook's note on the chain:** Restricted ankle dorsiflexion increases the demand on the quadriceps during squat-pattern movements (the shin cannot travel forward so the knee tracks poorly). This compounds patellar tendon stress. If your tendinopathy keeps returning, check your ankle mobility.

### Red Flags

- Complete inability to straight-leg raise (possible patellar tendon rupture — significant force required)
- Severe swelling within the knee joint after exercise
- Pain at night or at rest (unprovoked patellar pain can indicate other pathology)
- Tendinopathy pain that has been chronic and progressive for > 4–6 months without improvement

### Rehab — Patellar Tendinopathy

#### Phase 1: Symptom Management & Isometric Loading (Weeks 0–4)

Replace provocative activity (squatting, jumping, running) with cycling or pool work — these maintain fitness without patellar tendon compression.

**Isometric Protocol (daily):**

The landmark study by Rio et al. (*BJSM*, 2015) showed that heavy isometric contractions at ~70% MVC reduced patellar tendon pain from 7.0/10 to 0.17/10 — immediately and sustained for 45 minutes. The mechanism is cortical analgesia: isometrics reduce the cortical inhibition of the quadriceps and temporarily block pain at the tendon. ([Rio et al., BJSM 49:1277](https://bjsm.bmj.com/content/49/19/1277))

| Exercise | Sets × Duration | Load / Cue |
|---|---|---|
| **Leg extension isometric** (machine, knee at 60° flexion) | 5 × 45 seconds | ~70% of max effort; steady hold — no bouncing |
| **Spanish Squat** (bands under feet, leaning back, knees forward) | 5 × 45 seconds | Bodyweight start; arms behind head or held in front |
| **Wall Sit** | 5 × 30–60 seconds | Thighs parallel; progress hold time weekly |

Rest 2 minutes between sets. Pain during: ≤ 5/10. Pain next morning: ≤ 3/10. If worse the following morning, reduce load.

#### Phase 2: Isotonic Recovery Loading (Weeks 2–6)

Introduce isotonic (moving) quadriceps loading. Tempo matters — slow eccentrics create more time-under-tension and are more effective for tendon remodeling.

| Exercise | Sets × Reps | Tempo |
|---|---|---|
| **Double-leg decline squat** (25° wedge) | 3 × 10 → 3 × 15 | 3s down, 1s pause, 2s up |
| **Leg extension** (concentric + eccentric) | 3 × 10–12 | 2s up, 3–4s down; increase weight when 15 reps comfortable |
| **Leg press** | 3 × 12–15 | Controlled; focus on mid-range quad loading |

#### Phase 3: Heavy Progressive Loading (Weeks 4–12)

Heavy slow resistance (HSR) training demonstrated superior tendon morphological changes and functional outcomes versus eccentric-only programs. ([Kongsgaard et al., *AJSM*, 2009 — PMID 19793213](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19793213/))

The JUMPER randomized trial (Breda et al., *BJSM*, 2021) found progressive tendon-loading exercise therapy superior to eccentric-only training at 24 weeks (28-point vs. 18-point VISA-P improvement). PTLE is now recommended as first-line conservative treatment. ([BJSM 55:501](https://bjsm.bmj.com/content/55/9/501))

| Exercise | Sets × Reps | Progression |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-leg decline squat** (25° board) | 4 × 15 → 4 × 6 | Increase load weekly; use pain-monitoring model |
| **Barbell Back Squat** | 4 × 15 → 4 × 6 | 3s eccentric; full depth as tolerated |
| **Leg Press** | 4 × 15 → 4 × 6 | Heavier load each week |
| **Hack Squat** | 4 × 15 → 4 × 6 | Alternative to barbell squat |

**Pain monitoring rule (from Rosen et al. protocol):** Pain ≤ 5/10 during exercise; pain the following morning ≤ 3/10 and no worse than the day before exercise. If morning pain is worsening week-on-week, reduce load or frequency.

#### Phase 4: Return to Sport (Weeks 12+)

Criteria: VAS pain < 1/10; symmetric single-leg squat; full squat depth pain-free.

- **Plyometric progression:** Double-leg box jump (soft landing) → single-leg hop → lateral hop → explosive drill
- **Takedown entries:** 50% speed → full speed over 2 weeks
- **Rolling return:** Positional sparring → 2–3 rounds → full rolling

*Ongoing maintenance:* Single-leg decline squats 2–3x/week indefinitely. The tendon needs ongoing loading to maintain its capacity.

---

## Part B: MCL and LCL Sprains

### What They Are

**MCL (Medial Collateral Ligament):** Runs along the inner (medial) side of the knee. Resists valgus force (knee caving inward). The most common knee ligament sprain in BJJ.

**LCL (Lateral Collateral Ligament):** Runs along the outer (lateral) side of the knee. Resists varus force (knee bowing outward). Less common, but significant in leg lock grappling.

**Grading:**
- Grade I: Fibres stretched but intact — mild pain, no instability
- Grade II: Partial tear — moderate pain, slight instability, 2–4 week timeline
- Grade III: Complete tear — significant instability, possible need for specialist review

### Mechanisms in BJJ / Lifting

- **MCL — guard work:** An opponent standing in your closed guard and leaning forward creates valgus stress on both knees. De la Riva and reverse DLR put the MCL at extreme length.
- **MCL — lifting:** Valgus collapse under a squat (knees caving in) applies repeated MCL stress — especially pronounced with heavy loads and poor hip abductor control.
- **LCL — heel hooks:** Heel hook submissions torque the tibia relative to the femur, stressing the LCL and posterior lateral corner. Novice leg-lock grapplers are most at risk.
- **LCL — outside trips:** Leg trips that apply varus force to the weight-bearing leg.

### Red Flags

- Immediate large swelling (whole knee fills with fluid within 2 hours) — possible ACL or meniscus involvement
- Audible pop at the time of injury — seek imaging
- Complete inability to bear weight
- Gross instability on valgus/varus stress test with no firm endpoint (Grade III)
- Numbness below the knee — possible peroneal nerve injury with severe LCL sprain

### Rehab — MCL/LCL Sprain

A 2024 systematic review confirmed: Grade I and II MCL injuries tolerate immediate weight-bearing and early exercise therapy well. Grade I average lay-off: ~10 days. Grade II average: ~35 days. Return-to-sport: 3–12 weeks. ([Svantesson et al., *BMJ Open Sport & Exercise Medicine*, 2024 — PMC11202733](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11202733/))

#### Phase 1: Protect & Activate (Days 1–7)

Goal: Control swelling; reactivate the quadriceps.

- **PEACE & LOVE:** Protect (avoid valgus/varus stress), Elevate, Compression. After 3–5 days: Load, Optimism, Vascularization, Exercise.
- **Quad Sets:** Lying on back, leg straight. Squeeze quads hard, hold 5–10 seconds. 3 × 10.
- **Straight Leg Raise:** Quad set first, then lift leg to 45°. 3 × 10.
- **Heel Slides:** Supine. Slide heel toward glute until comfortable. 2 × 10, 3–4x/day.
- **Non-load CV:** Pool walking, upper body ergometer.

#### Phase 2: Recovery & Progressive Loading (Weeks 1–4 for Grade I; Weeks 2–6 for Grade II)

| Exercise | Sets × Reps | Cue |
|---|---|---|
| **Terminal Knee Extension** (light band behind knee) | 3 × 15 | Full extension contraction; feel VMO |
| **Bilateral Wall Squat** | 3 × 10–15 | Knees track over toes; no valgus |
| **Standing Hip Abduction** | 3 × 15 | Balance on injured leg; abduct other |
| **Single-Leg Balance** | 3 × 30–60s | Eyes open → eyes closed → foam pad |
| **Step-Up with slow step-down** | 3 × 10 per leg | 3s eccentric step-down; control valgus |

#### Phase 3: Strengthening & Re-Exposure (Weeks 3–8)

| Exercise | Sets × Reps | Note |
|---|---|---|
| **Lateral Lunge** | 3 × 10 | Re-exposes MCL to controlled valgus load |
| **Bulgarian Split Squat** | 3 × 10–12 | Add load progressively |
| **Lateral Band Walk** | 3 × 15 steps | Mini-band above knees; slight squat |
| **Lateral Step-Down** | 3 × 10–15 | 3–4s eccentric; key proprioception drill |
| **Lateral Lunge + Single-Leg Balance** | 3 × 10 | Add medball reach for perturbation |

#### Phase 4: Return to Sport (Weeks 6–12)

Criteria: Full active ROM pain-free; quad/glute strength ≥ 90% of other side; no pain with stress testing.

- **Running:** Straight line → figure-8 → lateral cutting → reactive change of direction
- **Grappling:** Pummeling/takedown entries (standing) → guard passing → guard rolling → full rolling
- **Guard return (MCL):** Closed guard first; open guard variants that hyperextend the MCL (DLR, RDLR) last

---

## Joint-by-Joint Context: Why Hip and Ankle Matter for Your Knee

This is where the Gray Cook joint-by-joint model pays dividends in the clinic:

- **Restricted ankle dorsiflexion** causes the tibia to stop travelling forward during a squat, forcing the knee into valgus or excessive internal tibial rotation — directly loading the MCL. Fix the ankle and the knee tracking often self-corrects.
- **Weak or stiff hips** allow the femur to adduct and internally rotate during single-leg stance — driving valgus at the knee. Strengthen the glutes and hip external rotators as part of every knee rehabilitation program.

*Gray Cook:* "There is a direct connection between ankle stiffness and knee pain. Lose ankle mobility, get knee pain."

---

## Citations

1. Rosen AB, et al. (2022). "Clinical Management of Patellar Tendinopathy." *Journal of Athletic Training.* [PMC9528703](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9528703/)
2. Rio E, et al. (2015). "Isometric exercise induces analgesia in patellar tendinopathy." *BJSM.* [49:1277](https://bjsm.bmj.com/content/49/19/1277)
3. Breda SJ, et al. (2021). "JUMPER trial: Progressive tendon-loading." *BJSM.* [55:501](https://bjsm.bmj.com/content/55/9/501)
4. Kongsgaard M, et al. (2009). "HSR vs eccentric for patellar tendinopathy." *AJSM.* [PMID 19793213](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19793213/)
5. Svantesson J, et al. (2024). "MCL rehabilitation systematic review." *BMJ Open Sport & Exercise Medicine.* [PMC11202733](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11202733/)
6. Cook G. (2010). *Movement.* Joint-by-joint: ankle → knee → hip chain.
7. Starrett K. (2015). *Becoming a Supple Leopard.* Squat and Pistol archetypes; ankle dorsiflexion mobility.


---


<a id="rehab_04_neck.md"></a>

# Neck Pain in Grapplers: Cervical Strain, Stiffness & Strength Protocols

> **Disclaimer:** This article is general education only. It is not a diagnosis or medical advice. If you have neck pain — especially with neurological symptoms — consult a physiotherapist or sports medicine doctor before training.

---

## The Short Version

Neck pain is one of the most common overuse and acute injuries in BJJ and wrestling. The cervical spine takes enormous compressive, shear, and rotational loads during takedowns, guard passing, bridging, and scrambles. Most cases are soft-tissue or facet-related and respond well to structured rehab — but red flags must be ruled out first. The fix is almost never rest alone. It's targeted deep flexor work, thoracic mobility, and progressive loading.

---

## What Is It? (The Kinesiology)

The cervical spine has seven vertebrae (C1–C7) stacked to allow three-dimensional movement: flexion/extension, lateral flexion, and rotation. It sits at the top of the kinetic chain and, critically, is downstream from the thoracic spine.

Donald Neumann's *Kinesiology of the Musculoskeletal System* (3rd ed.) emphasises that the cervical spine relies on two largely independent muscular systems:

- **Global movers** (sternocleidomastoid, upper trapezius, scalenes): generate gross head and neck motion but are poor stabilisers under load.
- **Deep cervical flexors** (longus colli, longus capitis): the true segmental stabilisers of the cervical spine — equivalent to the multifidus and TA at the lumbar level. They provide the continuous low-level co-activation that protects intervertebral joints during high-speed loading.

Gray Cook, in *Movement: Functional Movement Systems* (2010), places the thoracic spine as the primary **mobility** joint immediately below the cervical spine. When thoracic mobility is lost — which is nearly universal in fighters and desk workers — the cervical spine compensates by increasing its range of motion. This creates excessive segmental stress, particularly at C4–C7 (the most commonly injured levels), and is a primary driver of chronic neck pain in athletes.

**Cook's cascade:** Lose thoracic mobility → neck is forced to move more → accelerated degenerative change and soft-tissue overload.

---

## How It Happens in BJJ, Wrestling & Lifting

### BJJ / Grappling Mechanisms
- **Neck cranks and guillotines:** Direct axial compression + forced rotation at end-range. High-risk for facet joint injury and disc involvement.
- **Bridging and granby rolls:** Repeated cervical extension under load (own bodyweight + partner's weight).
- **Sprawling and shots:** Rapid cervical flexion/extension cycles (whiplash pattern) — strain to posterior soft tissue.
- **Cross-face pressure:** Sustained lateral force on the cervical spine over multiple rounds.
- **Head-heavy guard work:** 20–30 minutes of sustained cervical flexion with isometric load (head against torso) fatigues deep flexors rapidly.

### Lifting Mechanisms
- **Heavy high-bar back squat:** Compressive load on cervical spine if bar is too high or athlete protects through cervical extension.
- **Overhead press:** Requires thoracic extension and cervical neutral — loss of either drives cervical hyperextension under load.
- **Deadlift neck position:** Chronic "head up" cue execution leads to sustained cervical extension under high axial load.

### Overuse Cumulative Pattern
Fighters training twice daily, 5+ days per week, often accumulate a chronic fatigue load on cervical soft tissue. The deep flexors habituate to a lengthened/inhibited position under persistent anterior head posture. The result: a neck that is simultaneously stiff **and** weak — moving through available range with no segmental control.

---

## Red Flags — Stop Training, See a Doctor

Stop immediately and seek medical assessment if any of the following are present:

| Red Flag | Why It Matters |
|---|---|
| Pain down one or both arms (radicular pattern) | Possible nerve root compression (C5–C7 disc herniation) |
| Pins and needles / numbness in hands or fingers | Neurological involvement — must be imaged |
| Weakness in arm, hand grip, or shoulder | Upper motor neuron or nerve root involvement |
| Pain after a direct blow to the head or neck | Possible fracture, ligamentous instability — do not load |
| Severe stiffness with fever or systemic symptoms | Meningitis, infection — emergency |
| Bilateral symptoms (both arms, both legs) | Central cord or spinal cord involvement — emergency |
| Dizziness, drop attacks, or visual disturbance with neck movement | Vertebrobasilar insufficiency |
| Pain that is constant, progressive, and unrelated to movement | Potential non-mechanical cause (tumour, infection) |

If neurological symptoms are present, **do not proceed with this protocol**. The JOSPT Clinical Practice Guideline for Neck Pain (2017) specifically flags radiculopathy as requiring medical triage before rehabilitation is initiated.

---

## Phased Rehab Protocol

### Phase 1 — Acute / Settle the Irritability (Days 1–7)

**Goal:** Reduce pain and muscle guarding; restore basic range of motion.

**What to do:**
- Avoid positions and movements that reproduce symptoms — don't force through pain.
- Gentle active range of motion: chin tucks (10 reps), gentle rotation left/right within pain-free range (10 each), lateral flexion ear-to-shoulder (10 each).
- Apply heat (not ice) to posterior neck muscles — heat reduces protective guarding more effectively than ice in non-acute trauma.
- Sleep with a supportive pillow that keeps neutral cervical alignment (not too high, not too flat).
- **Thoracic mobility work:** Begin immediately (foam roller thoracic extensions, 10 reps × 2 sets). Per Gray Cook's cascade principle, freeing T-spine immediately reduces compensation load on the cervical spine.

**What to avoid:**
- Heavy loading of any kind (squats, deadlifts, overhead press).
- Rolling. Full stop.
- Prolonged static positions (phone in flexion, etc.).

**Training modification:** Lower-body work and horizontal pulling are generally tolerated. Use a cervical collar/neck brace only if prescribed by a clinician for structural instability — passive support delays deep flexor reactivation.

---

### Phase 2 — Motor Control / Deep Flexor Reactivation (Weeks 1–3)

**Goal:** Reactivate deep cervical flexors (DCF); decouple global mover dominance.

The deep neck flexor (DNF) activation protocol — validated by Jull et al. and widely used in physiotherapy practice — is the cornerstone of cervical rehab.

**Deep Cervical Flexor (DCF) Protocol:**
1. **Craniocervical flexion test (CCFT) / Chin Tuck:**
   - Lie supine on floor or bench, small rolled towel under cervical curve.
   - Gently nod the head — "yes" motion — as if pressing the back of the skull into the floor. This is NOT a full neck flexion; it is a subtle upper cervical movement.
   - Hold 10 seconds × 10 reps. Breathe normally throughout.
   - **Key coaching cue:** No SCM (neck cord) should become prominent. If you see the neck muscles bulge, you're using global movers, not deep flexors.
   - Progress: from 10-second holds → 30-second holds → functional positions (seated, standing).

2. **Cervical retraction:**
   - Seated or standing. Draw the head directly backward ("make a double chin") without tilting. Hold 5 seconds × 15 reps.
   - Progressions: retraction + rotation, retraction + lateral flexion.

3. **Prone cobra / cervical extension against gravity:**
   - Prone position, hands at sides. Lift the head 2–3 cm off the ground with chin tucked. Hold 5–10 seconds × 8–10 reps.
   - Targets deep posterior extensors (multifidus cervicis).

**Frequency:** 2–3 × daily during Phase 2. These are low-load, high-frequency interventions.

**Research support:** A 2021 systematic review (PMID 33521131) confirmed that deep cervical flexor training significantly outperforms general exercise for reducing neck pain intensity and disability. The protocol above is adapted from Physiopedia's Deep Neck Flexor Stabilisation Protocol.

---

### Phase 3 — Progressive Strengthening (Weeks 3–8)

**Goal:** Build cervical and upper thoracic strength under progressively higher loads; restore sport-specific ranges.

**Exercises:**

1. **Neck isometrics (4 directions):**
   - Seated. Apply manual resistance with hand or resistance band. Press into resistance (flex, extend, lateral flex each side) — hold 5 seconds × 10 reps each direction.
   - No pain. Mild to moderate effort only.
   - Progress to 10-second holds, then to dynamic resisted ROM.

2. **Banded or plate neck flexion / extension:**
   - Prone on bench (for extension): small plate on back of head, controlled 3-second lowering, 2-second hold, 3-second raising. 3 × 10.
   - Supine on bench (for flexion): same setup, chin-tuck position throughout.
   - **Cue from Starrett (*Becoming a Supple Leopard*, 2015):** Maintain a "packed neck" (retracted chin, long cervical spine) during all loaded work. Never load the neck into forward head posture.

3. **Cervical rotation with resistance band:**
   - Anchor band at head height. Rotate against band resistance 3 × 12 each side. Slow tempo.

4. **Scapular stability work:** Lower trapezius, serratus anterior. A destabilised scapula forces the upper trapezius to compensate, which chronically overloads the cervical region via the levator scapulae. Include: prone Y-T-W, band pull-aparts, wall slides.

5. **Thoracic extension over foam roller:** 60 seconds × 2–3 sets per session. Non-negotiable for cervical health per Gray Cook's T-spine mobility principle.

6. **Return to overhead pressing:** Only when cervical neutral is achievable throughout the full press range. Use a landmine press as regression if overhead is still uncomfortable.

---

### Phase 4 — Return to Rolling / Sport (Weeks 6–12+)

**Goal:** Tolerate full grappling loads with no symptom reproduction or neurological signs.

**Criteria to begin return-to-sport:**
- Full pain-free cervical range of motion (flex, extend, rotate, lateral flex).
- Able to hold neck isometric resistance × 10 seconds in all directions without pain.
- No neurological symptoms at any point.
- Thoracic mobility restored to at least neutral.

**Return-to-rolling progression:**
1. **Pummelling and grip-fighting only** (no takedowns, no guard, no top pressure). 2–3 × 5-minute rounds.
2. **Positional drilling** (guard passing, hip escapes) without live resistance.
3. **Light controlled sparring** with a trusted partner who knows your status. Avoid neck cranks, guillotines, and cross-face initially.
4. **Full live rolling** — only when you've had 2 consecutive symptom-free training sessions at the previous stage.

**Ongoing neck maintenance (Starrett-inspired):** 10 minutes of daily neck and upper back soft-tissue + mobility work. Priority areas per Starrett's area map: Area 1 (jaw/neck soft tissue) and Area 2 (upper back/T-spine). Use a lacrosse ball on the suboccipitals and upper traps post-training.

**Lifting return:** Resume heavy squats, deadlifts, and overhead press only when you can demonstrate neutral cervical position under load. Film from the side. A "neck craning" or chin-poke pattern under load is a sign the deep flexors are not yet holding position.

---

## Why Loading Works

The evidence for active rehabilitation over passive rest for cervical spine injuries is now robust. The JOSPT Clinical Practice Guideline for Neck Pain (2017) recommends:
- Cervical range of motion exercise
- Deep neck flexor strengthening
- Cervicothoracic and thoracic manipulation/mobilisation
- Supervised exercise over advice alone

The mechanism: deep cervical flexors (longus colli in particular) provide the continuous segmental stabilisation that prevents microtrauma to facet joints and discs during dynamic loading. When these muscles are inhibited by pain or chronic posture, global movers (SCM, upper trap, scalenes) take over — they generate force but provide no fine segmental control, so the joint continues to accumulate damage despite apparent movement.

Progressive loading reconstitutes DCF activation, restores mechanoreceptor input to the joint, and drives collagen remodelling in strained soft tissues. Passive rest does none of this.

---

## Summary Protocol Snapshot

| Phase | Duration | Focus | Key Exercises |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 — Settle | Days 1–7 | Pain reduction, range of motion | Chin tuck, gentle ROM, T-spine rolling |
| 2 — Motor Control | Weeks 1–3 | DCF reactivation | CCFT/chin tuck holds, prone cobra, cervical retraction |
| 3 — Strengthen | Weeks 3–8 | Load tolerance, strength | Neck isometrics (4 directions), banded rotation, scapular work |
| 4 — Return to Sport | Weeks 6–12+ | Sport-specific loading | Pummelling → drilling → controlled sparring → full rolling |

---

## Citations

1. JOSPT Clinical Practice Guideline: Neck Pain — https://www.jospt.org/doi/10.2519/jospt.2017.0302
2. Deep Cervical Flexor Training Meta-Analysis (PMID 33521131) — https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33521131/
3. Deep Neck Flexor Stabilisation Protocol (Physiopedia) — https://www.physio-pedia.com/Deep_Neck_Flexor_Stabilisation_Protocol
4. Cervical Sprain (StatPearls, NBK541016) — https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK541016/
5. Neck Pain in MMA and BJJ (Elite HP) — https://www.elitehp.com.au/blog/neck-pain-from-mma-and-bjj-causes-treatment-and-prevention
6. Gray Cook — *Movement: Functional Movement Systems* (2010)
7. Donald Neumann — *Kinesiology of the Musculoskeletal System* (3rd ed.)
8. Kelly Starrett — *Becoming a Supple Leopard* (2nd ed., 2015)


---


<a id="rehab_05_elbow_forearm.md"></a>

# Elbow & Forearm Tendinopathy: Medial / Lateral Epicondylalgia and Grip Overload

> **Disclaimer:** This article is general education only. It is not a diagnosis or medical advice. Consult a physiotherapist or sports medicine doctor before beginning any rehabilitation programme, especially if symptoms are severe or persistent.

---

## The Short Version

Medial and lateral elbow tendinopathy are the most common elbow problems in grapplers and strength athletes. They're driven by repetitive high-force loading of the wrist flexors (medial = "golfer's elbow") or extensors (lateral = "tennis elbow"). Both respond well to the same underlying principle: progressive tendon loading using the Cook/Purdam tendinopathy continuum model — start with isometrics, build to heavy slow resistance, then sport-specific loading. The Tyler Twist and eccentric/isometric protocols are the two most evidence-supported non-surgical interventions.

---

## What Is It? (The Kinesiology)

### Lateral Epicondylalgia (LE) — "Tennis Elbow"

The lateral epicondyle of the humerus is the common attachment point for the wrist and finger extensor group — primarily **extensor carpi radialis brevis (ECRB)**. Under repeated tensile load, the tendon undergoes pathological change: the normal collagen architecture (crimped parallel fibres) is replaced by disorganised fibres, neovascularisation, and a failed healing response. This is **not** primarily inflammatory — it is a failed tendon repair process. Hence the term *tendinopathy* rather than tendinitis.

Donald Neumann (*Kinesiology of the Musculoskeletal System*, 3rd ed.) describes ECRB's function as wrist extension stabilisation during grip activities — it acts as a constant isometric stabiliser when you grip hard, even if the wrist itself doesn't move. This is why gripping intensely (gi chokes, bar holds, pulling) loads the lateral tendon continuously even without wrist movement.

### Medial Epicondylalgia (ME) — "Golfer's Elbow"

The medial epicondyle is the common attachment for wrist flexors and the forearm pronator group — primarily **flexor carpi radialis (FCR)** and **pronator teres**. In BJJ, this is heavily loaded during grip-intensive pulling, collar chokes, wrist control, and any movement requiring forearm pronation under resistance.

The ulnar nerve also runs in the cubital tunnel immediately posterior to the medial epicondyle. When ME is present, ulnar nerve irritation may coexist — tingling in the ring and little finger is a sign.

### Cook/Purdam Tendinopathy Continuum

Jill Cook and Craig Purdam's tendinopathy continuum model (*BJSM*, 2009) is the foundational framework for understanding and treating tendon pain. Three stages:

1. **Reactive tendinopathy:** Acute spike in load — tendon cells respond with rapid (but disorganised) increase in matrix proteins. Short-term, pain-protective. Load reduction + isometrics are indicated.
2. **Tendon disrepair:** Failed healing attempt — collagen disorganisation, neovascularisation, pain. Targeted loading at moderate intensity drives remodelling.
3. **Degenerative tendinopathy:** Large areas of pathological tissue, often in older athletes or chronically overloaded tendons. Still responds to loading, but prognosis and timeline extend.

**Clinical implication:** Don't avoid loading the tendon. Load it correctly. Tendons do not heal with rest — they require mechanical stimulation to reorganise collagen.

---

## How It Happens in BJJ, MMA & Lifting

### BJJ / Grappling Mechanisms

- **Gi gripping:** Constant isometric load on wrist extensors (LE) and flexors (ME) during every roll. A 90-minute gi session may involve 400–600 individual grip cycles.
- **Collar and lapel chokes (RNC, bow-and-arrow, cross-collar):** Forearm supination and pronation under load — primary ME loading pattern.
- **Sleeve control and wrist fighting:** Isometric ECRB loading (LE).
- **Spider guard:** Sustained wrist extension with finger hook — direct lateral tendon load.
- **Sprawling and post-fall impact:** Sudden eccentric demand on forearm extensors during weight-bearing through the hand.

### Lifting Mechanisms

- **Barbell rows and deadlifts:** Sustained grip load. As grip fatigue sets in, the forearm extensors compensate to stabilise the wrist — increasing ECRB load.
- **Pull-ups and chin-ups:** Cumulative wrist flexor loading (ME).
- **Olympic lifting / cleans:** High-speed wrist extension under bar load — LE mechanism.
- **Wrist curl accessory work:** Direct flexor overload (ME) — especially when performed with too much volume too quickly.

### Common Onset Pattern in Athletes

Grapplers rarely develop one isolated episode. The typical pattern is:
1. High-volume training block (camp, tournament prep) creates reactive tendinopathy.
2. Athlete trains through it — tendon enters disrepair stage.
3. Pain becomes constant, especially in the first grip of the day.
4. Athlete tries rest — temporarily better — then returns and reloads too fast.
5. Chronic cycle of flares and partial recovery.

The fix is loading the tendon correctly through the disrepair stage, not avoiding load.

---

## Red Flags — See a Doctor

| Red Flag | Why It Matters |
|---|---|
| Tingling / numbness in ring + little finger | Ulnar nerve irritation or cubital tunnel syndrome — needs clinical assessment |
| Weakness in grip or wrist extension that is new | Possible nerve entrapment or tendon rupture |
| Swelling, heat, or redness at the elbow joint | Rule out bursitis, gout, or septic arthritis |
| Locking or clicking with a specific arc of elbow motion | Possible loose body, osteochondral lesion |
| Pain at rest / night pain that is constant and severe | Refer for imaging |
| Pain that is diffuse (forearm, not epicondyle point) | Consider differential: compartment syndrome, nerve entrapment |

---

## Phased Rehab Protocol

### Phase 1 — Reactive / Settle the Load (Days 1–14)

**Goal:** Reduce tendon irritability; identify and modify load.

**Load modification:**
- Reduce grip volume first — not eliminate. Complete rest allows tendon sensitisation to worsen.
- Wear a counterforce brace (epicondyle strap) during training if continuing — does not fix the tendon but temporarily unloads the proximal attachment during activity.
- Avoid provocative grips where possible (e.g., switch grip types, use no-gi if gi gripping is the primary driver).
- Ice post-training: 10 minutes on lateral or medial epicondyle after loading. Reduces local pain sensitisation.

**Phase 1 loading: Isometrics**

Rio et al. (*BJSM*, 2015) demonstrated that isometric exercise at high load produces significant immediate pain relief in tendinopathy — the mechanism is cortical inhibition of pain processing.

- **Lateral epicondylalgia:**
  - Wrist extension isometric: Seated, forearm on thigh, wrist over the edge. Hold a dumbbell or push against a fixed surface in wrist extension. 5 × 45-second holds at 6–7/10 effort. Rest 2 minutes between sets.
  - Perform 2 × daily.
  - Pain should be ≤4/10 during, and should settle within 24 hours.

- **Medial epicondylalgia:**
  - Wrist flexion isometric: Same setup, wrist flexion direction. 5 × 45-second holds.

**Key principle:** Isometrics do not have a significant eccentric component, so they do not load the damaged proximal attachment as heavily as dynamic exercises. They allow training effect without reactive flare.

---

### Phase 2 — Tendon Loading / Heavy Slow Resistance (Weeks 2–6)

**Goal:** Drive collagen remodelling through progressive mechanical loading.

**Heavy Slow Resistance (HSR) is the primary Phase 2 protocol.** A 2022 systematic review (*PMC8911334*) confirmed that HSR outperforms eccentric-only protocols for both pain reduction and functional improvement in lateral epicondylalgia. Eccentric-only loading is no longer considered the gold standard.

**Lateral Epicondylalgia — Tyler Twist Protocol + HSR:**

The Tyler Twist (*PMC2971639*) is one of the most validated single exercises for LE:
- Use a Therabar or rubber flex bar (medium resistance for most athletes).
- Grip the bar with affected arm in pronation (knuckles down), other hand in supination (knuckles up).
- Twist the bar into full pronation with the unaffected hand while maintaining grip with the affected hand.
- Slowly reverse the twist (eccentric component for the affected wrist extensors).
- 3 × 15 daily. Begin with lightest resistance, progress every 1–2 weeks.

**HSR Wrist Extension (LE):**
- Seated, forearm supported on a bench. Dumbbell in affected hand.
- Full wrist extension (concentric) — 3 seconds up; 4-second lowering (eccentric).
- 3 × 12–15 reps. 3 sessions/week. Progress load every 2 sessions if pain ≤3/10.

**HSR Wrist Flexion (ME):**
- Same setup, wrist flexion. 3 × 12–15 reps, 3–0–3 tempo.
- Add pronation (palm rotates downward) under load for compound pronator teres loading.

**Grip strengthening:**
- Stress ball or grip trainer: progressive grip holds. 3 × 20 light, working to 3 × 10 heavy.
- EZ bar or dumbbell finger extension: resist finger extension eccentrically (targets EDC/ECRB complex for LE).

**Forearm soft tissue work (Starrett — *Becoming a Supple Leopard*, 2015):**
Starrett designates Area 5 (arm/elbow/forearm) as requiring consistent soft-tissue maintenance in athletes. Protocol:
- Lacrosse ball/barbell smashing on forearm extensors (lateral compartment) and flexors (medial compartment): 60–120 seconds on each tight area.
- Forearm floss band (voodoo band): wrap from wrist to elbow with moderate compression, make a fist and full wrist circles 30 × each direction, then full elbow flexion/extension 20 reps. Remove band.
- Goal: restore tissue sliding capacity between ECRB and the underlying structures. Starrett notes that tissue restriction at the elbow is almost always bilateral in grapplers even when pain is unilateral.

---

### Phase 3 — Functional Strengthening (Weeks 6–10)

**Goal:** Build sport-specific grip and forearm strength under progressive load.

**Farmer's carries:** Load a trap bar or dumbbells and walk for distance. Start light, build over weeks. Continuous isometric grip under axial load.

**Towel pull-ups:** Drape two towels over a pull-up bar. Grip the towels (simulates gi grip demand) and perform pull-ups. Eliminates bar-specific mechanics. Begin with partial ROM or band-assisted.

**Plate pinch walks:** 10kg plate, pinched with fingers only. Walk for 20–30 metres × 3 sets.

**Wrist extension/flexion with full forearm rotation:**
- Add pronation/supination to the wrist curl pattern — combines all forearm muscle groups.

**Elbow flexion strength (biceps/brachioradialis):**
- Hammer curl (neutral grip): 3 × 12 heavy. The brachioradialis supports both LE and ME loading patterns and is often underdeveloped.

---

### Phase 4 — Return to Full Gripping / Rolling (Weeks 8–12+)

**Goal:** Full return to gi and no-gi grappling with symptom-free grip work.

**Criteria for return to sport:**
- Pain-free wrist extension and flexion through full range under moderate load.
- Grip endurance: can sustain moderate grip × 60 seconds without pain.
- Isometric wrist extension 6–7/10 effort × 45 seconds, no pain during or within 24 hours.
- No morning stiffness or first-grip pain.

**Return progression:**
1. No-gi training only (reduces grip demand significantly). Light positional work.
2. Gi training with reduced grip intensity — back grip, body grip over collar grips.
3. Full gi live rolling, monitoring 24-hour symptom response.
4. Full training load.

**Ongoing maintenance (Starrett Area 5):**
- Daily forearm rolling or flossing band work, 5–10 minutes.
- Do not wait for pain to return — maintain tissue quality ongoing.

**MGH Epicondylitis Protocol note:** The Massachusetts General Hospital Physical Therapy rehabilitation protocol for medial/lateral epicondylitis provides a structured milestones-based progression and is one of the most cited clinical templates for post-surgical and conservative management.

---

## Why Loading Works

Tendons are mechanosensitive structures. Type I collagen synthesis (the functional collagen in tendons) is stimulated by mechanical load through a process called mechanotransduction — strain on the tendon matrix is sensed by tenocytes (tendon cells), which respond by upregulating collagen production and remodelling disorganised tissue.

Rest allows pain to subside but does not drive collagen remodelling. When the athlete returns to the same grip loading without a stronger tendon, the cycle repeats. Progressive loading — starting with isometrics (no joint movement, controlled strain) → slow eccentric/concentric (controlled strain with joint movement) → fast and dynamic (sport-specific loading) — works because each stage progressively increases mechanical stimulus to the tendon without exceeding its current load tolerance.

This is the practical application of Cook and Purdam's continuum: match load to tendon stage, progress systematically, do not skip to high-load dynamic work before the tendon has adapted to slow heavy resistance.

---

## Summary Protocol Snapshot

| Phase | Duration | Key Method | Key Exercises |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 — Settle | Days 1–14 | Load modification + isometrics | Wrist extension/flexion isometrics 5 × 45s |
| 2 — Remodel | Weeks 2–6 | Heavy Slow Resistance (HSR) | Tyler Twist, HSR wrist curls, voodoo band flossing |
| 3 — Functional | Weeks 6–10 | Sport-prep strength | Farmer's carries, towel pull-ups, plate pinch |
| 4 — Return to Sport | Weeks 8–12+ | Sport-specific loading | No-gi → gi drilling → full live rolling |

---

## Citations

1. Cook & Purdam Tendinopathy Continuum (*BJSM* 2009) — https://bjsm.bmj.com/content/43/6/409
2. Heavy Slow Resistance vs Eccentric (LET, *PMC8911334*, 2022) — https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8911334/
3. Tyler Twist RCT (*PMC2971639*) — https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2971639/
4. Rio et al. Isometrics for Tendinopathy (*BJSM* 2015) — https://bjsm.bmj.com/content/49/19/1277
5. MGH Epicondylitis Rehab Protocol — https://www.massgeneral.org/assets/MGH/pdf/orthopaedics/sports-medicine/physical-therapy/rehabilitation-protocol-for-medial-lateral-epicondylitis.pdf
6. StatPearls Medial Epicondylitis (NBK519000) — https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK519000/
