<a id="mobility_01_shoulders.md"></a>

# Opening the Shoulders: Overhead Position, Internal Rotation & Thoracic Spine for Grapplers and Lifters

> **Disclaimer:** This article is general education only. It is not a diagnosis or medical advice. If you have pain during any of these assessments or drills, consult a physiotherapist before proceeding.

---

## Why Shoulder Mobility Matters

Shoulder mobility is the overhead athlete's and grappler's most critical movement quality. Limited shoulder range — whether in overhead, internal rotation, or external rotation — doesn't just create shoulder injuries. Per Gray Cook's joint-by-joint framework (*Movement: Functional Movement Systems*, 2010), **restricted shoulder mobility forces compensations into the cervical spine** (neck pain, headaches) and **lumbar spine** (low back arching to achieve overhead). You fix the mobile joint (glenohumeral/thoracic) to protect the adjacent stable joints (cervical, lumbar).

In BJJ and MMA, the shoulders are loaded in nearly every range of motion:
- Guard passing: shoulder protraction and anterior load.
- Underhooks and overhooks: sustained internal and external rotation under load.
- Guillotines and arm drags: extreme shoulder flexion + internal rotation.
- Kimuras and americanas: end-range ER and IR — these are attacks. If you can't actively access these ranges, a submission locks in faster.
- Heavy press and overhead work: requires a stacked kinetic chain from ankle to wrist.

Kelly Starrett (*Becoming a Supple Leopard*, 2nd ed., 2015) built his entire shoulder mobility system around four shoulder archetypes — positional benchmarks that every athlete should be able to hit. If you can't hit them, you have a performance and injury risk problem.

---

## Anatomy Quick Reference (What Limits Shoulder Mobility)

Donald Neumann (*Kinesiology of the Musculoskeletal System*, 3rd ed.) identifies the primary contributors to shoulder mobility:

**Glenohumeral (GH) joint:** Ball-and-socket with extreme degrees of freedom. GH mobility depends on:
- Posterior capsule extensibility (tight = limits internal rotation, horizontal adduction)
- Anterior capsule (tight = limits external rotation, overhead)
- Rotator cuff muscle length (especially subscapularis IR, infraspinatus/teres minor ER)
- Labral integrity (structural — not a mobility issue, but masquerades as one)

**Scapulothoracic (ST) joint:** The scapula must upwardly rotate during shoulder elevation. Neumann's scapulohumeral rhythm: **2:1 ratio of GH to ST motion**. For every 3° of shoulder elevation, 2° is GH and 1° is ST. If ST motion is restricted (tight serratus anterior, weak lower trap, stiff T-spine), GH must compensate — which stresses the rotator cuff and impinges the subacromial space.

**Thoracic spine:** The T-spine must extend as you raise your arms overhead. A flexed, stiff T-spine blocks scapular upward rotation and tilts the scapula anteriorly — directly increasing subacromial impingement risk. Per Gray Cook's cascade, **T-spine is the root cause of most shoulder mobility problems in athletes.**

**Lats (latissimus dorsi):** A common forgotten restrictor. The lat attaches to the humerus and, when tight, limits shoulder flexion and overhead reach — particularly when combined with anterior tilt of the pelvis (which most athletes also have).

**Pectoralis minor:** Tight pec minor pulls the scapula into anterior tilt and downward rotation, disrupting scapulohumeral rhythm and narrowing the subacromial space.

---

## Self-Assessment: Four Starrett Shoulder Archetypes

Before you mobilise, know your starting position. Kelly Starrett's four shoulder archetypes test your ability to access the positions the shoulder is required to hit in sport and lifting:

### Archetype 1 — Overhead (Arms Elevated)
**Test:** Stand against a wall. Press the entire back flat (lower back, upper back, head). Raise both arms overhead and try to touch the wall with the backs of your hands, keeping elbows straight.
- **Pass:** Arms flat on the wall, no rib flare, no lumbar extension.
- **Fail:** Arms can't reach the wall, or reaching requires lumbar arch or rib flare.
- **Limiting factors:** Posterior capsule tightness, lat restriction, T-spine extension deficit.

### Archetype 2 — Press (Arms Fully Extended Front / Overhead)
**Test:** Overhead press position assessment. Hold a PVC pipe or broomstick overhead. Can you achieve a vertical bar path while maintaining neutral lumbar? Arms should be directly over ears.
- **Pass:** Bar over ears, ribs down, neutral spine.
- **Fail:** Bar drifts forward (shoulder flexion limit), ribs flare to compensate.

### Archetype 3 — Hang (Arms at Sides / Behind Body)
**Test:** Hands clasped behind back. Can you extend the shoulder far enough to link fingers without rounding forward or shrugging?
- **Pass:** Fingers clasp comfortably with upright posture.
- **Fail:** Requires shoulder protraction or thoracic rounding.
- **Limiting factors:** Anterior capsule, pec minor, biceps tendon.

### Archetype 4 — Front Rack (Elbows Up, Arms Forward)
**Test:** Front rack position for front squat or clean. Elbows up parallel to floor, bar across deltoids, fingers lightly resting on bar. Assess elbow height and wrist position.
- **Pass:** Elbows at or above parallel, wrist neutral, no forearm breakdown.
- **Fail:** Elbows drop, wrists break, or requires crossing arms.
- **Limiting factors:** Lat tightness, triceps, posterior capsule.

**Also assess GIRD (Glenohumeral Internal Rotation Deficit):**
Neumann notes that GIRD of 10–20° or greater significantly increases shoulder pathology risk. Lie supine with the arm at 90° abduction. Have a partner stabilise the scapula. Compare internal rotation range side to side. Asymmetry of 15°+ warrants targeted posterior capsule work.

---

## What Restricts Shoulder Mobility: The Common Culprits

| Structure | Effect | Athlete Pattern |
|---|---|---|
| Posterior glenohumeral capsule | Limits IR, horizontal adduction | All overhead athletes, bench pressers |
| Lat (LD) | Limits shoulder flexion + overhead | Heavy pullers, gymnasts, grapplers |
| Pec minor | Tilts scapula anterior → impingement | Bench press-dominant athletes |
| Upper traps (tight) | Elevates scapula, disrupts rhythm | Stressed athletes, desk workers |
| T-spine extension | Blocks scapular upward rotation | Everyone with a desk job |
| Subscapularis | Limits ER (if tight) | Overhead athletes |

---

## Mobility Protocol: Opening the Shoulders

Kelly Starrett's mobility system (*Becoming a Supple Leopard*) prescribes a combination of **soft-tissue work (smashing) + joint mobilisation (distraction/compression) + positional end-range loading**. Area 3 (posterior shoulder/lat) and Area 4 (anterior shoulder/chest) are the two primary target areas for shoulder mobility.

### Step 1 — Soft Tissue: Posterior Shoulder + Lat

**Posterior shoulder smash (Area 3):**
- Lie on your side, lacrosse ball under the posterior deltoid/infraspinatus.
- Reach the arm across your body (horizontal adduction) to increase pressure. Hold on tight spots 30–60 seconds.
- Move ball 2–3 positions across the posterior cuff.
- 2–3 minutes per side.

**Lat smash:**
- Place a barbell or lacrosse ball in the lat, just posterior to the armpit.
- Arm overhead, create compression, internally and externally rotate the arm.
- 60–90 seconds per side.
- This is one of the most effective drills for improving overhead position.

### Step 2 — Soft Tissue: Anterior Shoulder + Pec

**Pec minor / anterior capsule smash (Area 4):**
- Lacrosse ball into the pec minor (just medial and inferior to the coracoid process — between the clavicle and the first few ribs).
- Apply anterior-to-posterior pressure. Hold tight spots.
- Move arm through horizontal adduction/abduction while holding pressure.
- 60–90 seconds per side.

**Upper trap + levator smash:**
- Trap the upper trap between a lacrosse ball and a wall. Rotate head away. Hold on tender areas 30–60 seconds. Move superiorly and inferiorly.

### Step 3 — T-Spine Mobilisation (Critical Foundation)

Per Gray Cook's cascade principle, open the T-spine first — everything above depends on it.

**T-spine extension over foam roller:**
- Place foam roller perpendicular to the spine at mid-thoracic level (T4–T8 is the most commonly restricted segment).
- Support the head with interlaced hands. Drop back over the roller into extension.
- 10 slow reps. Move roller up and down the T-spine in 2–3 positions.
- This is the single most important drill for shoulder overhead position.

**Thread the needle:**
- Start in quadruped (all fours). Slide one arm under the body, rotating the thorax and reaching for the opposite wall.
- 10 reps each side with a 2-second hold at end range.
- Targets unilateral T-spine rotation — important for the asymmetric positions of BJJ.

**Quadruped thoracic rotation with hip lock:**
- Quadruped, one hand behind head. Lock the hips by sitting back slightly. Rotate thorax, bringing elbow to the ceiling.
- 10 reps each side.

### Step 4 — Glenohumeral Joint Mobilisation

**Banded shoulder distraction (posterior capsule / IR):**
- Loop a resistance band high on a rig. Stand facing the rig, step back to create tension. Place the band over the posterior shoulder/armpit area. Let the band pull the humeral head anteriorly.
- From this distracted position, rotate the arm internally (thumb down) 10 reps, then hold at end-range IR for 30 seconds.
- This is Starrett's primary posterior capsule mobilisation.
- 2–3 minutes per tight side.

**Sleeper stretch (posterior capsule):**
- Lie on the affected side, shoulder at 90° flexion, elbow at 90°.
- Use the top hand to gently press the bottom wrist toward the floor (internal rotation direction).
- Hold 30–45 seconds. 3 sets.
- Supported by evidence for reducing GIRD and shoulder impingement in overhead athletes.

**Wall angel / reach:**
- Stand with back flat against wall. Press the dorsal surface of forearms and wrists against the wall. Slide arms overhead maintaining full contact.
- 10 reps slow. If contact breaks at any point (forearm lifts off wall), that is your end of shoulder flexion + T-spine extension range.

### Step 5 — End-Range Loading (Positional Work)

**Banded overhead reach hold:**
- Loop a band from a low anchor. Attach to one wrist. Reach overhead. Let the band pull the arm into true overhead elevation. Hold 30–60 seconds per side.
- Creates capsular stretch at the very end of shoulder flexion range.

**Doorway stretch for anterior chain:**
- Stand in a doorway. Place forearm on the frame at 90° abduction. Lean through the door slowly, keeping shoulder packed (not shrugged). Feel the stretch across the anterior shoulder and pec.
- Hold 30–45 seconds per side. Do not let the shoulder migrate forward (shrug).

**Overhead squat hold:**
- With a PVC pipe or empty barbell overhead, hold the overhead squat position for 30–60 seconds.
- This integrates shoulder overhead mobility with hip and ankle mobility — a full-chain assessment and drill simultaneously.

---

## BJJ / Lifting Application

**For grapplers:** The overhead archetype determines how quickly you get tapped from kimuras and americanas. If your IR is blocked, the submission engages at 40° rather than 80° — you have half the time to escape. Spend 5 minutes per session on posterior capsule work (banded distraction or sleeper stretch) and 3 minutes on pec minor smash.

**For overhead pressing athletes:** T-spine extension and lat mobility are the two bottle-necks to a pain-free overhead press. Address them before every overhead session. If you can't pass the overhead wall test, you should not be loading overhead — you're pressing with lumbar extension, not shoulder flexion.

**Starrett's prescription:** 10–15 minutes of daily shoulder mobility work for athletes with existing restrictions. Use Areas 2 (T-spine), 3 (posterior shoulder), and 4 (anterior shoulder) from his area map. Prioritise the drills that match your failing archetype.

---

## Session Structure Suggestion

**Before training (5–8 minutes):**
1. T-spine foam roller — 2 minutes
2. Lat smash — 60 seconds per tight side
3. Banded shoulder distraction — 2 minutes per tight side
4. 10 wall angels (active end-range practice)

**After training / standalone session (10–15 minutes):**
1. Posterior shoulder lacrosse ball smash — 2 min/side
2. Pec minor lacrosse ball — 90 sec/side
3. Thread the needle — 10 reps/side
4. Sleeper stretch — 3 × 30 sec/side
5. Overhead banded reach hold — 60 sec/side

---

## Citations

1. Gray Cook — *Movement: Functional Movement Systems* (2010)
2. Kelly Starrett — *Becoming a Supple Leopard* (2nd ed., 2015)
3. Donald Neumann — *Kinesiology of the Musculoskeletal System* (3rd ed.)
4. JOSPT Rotator Cuff Clinical Practice Guideline (2025) — https://www.jospt.org/doi/10.2519/jospt.2025.13182
5. Rio et al. Isometrics for Tendinopathy (*BJSM* 2015) — https://bjsm.bmj.com/content/49/19/1277


---


<a id="mobility_02_hips.md"></a>

# Opening the Hips: Flexion, Extension, Rotation & the Deep Squat for Grapplers and Lifters

> **Disclaimer:** This article is general education only. It is not a diagnosis or medical advice. If you have hip pain during any of these assessments or drills, consult a physiotherapist before proceeding.

---

## Why Hip Mobility Matters

The hip is the engine of athletic movement — and the most commonly restricted joint in strength athletes and grapplers. Per Gray Cook's joint-by-joint framework (*Movement: Functional Movement Systems*, 2010), the hip is a **mobility** joint. It must move freely and multi-planarly. When it doesn't:

- The lumbar spine compensates by moving instead of staying stable → **chronic low back pain**.
- The knee joint absorbs rotation it was never designed for → **knee pain and ACL/meniscus risk**.
- The thoracic spine compensates further upstream → full-chain dysfunction.

Cook's cascade is clear: **restricted hip mobility is often the origin point of both low back pain and knee pain** in athletes. Fix the hips first.

In BJJ and grappling, hip mobility is not optional — it is a direct competitive skill. Half-guard retention, deep guard, berimbolo, back-takes, hip escapes, and bridging all require extreme ranges of hip flexion, extension, external rotation, and internal rotation, often under load and in compromised positions. In lifting, the deep squat, Romanian deadlift, and sumo deadlift are all hip-mobility-gated movements.

Kelly Starrett (*Becoming a Supple Leopard*, 2nd ed., 2015) describes three hip archetypes — positional templates that every athlete should be able to achieve.

---

## Anatomy Quick Reference

Donald Neumann (*Kinesiology of the Musculoskeletal System*, 3rd ed.) describes the hip as a ball-and-socket with 3 degrees of freedom:
- Flexion / Extension (sagittal plane — the most used in lifting)
- Abduction / Adduction (frontal plane — critical for guard and BJJ stance)
- Internal / External rotation (transverse plane — the most restricted in athletes)

**Primary hip mobility limiters:**
- **Hip capsule (posterior and inferior):** The first restrictor in hip IR and extension. Responds to joint mobilisation (distraction + internal rotation bias).
- **Psoas / Iliacus (hip flexors):** Tight hip flexors are near-universal in athletes who train hard and sit between sessions. Limits hip extension range — the most critical range for deadlift lockout, sprint mechanics, and guard recovery.
- **Piriformis and deep external rotators:** Limit internal rotation. Often the cause of the "hip impingement-feeling" in deep squat that is actually muscular, not bony.
- **Adductors:** Limit hip abduction (important for wide stance and guard) and contribute to limited IR when tight.
- **Rectus femoris:** Tight rec fem limits hip extension with knee flexion simultaneously — blocks lunge and kicking mechanics.
- **TFL/IT band:** Lateral hip tension contributes to hip abduction stiffness and hip drop under load.

**Bony / structural hip shape:** A small percentage of athletes have structurally shallow acetabulae (more hip IR/flexion available) or deep/anteverted/retroverted hips (less IR or less ER). If hip impingement symptoms (sharp pinch at front of hip in deep flexion) persist despite thorough soft-tissue and mobilisation work, consult a sports medicine doctor for structural assessment before pushing through.

---

## Self-Assessment: Three Starrett Hip Archetypes

### Archetype 1 — Squat (Hip Hinge — Two Shapes)

**Shape A: Below-Parallel Squat**
- Stand feet shoulder-width or slightly wider, toes 15–30° out.
- Squat to below parallel (hip crease below knee level).
- **Pass:** Full depth achieved with: neutral spine (no rounding), heels flat, knees tracking toes, chest upright.
- **Fail:** Heels rise, lumbar rounds ("butt wink"), knees cave (valgus), unable to reach depth.

**Shape B: Deadlift Hinge**
- Stand feet hip-width, hinge at hip keeping spine neutral.
- Hip crease should move back while shins stay vertical.
- **Pass:** Deep hip hinge with neutral spine, hamstring tension felt, not low back tightness.
- **Fail:** Rounding of lumbar spine early in hinge — indicates posterior chain restriction (hamstrings, hip capsule) or motor control deficit.

### Archetype 2 — Pistol (Full Hip Flexion + Full Ankle Dorsiflexion)

- Single-leg squat to full depth (thigh below parallel on one leg, other leg extended forward).
- **Pass:** Achieved with neutral spine, no collapse, non-squatting leg horizontal.
- **Fail:** Unable to reach depth, lumbar rounds, collapse inward.
- Note: Pistol failure often reflects **ankle dorsiflexion deficit** as much as hip restriction. Assess both.

### Archetype 3 — Lunge (One Leg Behind — Running / Throwing / Guard Recovery)

- Step into a deep lunge. Back knee on floor.
- **Pass:** Hip of the rear leg achieves near-full extension (minimal anterior pelvic tilt), front knee tracks over toe.
- **Fail:** Anterior pelvic tilt on rear hip (hip flexors pulling the pelvis forward), trunk leans forward to compensate, unable to keep upright torso.

**Additional assessment — 90/90 Test (Hip IR + ER):**
- Sit on the floor, both legs at 90° hip/knee angles, one in front (ER) and one to the side (IR).
- The range in both directions and whether you can sit upright in both positions indicates both ER and IR mobility.
- Most grapplers will have one side significantly more restricted — asymmetry in rotation is the most clinically significant finding.

---

## What Restricts Hip Mobility: The Common Culprits

| Structure | Effect on Movement | Who Gets This |
|---|---|---|
| Hip capsule (posterior/inferior) | Limits IR and hip flexion depth | All heavy squatters and grapplers |
| Psoas/iliacus | Limits hip extension, anterior pelvic tilt | Athletes who sit between training sessions |
| Piriformis/deep ERs | Limits hip IR | Powerlifters, cyclists |
| Adductors | Limits abduction and contributes to IR deficit | All grapplers (constant adductor loading) |
| Rectus femoris | Limits hip extension with knee bent | Sprinters, kickers, guard players |
| TFL/ITB | Lateral hip stiffness, hip abduction restriction | Runners, cyclists, lateral athletes |

---

## Mobility Protocol: Opening the Hips

Starrett's mobility prescription for hips uses Areas 6 (trunk/psoas), 7 (glutes/hip capsule), 8 (hip flexors/quads), and 9 (adductors) from his body map.

### Step 1 — Soft Tissue: Glutes + Hip Capsule (Area 7)

**Glute smash (lacrosse ball):**
- Sit on a lacrosse ball placed under the glute (posterior hip).
- Cross the leg (figure-4 position to increase pressure).
- Work the ball around the entire glute — superior, middle, inferior fibres.
- When you find a tight spot, pin it and flex/extend the hip, or rotate IR/ER while holding pressure.
- 2–3 minutes per side.

**Piriformis smash:**
- Same setup. Target the area lateral to the tailbone and inferior to the posterior superior iliac spine (PSIS). This is where piriformis and the deep external rotators sit.
- Flex the hip 90° and hold pressure on tender areas.
- 60–90 seconds per side.

### Step 2 — Soft Tissue: Psoas + Hip Flexors (Area 8)

**The Couch Stretch — Starrett's Primary Hip Flexor Protocol:**

Starrett calls the couch stretch the "king of hip extension restoration." It is the primary prescription for anyone with anterior pelvic tilt, limited hip extension, or deadlift lockout issues.

Setup:
- Back foot elevated on a couch, wall, or box. Rear shin vertical against the surface.
- Front leg forward, shin vertical.
- Drive the hips forward (anterior pelvis) while squeezing the glute of the rear leg. The glute squeeze is non-negotiable — it drives the hip into true extension rather than just anteriorly tilting the pelvis.
- Keep the torso upright.
- Hold: 2 minutes per side, minimum. Starrett recommends 2–5 minutes per side for tight athletes.

This drill specifically targets the rectus femoris, psoas, and anterior hip capsule simultaneously.

**Quad smash (barbell or foam roller):**
- Lie prone. Place a barbell (in a rack at floor height) or foam roller across the anterior thigh.
- Apply body weight pressure and flex/extend the knee, rotating the hip. Work from just above the knee to the hip crease.
- 2 minutes per side.

### Step 3 — Soft Tissue: Adductors (Area 9)

**Adductor smash:**
- Lie prone. Place a foam roller or barbell perpendicular to the inner thigh.
- Apply pressure and rotate the hip into ER/IR. Work from knee to groin.
- 90 seconds per side. Often very tender in grapplers.

**Banded adductor mobilisation:**
- Loop a band around the inner thigh/groin crease. Step into a wide stance with the banded leg forward.
- Drop into a lateral lunge position with the banded leg bent, other leg straight. The band pulls the femur into abduction.
- Pulse in/out of the bottom position 10 times, or hold for 30 seconds.
- Restores adductor length + hip abduction range simultaneously.

### Step 4 — Joint Mobilisation: Hip Capsule

**Banded hip distraction — Hip Flexion (squatting pattern):**
- Loop a band around the top of the femur (as high as possible into the crease). Anchor the band behind you.
- Step forward to create tension (band pulling hip posteriorly).
- Drop into a squat. The band distracts the femoral head posteriorly, opening the anterior capsule.
- 10 slow squats or 60-second hold at depth per side.
- Starrett considers this one of the most effective hip mobilisations — it works by creating joint space and then moving the joint through range within that space.

**Banded hip distraction — Hip Extension:**
- Same band setup, but face away from the anchor.
- Step forward into a deep lunge. The band distracts the hip forward (posterior capsule).
- Hold 60 seconds per side or perform 10 reps of knee drive forward.

**90/90 Hip Stretch (IR + ER end-range loading):**
- Sit in 90/90 position. Hold for 60 seconds on the restriction side.
- Progress: lean the torso forward over the front leg (hip IR bias) or rotate the torso toward the rear leg (hip ER bias).
- 2–3 rounds per side, focusing on the more restricted rotation direction.

### Step 5 — End-Range Loading (Positional Work)

**Deep squat hold:**
- Use a rig, suspension straps, or counterweight to help hold the bottom position.
- Feet shoulder-width, 15–30° toe out. Elbows in, chest up.
- Hold 1–3 minutes total at bottom. Breathe and relax into the position.
- This is active end-range hip flexion with external rotation — the highest demand position for the hip capsule and adductors.

**Hip 90/90 rotations (controlled articular rotations — CARs concept):**
- Lying supine, hip and knee at 90°. Actively rotate the hip from IR to ER and back, controlling the full arc.
- 10 slow, controlled reps per side.
- These reinforce motor control through the full available range.

**Jefferson curl (cautious — advanced):**
- Stand on a step with a light barbell. Slowly roll down through the entire spine, allowing full hip flexion + posterior chain lengthening.
- This integrates hip flexion, hamstring length, and lumbar flexion — excellent for grapplers who need full hip flexion range in guard.
- Start with zero load. Add load conservatively over weeks. 3 × 5 reps with 5-second descent.

---

## BJJ / Lifting Application

**Guard players and bottom game:** Hip IR and adductor mobility directly determine your guard range and the positions you can maintain. Work the 90/90 drill and adductor smash daily.

**Takedown / wrestling:** Hip extension range (psoas length) determines the depth and recovery speed of your shots and sprawls. Couch Stretch 2 min/side every session.

**Squatters and deadlifters:** Posterior hip capsule (banded distraction) and adductors are your limiting factors in squat depth and sumo stance width. Address them before every lower-body session. Do not chase depth with lumbar rounding — that's a mobility bypass, not a mobility solution.

**Starrett's prescription:** 10 minutes of daily hip mobility. Priority: couch stretch (hip flexors), glute/piriformis smash, banded hip distraction. Consistency over intensity. Two minutes a day of couch stretch beats 20 minutes once a week.

---

## Session Structure Suggestion

**Before training (5–8 minutes):**
1. Banded hip distraction (flexion bias) — 60 sec/side
2. 90/90 hold — 60 sec each position
3. 10 hip CARs per side (slow)
4. 10 deep squat hold with rig (30 seconds at bottom)

**After training / standalone session (12–15 minutes):**
1. Couch stretch — 2 min/side
2. Glute smash (lacrosse ball) — 2 min/side
3. Adductor smash — 90 sec/side
4. Quad smash — 2 min/side
5. Banded hip distraction (extension bias) — 60 sec/side
6. Deep squat hold — 2 minutes total

---

## Citations

1. Gray Cook — *Movement: Functional Movement Systems* (2010)
2. Kelly Starrett — *Becoming a Supple Leopard* (2nd ed., 2015)
3. Donald Neumann — *Kinesiology of the Musculoskeletal System* (3rd ed.)


---


<a id="mobility_03_ankles.md"></a>

# Ankle Mobility: Dorsiflexion, Squat Depth & Joint Mobilisation for Athletes

> **Disclaimer:** This article is general education only. It is not a diagnosis or medical advice. If you have ankle pain during any of these assessments or drills, consult a physiotherapist before proceeding.

---

## Why Ankle Mobility Matters

The ankle is the first joint in the kinetic chain. Its primary athletic demand is **dorsiflexion** — the movement where the shin moves forward over the foot (think: bottom of a squat, landing from a jump, the front-foot position during a takedown shot). When dorsiflexion is restricted, every joint above pays the price.

Gray Cook makes this explicit in *Movement: Functional Movement Systems* (2010): the ankle is a **mobility** joint. When it loses mobility:

- **Knee pain** develops — the knee compensates for the blocked dorsiflexion by collapsing into valgus (knees cave in) or by shifting load to the patella via a forward-tilting shin.
- **Low back pain** follows — restricted squat depth drives lumbar rounding at the bottom of the squat or deadlift.
- **Hip dysfunction** — the athlete with stiff ankles compensates with excessive forward lean, loading the hip in an anterior tilt pattern.

Cook's joint-by-joint cascade starting from the bottom: **ankle mobility restriction cascades into knee, hip, and lumbar pathology.** This is why ankle mobility work belongs in every strength athlete's and grappler's warm-up, not just in the programme of someone with ankle pain.

Kelly Starrett (*Becoming a Supple Leopard*, 2nd ed., 2015) addresses the ankle in Areas 12 (shin), 13 (calf), and 14 (ankle/foot) of his body map and describes ankle restriction as one of the most undertreated mobility problems in athletes.

---

## Anatomy Quick Reference

Donald Neumann (*Kinesiology of the Musculoskeletal System*, 3rd ed.) describes the talocrural (ankle) joint as a hinge joint between the tibia/fibula (mortise) and the talus. The primary motion is:

- **Dorsiflexion (DF):** Shin moves forward, toes point toward shin. Normal range: 15–20°.
- **Plantarflexion (PF):** Toes point away. Normal range: 40–50°.

**Why dorsiflexion is the limiting factor:**
The talus must glide posteriorly in the mortise as the shin moves forward. If the posterior ankle structures are tight — or if the talar posterior glide is restricted (joint mobility issue) — dorsiflexion stops short. The athlete then compensates with heel rise or tibial external rotation (knee cave).

**What restricts dorsiflexion:**

| Structure | Type | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Gastrocnemius (calf) | Soft tissue — muscle | Restricts DF with knee extended |
| Soleus | Soft tissue — muscle | Restricts DF with knee bent (more sport-specific) |
| Achilles tendon | Soft tissue — tendon | Passive restriction in DF |
| Posterior ankle capsule | Joint capsule | Restricts talar posterior glide — joint mob required |
| Peroneal tightness | Lateral soft tissue | May contribute to supination/ankle inversion bias |
| Previous lateral ankle sprain | Structural | Scar tissue in anterior capsule — can block DF and talar glide |

**Key distinction:** Muscle tightness responds to stretching. Joint mobility restriction (talar glide) responds to joint mobilisation. You need to address both — and a common error is athletes who only stretch the calf and wonder why squat depth doesn't improve.

---

## Self-Assessment: Dorsiflexion Tests

### Test 1 — Wall Ankle Dorsiflexion Test (Weight-Bearing DF)

This is the gold standard field test for dorsiflexion range:

1. Stand facing a wall. Place the big toe of the test foot against the wall (or a chalk line on the floor).
2. Step the foot back 4–5 cm from the wall.
3. Drive the knee toward the wall over the 2nd toe, keeping the heel flat on the floor.
4. **Pass:** Knee touches the wall (or you achieve this from 5 cm back) without heel rise and without knee deviation medially.
5. **Fail:** Heel lifts, or you cannot touch the wall from 4 cm.
6. **Asymmetry:** Greater than 1–2 cm side-to-side difference is clinically significant.

**Norm:** Most athletes should be able to achieve the wall touch from at least 4 cm. Elite athletes and gymnasts typically hit 7–10 cm.

### Test 2 — Overhead Squat Baseline

Stand feet shoulder-width. Raise both arms overhead. Perform a squat to full depth.
- **Signs of DF restriction:** Heels rise off the floor, excessive forward lean (torso nearly horizontal), knees cave inward.
- Note: overhead squat failure can also reflect hip or T-spine restriction. Confirm DF is the culprit by placing a 5–10mm heel raise under both heels and retesting. If squat depth improves significantly, DF is the primary limiter.

### Test 3 — Single-Leg Squat / Pistol Test

Lower yourself onto one leg to squat depth. Watch knee tracking.
- Knee cave (valgus collapse) combined with limited depth = DF restriction contribution.

---

## Mobility Protocol: Opening the Ankles

### Step 1 — Soft Tissue: Calf + Achilles (Areas 13)

**Calf smash with barbell:**
- Sit on the floor, place a barbell (or foam roller) under the calf, just proximal to the Achilles.
- Apply body weight. Point and flex the foot 20 times, then rotate the ankle in circles.
- Move the barbell from the lower calf (soleus) to the upper calf (gastrocnemius), 3–4 positions.
- 90 seconds per position per side.
- Starrett uses the barbell for deeper tissue penetration than a foam roller — it's more effective but more intense.

**Gastroc smash with lacrosse ball:**
- Target the Achilles tendon/lower calf junction (the soleus-gastroc interface).
- Apply weight through the leg. Plantarflex and dorsiflex the foot while holding pressure.
- 60–90 seconds per side.

**Shin smash (Area 12 — anterior compartment):**
- Use a barbell or massage stick across the anterior compartment (tibialis anterior and peroneals).
- Apply compression and flex/extend the foot against the pressure.
- 60 seconds per side.
- Anterior compartment tightness can contribute to ankle restriction by limiting tibial forward movement.

### Step 2 — Stretching: Static Calf and Soleus

**Gastroc stretch (knee straight):**
- Stand at a wall, place one foot back, heel flat, toes pointing slightly inward.
- Drive the hips forward keeping the knee locked.
- Hold 45–60 seconds × 2 sets per side.
- **Key coaching cue:** Keep the arch of the foot up (avoid pronating/collapsing inward). Collapsing the arch is a mobility bypass, not a true stretch.

**Soleus stretch (knee bent):**
- Same position but bend the back knee and drive it toward the wall.
- The bent knee takes the gastrocnemius out of the equation and isolates the soleus — the deeper, more sport-relevant calf muscle.
- Hold 45–60 seconds × 2 sets per side.
- This stretch is more relevant for squatting and athletic mechanics than the straight-leg stretch.

### Step 3 — Joint Mobilisation: Talar Posterior Glide

This is the most important step for athletes whose DF restriction persists despite soft-tissue work. If the talus cannot glide posteriorly in the mortise, no amount of calf stretching will fix the problem.

**Banded ankle distraction (posterior talar glide):**
- Loop a resistance band around the ankle at the talar neck level (just distal to the malleoli).
- Anchor the band behind you, low to the ground.
- Step forward into a lunge position with the banded foot forward.
- The band applies a posterior force to the talus, encouraging posterior glide as you drive the knee forward.
- Drive the knee forward over the 2nd toe — 15 slow reps or hold 30–60 seconds at end range.
- 2–3 minutes per restricted side.

Per Starrett: this is the primary ankle mobilisation for squatting and athletic populations. The band creates distraction that makes the natural glide available — something stretching alone cannot achieve.

**Kneeling ankle dorsiflexion (weight-bearing mob):**
- Kneel on one knee, front foot flat on the floor.
- Drive the front knee forward over the toes, keeping the heel down.
- 15 controlled reps → 30-second hold at the end range position.
- Can be loaded by placing a dumbbell on the front thigh.

### Step 4 — End-Range Loading: Loaded Squat Positions

Once range is open, you need to own it under load. Mobility without motor control reverts quickly.

**Goblet squat hold at depth:**
- Hold a kettlebell or dumbbell at chest height.
- Descend to full squat depth (below parallel). Sit in the hole.
- Use the elbows against the inner knees to gently push the knees out (do not force it).
- Hold 1–3 minutes cumulative.
- This is active end-range loading of ankle DF + hip flexion simultaneously. The goblet counterbalance allows a more upright torso and genuine heel-flat depth.

**Calf raise + eccentric lowering (loaded DF end range):**
- Stand on the edge of a step. Rise onto toes, then slowly lower the heels below step level.
- 3-second lowering. 3 × 15 reps.
- Trains the ankle through its full range under load — strength at end range is required for true joint ownership.

**3-way ankle mobility drill (Starrett warm-up):**
- Stand feet together. Perform 10 squats with feet parallel, then 10 squats with feet turned in (pigeon-toed), then 10 squats with feet wide and toes out.
- Each position challenges a different aspect of ankle DF and hip ER/IR combination.
- Excellent before lower-body training.

---

## Previous Ankle Sprain and DF Loss

Lateral ankle sprains (inversion sprains — the most common ankle injury in sport) frequently result in:
- **Anterior capsule thickening:** The anterior talofibular ligament (ATFL) thickens during healing and impinges in DF. This responds to banded talar mobilisation.
- **Loss of peroneal control:** Peroneal weakness after inversion sprain causes the ankle to remain supination-biased, reducing available pronation during dorsiflexion.
- **Posterior impingement** (less common): Os trigonum or posterior capsule thickening blocks PF — relevant for ballet and kicking athletes.

If you've had repeated ankle sprains, add single-leg balance training and peroneal strengthening (banded eversion) to your programme — not just mobility work. Ankle stability is the other side of the coin.

---

## BJJ / Lifting Application

**Grapplers:** Ankle dorsiflexion directly affects the quality of your single-leg takedown shots. A restricted ankle forces the front knee to cave or the torso to lean forward excessively during a deep shot — reducing power generation and increasing injury risk at the knee. The corollary: improved DF = deeper, more powerful shots with better knee alignment.

Guard and closed guard players: the ankle is constantly loaded in plantarflexion and dorsiflexion during guard hooks, triangles, and body lock positions. Ankle soft-tissue work (calf smash, shin area) maintains tissue quality and reduces cramping that disrupts guard work.

**Squatters:** Heel rise during squats is almost always DF-related. Before adding heel wedges as a permanent fix, address the DF restriction with banded talar mobilisation, soleus stretching, and deep squat holds. The goal is to eliminate the need for wedges.

**Starrett's prescription:** 5–10 minutes of ankle work daily for athletes with DF restriction. Banded distraction + calf smash are the two highest-yield drills. At minimum, run through the banded distraction drill before every lower-body session.

---

## Session Structure Suggestion

**Before training (5 minutes):**
1. Banded ankle distraction — 90 sec/side
2. Soleus stretch (knee bent) — 45 sec/side
3. 10 wall DF dribbles per side (drive knee to wall, 10 fast reps)
4. 10 goblet squat holds at depth (5 seconds each)

**After training / standalone session (10–12 minutes):**
1. Calf smash (barbell) — 2 min/side (3 positions)
2. Gastroc stretch (straight leg) — 60 sec/side
3. Soleus stretch (bent knee) — 60 sec/side
4. Banded talar mobilisation — 2 min/side
5. Goblet squat hold — 2 minutes total

---

## Citations

1. Gray Cook — *Movement: Functional Movement Systems* (2010)
2. Kelly Starrett — *Becoming a Supple Leopard* (2nd ed., 2015)
3. Donald Neumann — *Kinesiology of the Musculoskeletal System* (3rd ed.)


---


<a id="mobility_04_joint_by_joint.md"></a>

# The Joint-by-Joint Chain: Why Pain Is Rarely Where the Problem Is

> **Disclaimer:** This article is general education only. It is not a diagnosis or medical advice. Consult a physiotherapist for individual assessment.

---

## The Idea That Changes How You Train

Most athletes treat pain where they feel it. Knee hurts → work on the knee. Back hurts → stretch the back. Shoulder aches → mobilise the shoulder.

This approach misses the most common cause of pain in strength athletes and grapplers: **compensation from a joint that was supposed to move but didn't**.

The joint-by-joint approach, developed by physical therapist Gray Cook and strength coach Mike Boyle, and detailed in Cook's *Movement: Functional Movement Systems* (2010), provides a simple but powerful map of the human body as a stack of alternating joints — each requiring either **mobility** or **stability** as its primary function. When a joint that should be mobile becomes stiff, the joint immediately above or below it (which is supposed to be stable) is forced to become mobile instead.

The result: the stable joint moves excessively → tissues are loaded beyond their tolerance → pain develops at the stable joint, not the mobile joint where the problem originated.

**The problem is upstream or downstream from where you hurt.**

---

## The Joint-by-Joint Map

Working from the ground up:

| Joint | Primary Need | Movement Plane |
|---|---|---|
| Foot | Mobility + stability | Multi-planar |
| Ankle | **Mobility** | Sagittal (dorsiflexion primary) |
| Knee | **Stability** | Sagittal (hinge) |
| Hip | **Mobility** | Multi-planar (sagittal + frontal + transverse) |
| Lumbar spine | **Stability** | Minimal ROM — stiffness is a feature, not a bug |
| Thoracic spine | **Mobility** | Sagittal (extension) + transverse (rotation) |
| Scapula | **Stability** | — (a platform, not a hinge) |
| Glenohumeral joint | **Mobility** | Multi-planar |
| Elbow | Mobility | Sagittal + pronation/supination |
| Wrist | Mobility | Sagittal + radial/ulnar deviation |
| Cervical spine | **Mobility** + stability | Multi-planar (but needs local stability) |

**The alternating pattern is not coincidental.** It reflects the evolutionary architecture of human locomotion. The mobile joints generate force and range. The stable joints transmit force safely and protect the body during loading.

---

## The Four Core Cascade Rules

These four relationships explain the majority of chronic pain in athletes:

### 1. Ankle Mobility → Knee Pain

**If the ankle can't dorsiflex**, the shin can't travel forward over the foot during squatting, landing, running, and shooting. The ankle essentially "blocks out" of the movement. Something has to give — and it's the knee. The tibia externally rotates, the knee collapses inward (valgus), and the patellofemoral joint absorbs a shear force it was never designed for.

**Result:** Patellofemoral pain syndrome ("runner's knee"), IT band syndrome, medial knee pain.

**Fix:** Restore ankle dorsiflexion. Not knee massage. Not IT band rolling (a symptom treatment, not a cause treatment). Ankle banded mobilisation + soleus stretching.

### 2. Hip Mobility → Low Back Pain

**If the hip can't move** — specifically, if hip flexion, extension, or rotation range is limited — the lumbar spine compensates. In the squat: the lumbar spine rounds into flexion to achieve depth (the "butt wink"). In the deadlift: the lumbar spine flexes under load because the hips can't hinge deeply enough. In the grappling scramble: the lumbar spine rotates excessively because the hips can't rotate.

The lumbar spine is supposed to be a stability joint — it has 5 degrees of total rotation, and only a few degrees of rotation per segment. It was never designed to be a primary movement joint under load.

**Result:** Posterior disc irritation, lumbar facet irritation, SI joint dysfunction, chronic low back pain.

**Fix:** Restore hip mobility (capsule, psoas, adductors, piriformis). The low back pain often resolves without touching the low back.

### 3. Hip Mobility → Knee Pain (Alternate Path)

This cascade runs in both directions through the knee. If the hip cannot generate adequate internal rotation, the femur stays externally rotated during squatting and running mechanics. The tibial rotation compensation loads the medial and lateral compartment of the knee asymmetrically.

Additionally, tight hip flexors (weak hip extension) create compensatory quadriceps-dominant patterns — the athlete uses quad extension to finish movements rather than hip extension. This overloads the patellofemoral joint.

**Fix:** Hip capsule mobilisation + posterior chain (glute/hamstring) activation.

### 4. Thoracic Spine Mobility → Neck Pain AND Shoulder Pain AND Low Back Pain

The thoracic spine is arguably the most under-addressed joint in athlete programming. It needs to extend (for overhead work) and rotate (for thoracic rotation in throwing, striking, guard passing, wrestling).

When it can't extend:
- The cervical spine extends more to compensate → chronic neck pain and cervicogenic headaches.
- The scapula can't upwardly rotate (needs T-spine extension as its base) → subacromial impingement, rotator cuff pathology.

When it can't rotate:
- The lumbar spine compensates with rotation it can't safely provide → disc and facet irritation.
- Golfers and baseball players who can't rotate through the T-spine drive it through the lumbar → they're constantly injured.

**Fix:** Foam roller T-spine extension + thoracic rotation drills (thread the needle, quadruped rotation). Daily, not occasionally.

---

## Practical Application: Self-Diagnosing the Cascade

When you experience pain, work through this checklist:

**Back pain?**
- Assess: hip mobility (90/90 test, hip hinge test, couch stretch).
- Assess: thoracic extension (overhead wall test, foam roller T-spine test).
- Almost always: the hips or T-spine are restricted. Treat those first.

**Knee pain?**
- Assess: ankle dorsiflexion (wall test, 4 cm mark).
- Assess: hip IR and extension.
- Treat the ankle and/or hip before assuming the knee is the problem.

**Shoulder / neck pain?**
- Assess: thoracic extension and rotation.
- Assess: scapular stability (does the scapula wing? Does it upwardly rotate during overhead?).
- Treat the T-spine and scapular stabilisers before loading the shoulder.

**Elbow pain?**
- Assess: shoulder mobility (particularly IR).
- Assess: wrist and forearm tissue quality.
- Treat upstream (shoulder) and downstream (forearm) before isolated elbow work.

---

## "Mobility Must Precede Stability"

This phrase from Cook's *Movement* is foundational. The full principle: you cannot successfully install stability training on top of a mobility restriction. The body will use the compensation pattern it already has, not the new motor pattern you're trying to train.

Example: Teaching the squat pattern to someone with severe ankle DF restriction. No matter how much technique coaching you provide, they will heel-rise and knee-cave because the ankle isn't mobile enough to support the pattern. Teaching them to "keep knees out" is fighting the kinetic chain, not fixing it. **Restore the ankle mobility first. Then teach the squat pattern. Then load the squat.**

The sequence is always: **Mobility → Motor Control → Strength.**

Missing the mobility step produces athletes who are strong in poor positions. This is common in strength sport. Loading a flawed pattern doesn't reinforce good movement — it embeds the compensation and accelerates structural wear at the compensating joints.

---

## FMS: A Screening System Built on Joint-by-Joint Logic

The Functional Movement Screen (FMS) is Gray Cook's practical application of joint-by-joint theory. Seven tests:

1. Deep Squat
2. Hurdle Step
3. Inline Lunge
4. Shoulder Mobility (bilateral arm raise)
5. Active Straight Leg Raise
6. Trunk Stability Push-Up
7. Rotary Stability

Each test maps to a specific mobility/stability demand. The scoring system identifies:
- **Asymmetry:** The most significant injury predictor. An athlete scoring 3 on the right and 1 on the left is at more risk than an athlete scoring 2 on both sides.
- **Pain during a test:** Automatic score of 0 and immediate physiotherapy referral.

Cook's principle: **Screen before training. Train to screen (correct patterns). Don't train around a dysfunction.**

For coaches applying this to athletes: you don't need to administer the full FMS to use joint-by-joint logic. The three most predictive tests for strength athletes are:
1. Deep squat (ankle, hip, T-spine screen)
2. Shoulder mobility (shoulder, T-spine screen)
3. Active straight leg raise (hip flexion, hamstring, lumbar stability screen)

Run these three before programming. They tell you where the mobility work goes.

---

## What This Means for Your Training Programme

**Daily mobility work is injury prevention.** Not an optional extra. Not just for when something hurts. The joint-by-joint framework predicts injury based on mobility restriction — before the pain arrives.

Kelly Starrett's prescription in *Becoming a Supple Leopard* is consistent with Cook's principles: 10–15 minutes of daily maintenance mobility is non-negotiable for athletes under significant load. The target is the mobile joints in the hierarchy — ankles, hips, T-spine, shoulders.

**Priority order for most strength athletes and grapplers** (based on most common restrictions seen in this population):

1. **Thoracic spine extension + rotation** (foam roller, thread the needle)
2. **Hip flexors and capsule** (couch stretch, banded hip distraction)
3. **Ankle dorsiflexion** (banded talar mobilisation, soleus stretch)
4. **Posterior shoulder / GIRD** (sleeper stretch, banded shoulder distraction)
5. **Adductors** (for grapplers specifically)

This is the maintenance sequence. If you have a specific existing restriction or injury, weight toward that area.

---

## The One Rule

Pain is a signal, not a location.

When something hurts, ask: **what joint that's supposed to be mobile is forcing this joint to move instead?** Start there.

The body is a system. Train it as one.

---

## Citations

1. Gray Cook — *Movement: Functional Movement Systems* (2010) — primary source for joint-by-joint framework, FMS, and cascade principles
2. Kelly Starrett — *Becoming a Supple Leopard* (2nd ed., 2015) — daily mobility system, area map, position-first principles
3. Donald Neumann — *Kinesiology of the Musculoskeletal System* (3rd ed.) — structural kinesiology of each joint, muscle function, biomechanical cascade mechanism
4. Mike Boyle — joint-by-joint approach (co-developed with Gray Cook, disseminated in *Advances in Functional Training*, 2010)


---

# EMBEDDED EXERCISE LIBRARY & SELECTION DATABASE (v11.2) — the authoritative movement source

The two parts BELOW are the engine's authoritative exercise-selection database:
PART 1 is the taxonomy/data-model (how to read and query the library); PART 2 is the
full tagged exercise library (538 entries across 20 movement patterns, each with
movement pattern, category tags, muscles, equipment, skill cost, fatigue cost,
loading type, best-for, progression/regression routes, cues, common mistakes,
constraint flags, and substitution options).

HARD SELECTION RULE (this overrides any tendency to invent):
- EVERY exercise printed in a client's program MUST be selected from this library
  (or be a direct, named progression/regression listed inside an entry's
  progression_route / regression_route / substitution_options field).
- NEVER invent a movement and NEVER echo a loose intake goal phrase as an exercise
  name. If the athlete's goal phrasing is non-standard (e.g. "90-degree HSPU"), find
  the matching real entry (the library HAS a "90-Degree Handstand Push-Up" entry and
  a "Wall Handstand Push-up" ladder) and program THAT canonical name.
- SELECTION ALGORITHM: (1) classify the named goal by exposure type via the Generator
  routing rule; (2) query the required movement_pattern; (3) filter by category_tags,
  constraint_flags (drop anything flagged for a reported sensitivity), skill_cost and
  fatigue_cost budget for the day; (4) climb/descend progression_route /
  regression_route to match the athlete's level; (5) use substitution_options for
  equipment limits. Substitutions stay inside the SAME movement_pattern.

GOVERNANCE (same as every internal layer):
- INTERNAL ONLY. Never surface the field labels, tags, skill/fatigue cost values,
  constraint-flag names, movement_pattern codes, or this database's existence in
  client-facing output. The client sees only the canonical exercise NAME plus plain
  coaching prescription (sets, reps, RPE/RIR, rest, brief cue). All field data is a
  reasoning tool only.
- GOAL-AGNOSTIC. The library is a menu, not a plan. Only pull entries that serve a
  goal THIS athlete actually named; never import an exercise because it appears here.

---

## PART 1 — EXERCISE LIBRARY TAXONOMY & DATA MODEL (internal)

# Exercise Library Taxonomy & Recommendations

**Purpose.** This document defines the data model for Raz Pilosof's elite strength program generator. It explains the hybrid taxonomy chosen for the library, the rationale, and the structural conventions every exercise entry must follow so that the generator can route, substitute, scale, and stack exercises automatically.

---

## 1. Recommended Taxonomy: Hybrid (Pattern Backbone + Category Tags)

Each exercise is indexed on **two orthogonal axes**:

1. **Primary movement pattern** — one and only one. This is the *coaching backbone*: the biomechanical role the exercise plays in a session.
2. **Category tags** — many. These are *secondary routing tags* for sport context, equipment family, contractile mode, and program intent.

### 1.1 Movement patterns (the backbone)

A movement pattern answers: *"What is the body actually doing under load?"*

| Pattern | Definition |
|---|---|
| vertical_push | Pressing a load overhead or from chest toward the ceiling |
| horizontal_push | Pressing a load away from the chest in the transverse plane |
| vertical_pull | Pulling a load toward the chest from overhead |
| horizontal_pull | Pulling a load toward the torso from in front |
| squat_knee_dominant | Knee-flexion-led lower-body loaded patterns |
| hinge_hip_dominant | Hip-flexion-led lower-body loaded patterns |
| lunge_split_squat | Staggered or single-leg knee-dominant patterns |
| loaded_carry | Walking or holding under external load |
| core_anti_extension | Resisting lumbar extension |
| core_anti_rotation | Resisting transverse-plane rotation |
| core_anti_lateral_flexion | Resisting frontal-plane side-bend |
| trunk_flexion_extension_rotation | Active spinal movement (sit-ups; back ext; Russian twist; Jefferson curl) |
| locomotion_conditioning | Running; rowing; skipping; sled; airbike; jump rope |
| jumps_throws_power | Ballistic/concentric-dominant power expression |
| grip_neck | Dedicated grip and cervical work |
| calf_ankle_foot | Tibialis; gastroc; soleus; foot intrinsics |
| scapular_shoulder_prep | Cuff; scap; band/cable rehab-style shoulder drills |
| elbow_wrist_forearm | Tendon health; forearm hypertrophy; finger work |
| hip_knee_prehab | VMO; glute med; adductor; hamstring tendon health |
| mobility_restoration | Active-range mobility; positional work; soft-tissue |

This list is finite, mutually exclusive at point of programming, and covers everything from a planche push-up to a Jefferson curl to a Copenhagen adduction.

### 1.2 Category tags (the routing layer)

Category tags answer: *"For whom; with what; in what mode; for what training intent?"*

| Tag | Use |
|---|---|
| powerlifting | SBD-adjacent; comp-relevant |
| strongman | Odd objects; medley; event work |
| calisthenics_gymnastics | Bodyweight skill work; rings; bars; floor |
| bodybuilding_accessory | Hypertrophy-focused accessory |
| core | Anything routed for trunk |
| prehab_rehab_support | Joint health; tendon resilience; pain-tolerance work |
| conditioning | Energy-system work |
| GPP | General preparedness; work-capacity carryover |
| combat_sport_support | BJJ; grappling; striking carryover |
| olympic_lifting_power | Snatch/clean derivatives; expressive ballistic |
| machine_cable | Fixed-path; constant tension |
| unilateral | Single-limb |
| isometric | Hold-based |
| eccentric | Slow lowering or eccentric-only |
| plyometric | Stretch-shortening cycle |

An exercise can carry several tags. A Zercher squat is `squat_knee_dominant` + tags `[strongman, GPP, combat_sport_support]`. A planche push-up is `horizontal_push` + tags `[calisthenics_gymnastics, isometric]`.

---

## 2. Why Hybrid Beats Pattern-Only or Category-Only

### Pattern-only (e.g. only "squat / hinge / push / pull / carry")
- **Pro**: Clean biomechanics; easy substitution math.
- **Con**: Collapses very different exercises into one bucket. A back squat, a pistol squat, a Zercher squat, and a Hatfield squat are all "squat" — but the generator must know one is a powerlifting main lift, one is unilateral skill work, one is a front-loaded grappling-friendly variant, and one is an assisted strength variant. Pattern-only loses this.

### Category-only (e.g. only "powerlifting / strongman / calisthenics / bodybuilding")
- **Pro**: Maps to athlete identity and goals.
- **Con**: Breaks substitution logic. The generator cannot ask, *"give me another vertical push that an athlete with cranky wrists can tolerate"* if the index is by category. A handstand push-up and a log press end up in different buckets despite being the same pattern.

### Hybrid (the recommendation)
- **The generator queries on pattern first** ("the athlete needs a vertical push today"), then **filters/sorts on tags** ("athlete trains strongman + has a wrist constraint → log press, not 90° HSPU").
- **Substitutions stay biomechanically valid** because they live inside one pattern bucket.
- **Goal-routing stays clean** because category tags carry sport identity.
- **Constraint flags** (wrist; lumbar; shoulder; knee) can be attached at the exercise level independent of either axis.

This is the same logic used in evidence-informed S&C software design and matches how elite coaches actually program — they think in patterns and tag for context.

---

## 3. Required Fields Per Exercise

Every entry in `comprehensive_exercise_library_v1.csv` carries these fields:

| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| exercise_name | Canonical name |
| movement_pattern | Exactly one pattern from §1.1 |
| category_tags | Semicolon-separated tags from §1.2 |
| primary_muscles | Prime movers (semicolon-separated) |
| secondary_muscles | Stabilizers and synergists |
| equipment | Bar; DB; KB; rings; sled; sandbag; bodyweight; band; machine; etc |
| skill_cost | low / medium / high / very_high — neural and technical demand |
| fatigue_cost | low / medium / high / very_high — systemic recovery cost |
| loading_type | bilateral; unilateral; isometric; eccentric; ballistic; tempo; bodyweight; banded |
| best_for | One-line intent ("max strength"; "hypertrophy"; "tendon health"; "skill") |
| progression_route | The next harder variation |
| regression_route | The next easier variation |
| key_cues | 1–3 coaching cues |
| common_mistakes | 1–3 fault patterns |
| constraint_flags | wrist; shoulder; lumbar; knee; etc — when *not* to use |
| substitution_options | 2–3 same-pattern swaps |
| notes | Free text; specialty context |

---

## 4. Scaling & Progression Conventions

- **Calisthenics/gymnastics**: every advanced skill has a documented regression chain. Example for planche push-up: *floor planche lean → tuck planche push-up → advanced tuck PPU → straddle PPU → full PPU*.
- **Strongman**: every event lift has both a *gym proxy* (e.g. axle clean & press ↔ continental + push press) and a *load scale* (sandbag weight ladders; yoke walk distance ladders).
- **Powerlifting**: variants are tagged for their training role — speed; max; volume; weak-point. The generator can pull "deficit deadlift" when the weakness flag is "off-the-floor".
- **Prehab/rehab support**: language is conservative. We say *support*, *tolerance*, *pain-gated*, and *coach/clinician review*. We never claim to diagnose, treat, or "fix" injury. The library *augments* clinician care, it does not replace it.

---

## 5. Constraint-Flag Conventions

A constraint flag is *not* a diagnosis. It is a heuristic the generator uses to **deprioritize** an exercise when the athlete has reported a sensitivity.

Standard flags: `wrist`, `elbow`, `shoulder`, `cervical`, `thoracic`, `lumbar`, `hip`, `knee`, `ankle`, `tendon_high_load`, `disc_flexion_sensitive`, `overhead_sensitive`, `grip_sensitive`.

Example: Jefferson curl carries `disc_flexion_sensitive`. Behind-the-neck press carries `overhead_sensitive` + `shoulder`. 90° HSPU carries `wrist` + `cervical` + `overhead_sensitive`.

---

## 6. What the Generator Can Do With This Schema

1. **Pattern-balanced day building** — pull one exercise per required pattern per session.
2. **Sport-routed selection** — filter by category tag set (e.g. strongman day = pull from `strongman` + `loaded_carry` + `grip_neck`).
3. **Constraint-aware substitution** — when an athlete logs a constraint, regenerate with that flag excluded.
4. **Progression/regression laddering** — climb up or down a skill chain via `progression_route`/`regression_route` strings.
5. **Skill+fatigue budgeting** — cap session-level skill_cost and fatigue_cost sums for athletes in recovery, in-season, or peaking.
6. **Substitution on equipment limits** — pivot via `substitution_options` when the athlete is in a hotel gym or has no rings.

---

## 7. Versioning

This is **v1**. Gaps and v2 targets are tracked in `exercise_library_QA_report.md`.

---

## PART 2 — FULL TAGGED EXERCISE LIBRARY (internal, 538 entries)

> Final merged exercise library for Raz Pilosof's elite strength program generator. Vol 1 (218 entries; internal duplicates removed) combined with Vol 2 additions. Schema and conventions documented in `exercise_library_taxonomy_and_recommendations.md`. Prehab/rehab entries use conservative language throughout. The library *supports tolerance* and *pain-gated exposure* under coach/clinician review; it does not diagnose or treat injury.

# Comprehensive Exercise Library — Final (538 entries)

> Conservative coaching note: prehab/rehab entries in this library *support tolerance* and *pain-gated exposure*. They do not diagnose or treat injury. Use under coach/clinician review for symptomatic athletes.

## Generator routing rule

The library is not a random menu. Before selecting any exercise, classify the athlete's named goal by exposure type, then choose the most specific recoverable exercise from the relevant pattern and category tags.

| Goal type | Direct exposure route | Support exposure route | Selection warning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Max barbell strength | Exact lift, comp-adjacent variation, pin/tempo/paused/partial route, or calibrated close substitute | Trunk, upper back, unilateral lower body, posterior chain, weak-point support | Do not treat isolated accessories as sufficient exposure to an elite strength goal. |
| Weighted calisthenics strength | Weighted target pattern, assisted exact pattern, partial ROM, isometric, or eccentric route | Scapular control, elbow/forearm tolerance, trunk tension, horizontal/vertical support | Do not replace a named bodyweight-strength goal with only pulldowns, rows, curls, or generic presses. |
| Bodyweight skill strength | Positional isometric, technical hold, lean, partial ROM, assisted skill, controlled eccentric, or low-volume exact exposure | Wrist/shoulder prep, straight-arm strength, scapular control, trunk shape | Do not collapse planche, lever, HSPU, or ring goals into generic hypertrophy work. |
| High-rep endurance | Density block, EMOM, ladder, repeat submax sets, or volume accumulation | Tissue tolerance, technique consistency, lower-cost accessories | Do not count max-strength pulling or pressing as high-rep endurance exposure. |
| Hypertrophy or body composition | Recoverable target-pattern volume with RIR control | Stable accessory lifts, machine/cable work, weak-point volume | Do not overuse neural singles when the stated adaptation is tissue volume. |
| Combat-sport conditioning | Demand-specific alactic repeatability, tempo capacity, aerobic recovery, loaded GPP, or restoration route | Neck, grip, trunk, scapular, hip/knee capacity based on the sport schedule | Do not add random hard conditioning that fights the main sport or strength stress. |
| Running/endurance | Aerobic base, tempo, speed-endurance, hill/flat exposure, or low-impact cyclic substitute | Calf/ankle/foot, hip capacity, trunk stiffness | Do not place high-impact fatigue where it degrades hard grappling or heavy lower-body work. |
| Pain-gated rebuilding | Pain-free isometric, reduced-ROM, slow tempo, HSR-style, supported variation, or adjacent pattern | Re-entry criteria and clinician-review triggers | Do not diagnose, test maxes, or delete the whole goal unless red flags justify it. |

Every exercise row should be explainable by goal transfer, pattern role, skill cost, fatigue cost, constraint flags, progression route, and regression route.

## vertical_push

### 90-Degree Handstand Push-Up
- **Movement pattern:** vertical_push
- **Category tags:** calisthenics_gymnastics
- **Primary muscles:** deltoid anterior; triceps; serratus
- **Secondary muscles:** upper chest; core; wrist flexors
- **Equipment:** parallettes
- **Skill cost / Fatigue cost:** very_high / high
- **Loading type:** bodyweight
- **Best for:** elite straight-arm-to-bent-arm transition strength
- **Progression route:** hollow-back press to handstand
- **Regression route:** freestanding HSPU; pseudo planche push-up
- **Key cues:** press up through scaps; legs stay vertical; hollow
- **Common mistakes:** hip pike for leverage; loss of vertical hips
- **Constraint flags:** wrist; cervical; shoulder
- **Substitution options:** freestanding HSPU; ring HSPU
- **Notes:** One of the hardest pressing skills in calisthenics; treat as skill; not volume.

### Axle Clean and Press
- **Movement pattern:** vertical_push
- **Category tags:** strongman; GPP
- **Primary muscles:** deltoid anterior; triceps
- **Secondary muscles:** upper back; grip; hamstrings; glutes
- **Equipment:** axle bar
- **Skill cost / Fatigue cost:** high / very_high
- **Loading type:** bilateral
- **Best for:** strongman medley press
- **Progression route:** continental + jerk
- **Regression route:** barbell clean and press
- **Key cues:** continental from belly; rebrace before press
- **Common mistakes:** trying to squat-clean a thick bar; soft brace
- **Constraint flags:** lumbar; grip_sensitive
- **Substitution options:** log press; barbell clean and press
- **Notes:** Thick axle grip is itself a training stimulus; expect grip to be the limiter.

### Banded Triceps Pushdown
- **Movement pattern:** vertical_push
- **Category tags:** bodybuilding_accessory
- **Primary muscles:** triceps
- **Secondary muscles:** rear delt
- **Equipment:** band
- **Skill cost / Fatigue cost:** low / low
- **Loading type:** bilateral
- **Best for:** triceps lateral head emphasis
- **Progression route:** cable pushdown
- **Regression route:** bodyweight dip
- **Key cues:** elbows pinned; full lockout; squeeze
- **Common mistakes:** flaring elbows; partial ROM
- **Constraint flags:** elbow
- **Substitution options:** cable pushdown; close-grip push-up
- **Notes:** Travel-friendly triceps.

### Box Pike Push-Up

- **Common mistakes:** hips drifting back; partial reps
- **Constraint flags:** wrist
- **Substitution options:** pike push-up; DB OHP
- **Notes:** Choose box height that puts hips directly above shoulders.

### Cable Lateral Raise

- **Common mistakes:** using upper trap; momentum
- **Constraint flags:** shoulder
- **Substitution options:** DB lateral raise; machine lateral raise
- **Notes:** Cable keeps tension at lengthened position better than DB.

### Scapular Plane Handstand Hold
- **Movement pattern:** vertical_push
- **Category tags:** calisthenics_gymnastics; isometric
- **Primary muscles:** deltoid; serratus; core
- **Secondary muscles:** wrist; forearms
- **Equipment:** floor; wall
- **Skill cost / Fatigue cost:** high / medium
- **Loading type:** isometric
- **Best for:** balance and shoulder positioning
- **Progression route:** freestanding handstand
- **Regression route:** wall handstand
- **Key cues:** stack ribs over hips; press the floor away; long line
- **Common mistakes:** overarching lumbar; banana shape; shrugging
- **Constraint flags:** wrist; shoulder; overhead_sensitive
- **Substitution options:** wall handstand; freestanding handstand
- **Notes:** Hand balancing in the kinder shoulder plane.

### Seated DB Shoulder Press
- **Movement pattern:** vertical_push
- **Category tags:** bodybuilding_accessory
- **Primary muscles:** deltoid anterior; deltoid lateral; triceps
- **Secondary muscles:** upper trap
- **Equipment:** dumbbells; bench
- **Skill cost / Fatigue cost:** low / medium
- **Loading type:** bilateral
- **Best for:** shoulder hypertrophy
- **Progression route:** standing DB OHP
- **Regression route:** machine shoulder press
- **Key cues:** neutral wrist; slight arc inward; full ROM
- **Common mistakes:** bouncing out of bottom; flared elbows when not desired
- **Constraint flags:** shoulder
- **Substitution options:** machine press; landmine press
- **Notes:** Neutral grip variant is the most shoulder-friendly default.

### Single-Arm Cable Triceps Pushdown
- **Movement pattern:** vertical_push
- **Category tags:** bodybuilding_accessory; machine_cable; unilateral
- **Primary muscles:** triceps
- **Secondary muscles:** rear delt
- **Equipment:** cable
- **Skill cost / Fatigue cost:** low / low
- **Loading type:** unilateral
- **Best for:** unilateral triceps overload
- **Progression route:** heavier single-arm pushdown
- **Regression route:** bilateral pushdown
- **Key cues:** elbow pinned; full lockout
- **Common mistakes:** shrugging; using shoulder
- **Constraint flags:** elbow
- **Substitution options:** rope pushdown; banded pushdown
- **Notes:** Asymmetry-aware triceps work.

### Single-Arm DB Press (Standing)
- **Movement pattern:** vertical_push
- **Category tags:** bodybuilding_accessory; unilateral
- **Primary muscles:** deltoid anterior; deltoid lateral; triceps
- **Secondary muscles:** core
- **Equipment:** dumbbell
- **Skill cost / Fatigue cost:** medium / medium
- **Loading type:** unilateral
- **Best for:** asymmetric overhead
- **Progression route:** heavier 1-arm DB press
- **Regression route:** half-kneeling 1-arm press
- **Key cues:** stand tall; ribs down; press up
- **Common mistakes:** leaning; arching lumbar
- **Constraint flags:** overhead_sensitive; shoulder
- **Substitution options:** half-kneeling DB press; landmine press
- **Notes:** Single-arm overhead loading.

### Split Jerk
- **Movement pattern:** vertical_push
- **Category tags:** olympic_lifting_power
- **Primary muscles:** deltoid anterior; triceps
- **Secondary muscles:** quads; glutes; adductors; core
- **Equipment:** barbell
- **Skill cost / Fatigue cost:** very_high / high
- **Loading type:** bilateral
- **Best for:** maximal overhead expression
- **Progression route:** competition jerk
- **Regression route:** push jerk
- **Key cues:** strong split; front shin vertical; bar over base of neck
- **Common mistakes:** short split; press-out; bar in front
- **Constraint flags:** overhead_sensitive; ankle; knee
- **Substitution options:** push jerk; push press
- **Notes:** Highest skill-cost overhead expression in the catalog.

### Stalder Press to Handstand
- **Movement pattern:** vertical_push
- **Category tags:** calisthenics_gymnastics
- **Primary muscles:** deltoid anterior; pec; triceps
- **Secondary muscles:** hip flexors; core; adductors
- **Equipment:** floor; parallettes
- **Skill cost / Fatigue cost:** very_high / high
- **Loading type:** bodyweight
- **Best for:** straight-leg pike press to handstand
- **Progression route:** hollowback press; one-arm stalder
- **Regression route:** straddle press; pike press from box
- **Key cues:** straddle wide; press hips up first; finish stacked
- **Common mistakes:** bending arms; using momentum; losing compression
- **Constraint flags:** wrist; shoulder
- **Substitution options:** straddle press to handstand; hollowback press
- **Notes:** Compression and shoulder flexion are the gates.

### Standing Barbell Overhead Press
- **Movement pattern:** vertical_push
- **Category tags:** powerlifting; strongman; bodybuilding_accessory; GPP
- **Primary muscles:** deltoid anterior; deltoid lateral; triceps
- **Secondary muscles:** upper trap; serratus; core; glutes
- **Equipment:** barbell; rack
- **Skill cost / Fatigue cost:** medium / high
- **Loading type:** bilateral
- **Best for:** overhead strength baseline
- **Progression route:** push press; log press; axle press
- **Regression route:** seated DB press; landmine press
- **Key cues:** ribs down; squeeze glutes; bar path through ears
- **Common mistakes:** excessive lumbar extension; pressing in front of face; soft midline
- **Constraint flags:** overhead_sensitive; lumbar; shoulder
- **Substitution options:** seated DB OHP; landmine press; log press
- **Notes:** The standing strict press is the strength reference for the vertical push pattern.

### Straddle Press to Handstand
- **Movement pattern:** vertical_push
- **Category tags:** calisthenics_gymnastics
- **Primary muscles:** deltoid anterior; pec; triceps
- **Secondary muscles:** hip flexors; adductors; core
- **Equipment:** floor
- **Skill cost / Fatigue cost:** high / high
- **Loading type:** bodyweight
- **Best for:** classic press to handstand
- **Progression route:** stalder press; hollowback press
- **Regression route:** pike press to handstand; box pike press
- **Key cues:** straddle wide; hinge over hands; press hips up
- **Common mistakes:** bending arms; jumping into handstand; losing compression
- **Constraint flags:** wrist; shoulder
- **Substitution options:** stalder press; pike press to handstand
- **Notes:** Foundational press to handstand.

### Tuck Planche Push-Up to Handstand
- **Movement pattern:** vertical_push
- **Category tags:** calisthenics_gymnastics
- **Primary muscles:** deltoid anterior; pec; triceps
- **Secondary muscles:** serratus; core
- **Equipment:** parallettes

- **Regression route:** tuck planche push-up; pseudo planche push-up
- **Key cues:** stay tucked tight; press through fingertips; protract at top
- **Common mistakes:** losing tuck; flaring elbows; piking under load
- **Constraint flags:** wrist; shoulder; elbow
- **Substitution options:** straddle PPU to handstand; pseudo planche PPU
- **Notes:** Combines planche hold with press. Skill+strength capstone.

### Wall Handstand Shoulder Tap
- **Movement pattern:** vertical_push
- **Category tags:** calisthenics_gymnastics; isometric
- **Primary muscles:** deltoid; serratus; core
- **Secondary muscles:** wrist; forearms

- **Regression route:** wall handstand hold
- **Key cues:** stack tall; shift fully over one hand; tap controlled
- **Common mistakes:** losing line; rushing taps
- **Constraint flags:** wrist; shoulder; cervical
- **Substitution options:** wall HS hold; chest-to-wall taps
- **Notes:** One-arm handstand build.

### Wall-Supported Handstand Push-Up (Deficit)
- **Movement pattern:** vertical_push
- **Category tags:** calisthenics_gymnastics
- **Primary muscles:** deltoid; triceps; pec

- **Regression route:** wall HSPU; pike push-up
- **Key cues:** head past hands; press straight; tight midline
- **Common mistakes:** flaring elbows; banana shape; partial ROM
- **Constraint flags:** wrist; shoulder; cervical; overhead_sensitive
- **Substitution options:** freestanding HSPU; pike PPU
- **Notes:** Adds eccentric ROM to wall HSPU.

### Z Press
- **Movement pattern:** vertical_push
- **Category tags:** strongman; bodybuilding_accessory; core
- **Primary muscles:** deltoid anterior; triceps
- **Secondary muscles:** core; upper back
- **Equipment:** barbell
- **Skill cost / Fatigue cost:** medium / medium
- **Loading type:** bilateral
- **Best for:** strict overhead without leg drive
- **Progression route:** standing OHP
- **Regression route:** seated DB OHP
- **Key cues:** long spine; toes up; brace before press
- **Common mistakes:** rounding to find leverage; legs lifting
- **Constraint flags:** overhead_sensitive; hamstring_tight
- **Substitution options:** seated OHP; tall-kneeling press
- **Notes:** Exposes any layback cheat present in standing press.


## horizontal_push

### Archer Push-Up
- **Movement pattern:** horizontal_push
- **Category tags:** calisthenics_gymnastics; unilateral
- **Primary muscles:** pec major; triceps
- **Secondary muscles:** serratus; core
- **Equipment:** bodyweight
- **Skill cost / Fatigue cost:** high / medium
- **Loading type:** unilateral
- **Best for:** one-arm push-up progression
- **Progression route:** one-arm push-up
- **Regression route:** push-up
- **Key cues:** straight arm stays straight; working arm presses at 45 degrees
- **Common mistakes:** bending the assist arm
- **Constraint flags:** wrist; shoulder
- **Substitution options:** ring archer push-up; assisted OAPU
- **Notes:** Top-tier unilateral horizontal push without equipment.

### Barbell Bench Press
- **Movement pattern:** horizontal_push
- **Category tags:** powerlifting; bodybuilding_accessory
- **Primary muscles:** pec major; triceps; deltoid anterior
- **Secondary muscles:** lat; serratus; core
- **Equipment:** barbell; bench; rack
- **Skill cost / Fatigue cost:** medium / high
- **Loading type:** bilateral
- **Best for:** max horizontal press strength
- **Progression route:** competition bench; pause bench
- **Regression route:** DB bench; floor press
- **Key cues:** upper back tight; feet driven; bar to lower sternum
- **Common mistakes:** elbows flared 90 degrees; bouncing off chest; loose upper back
- **Constraint flags:** shoulder; wrist
- **Substitution options:** DB bench; floor press; machine chest press
- **Notes:** The PL gold standard. Always coach the arch and the lat tightness.

### Board Press (2-3 Board)
- **Movement pattern:** horizontal_push
- **Category tags:** powerlifting
- **Primary muscles:** pec; triceps; deltoid anterior
- **Secondary muscles:** core
- **Equipment:** barbell; boards
- **Skill cost / Fatigue cost:** medium / high
- **Loading type:** bilateral
- **Best for:** lockout overload
- **Progression route:** heavier board press
- **Regression route:** floor press
- **Key cues:** pause on boards; tight back; press strong
- **Common mistakes:** bouncing; losing tension
- **Constraint flags:** shoulder; elbow
- **Substitution options:** floor press; pin press
- **Notes:** Lockout-specific overload.

### Bulgarian Dip (Rings)
- **Movement pattern:** horizontal_push
- **Category tags:** calisthenics_gymnastics
- **Primary muscles:** pec major; triceps; deltoid anterior
- **Secondary muscles:** cuff; core
- **Equipment:** rings
- **Skill cost / Fatigue cost:** high / medium
- **Loading type:** bodyweight
- **Best for:** bridge to iron cross/impossible dip prep
- **Progression route:** impossible dip; iron cross progressions
- **Regression route:** ring dip with deep turnout
- **Key cues:** rings turn wide and out at top of every rep
- **Common mistakes:** incomplete turnout
- **Constraint flags:** shoulder; elbow
- **Substitution options:** ring dip; ring support hold
- **Notes:** A turnout-aggressive ring dip; do not rush load.

### Cable Chest Fly
- **Movement pattern:** horizontal_push
- **Category tags:** bodybuilding_accessory; machine_cable
- **Primary muscles:** pec major
- **Secondary muscles:** deltoid anterior
- **Equipment:** cable
- **Skill cost / Fatigue cost:** low / low
- **Loading type:** bilateral
- **Best for:** pec hypertrophy in lengthened position
- **Progression route:** high-to-low or low-to-high variations
- **Regression route:** DB fly
- **Key cues:** soft elbow; squeeze at midline; long stretch
- **Common mistakes:** bending elbows excessively; short ROM
- **Constraint flags:** shoulder
- **Substitution options:** DB fly; pec deck
- **Notes:** Bias the lengthened ROM for hypertrophy.

- **Constraint flags:** shoulder
- **Substitution options:** incline DB fly; pec deck

### Impossible Dip (Russian Dip)
- **Movement pattern:** horizontal_push
